# E-Learning Localization Best Practices

[![CommunityTeam's avatar](https://api.articulate.com/id/v1/avatars/auth0%7C0d3742d0-b37d-0131-2ee8-22000b2f96a1)](https://community.articulate.com/users/communityteam/580435)

[CommunityTeam](https://community.articulate.com/users/communityteam/580435)

Staff

11 months ago

![](https://community.articulate.com/t5/s/rwgqn69235/images/bS0xMjIwODIyLVR4T1JvVw?image-dimensions=999x624&revision=9)

You’ve just created an amazing course with beautiful graphics, engaging content, and relevant knowledge checks—and you’re ready to share your course. Then, you get the word: This course will be translated into 10 different languages and shared with learners all over the world.

Immediately, you start asking yourself how translation will impact your content. Will the key messages still come across as intended? Will the graphics make sense? And will the training have the same impact you want it to have on your workforce, no matter what language they speak?

Good news! It’s not too late to adapt your course for your global audience. Use this list of best practices to review and update your course before you translate to make the localization process fast and easy.

And next time you build a course, make sure to [identify your audience](https://community.articulate.com/blog/articles/how-to-do-an-e-learning-audience-analysis/1151682) before you get started. That way you know if you need to keep these tips in mind as you’re designing.

We’ll start out with some content tips, and then go over some guidelines for graphics. Here we go!

## Course Content Tips

### 1\. Use active voice.

Say what you mean and eliminate any unnecessary words. Write in the active voice and use adverbs and adjectives sparingly.

❌ Your bicycle has been damaged.

✅ A car crushed your bicycle.

### 2\. Keep it short and sweet.

Your objective is to get your point across to learners. Short, simple sentences are easier to understand and translate. Remember: If the translator misunderstands what you’ve written, your message will be lost in translation.

❌ Michelle is going to visit her grandparents who live in Boston after her work meeting.

✅ Michelle is going to Boston for work. Afterward, she’ll visit her grandparents

who live nearby.

### 3\. Use basic vocabulary.

Don’t use “utilize” where a simple “use” will do. Jargon, slang, idioms, and colloquial expressions are often difficult or impossible to translate. Plus, they make the content harder to understand, even for native speakers!

❌ Let me know when you get your ducks in a row.

✅ Let me know when your bags are packed.

If you have to use industry jargon, brand terms, or other complicated words, make sure to choose a translation tool like [Articulate Localization](https://www.articulate.com/360/localization) that allows you to [create a glossary](https://access.articulate.com/support/article/How-to-Use-the-AI-Translation-Glossary?_ga=2.96632799.1173809142.1721720459-625985929.1679688420). That way, these terms are translated correctly everywhere they appear.

### 4\. Use terms consistently.

It can be tempting to use synonyms to avoid repetition. However, when you’re writing for localization, it’s best to use terms consistently to avoid confusion and mistranslation.

❌An authoring tool is a program that allows you to create multimedia

projects, like e-learning courses. There are many different authoring apps

on the market.

✅ An authoring tool is a program that allows you to create multimedia

projects, like e-learning courses. There are many different authoring tools

on the market.

### 5\. Avoid humor, sarcasm, pop culture references, and sports analogies.

It can be tempting to use humor, cultural references, and emojis to catch your learners' attention. But humor varies greatly from one culture to the next, making it one of the most difficult things to translate. And when you include cultural references, you run the risk that translators will translate the words literally instead of figuratively. Even emojis—which might feel universal—are often used to mean different things by different groups of people. When you’re creating content for people worldwide, it's best to aim to be culture-neutral, so nothing gets lost in translation.

❌ The ball is in your court. (Sports reference.)

✅ It’s your responsibility to do something now.

### 6\. Avoid phrasal verbs.

A phrasal verb is an expression that includes a verb and an adverb or a preposition. For example: to look down on, to bring up, to fill out, etc.

What makes these expressions particularly difficult for non-native English speakers is that the meaning often changes dramatically depending on the preposition or adverb that follows the verb. For example, “to get back at” means “to retaliate” or “to get revenge,” but “to get back into” means “to become interested again.” Whenever possible, replace phrasal verbs with an equivalent one-word verb to ensure correct translation.

❌ Did you hand in your assignment?

✅ Did you submit your assignment?

### 7\. Use relative pronouns.

English speakers often omit relative pronouns like “that” or “which” to shorten sentences without changing the meaning. For example, “The clothes he wore were new” means the same as “The clothes that he wore were new.” It’s better to include relative pronouns for translation purposes, as they improve readability and comprehension.

❌ The clothes I bought are hanging in the closet.

✅ The clothes that I bought are hanging in the closet.

### 8\. Avoid ambiguity.

When you’re writing for localization, you want to make absolutely certain that there’s only one way to interpret your text. That’s because ambiguity in your content can lead to mistranslation.

For example, “I saw a woman on a mountain with binoculars” could mean either you saw a woman standing on a mountain who had binoculars or you were looking at a mountain through your binoculars and saw a woman.

One way to improve clarity is to replace pronouns (like “he” or “she”) with proper nouns.

❌ Jane isn’t coming over tonight. Tell Sarah we’ll see her next week. (Who does the “her” in the second sentence refer to: Jane or Sarah? It’s unclear.)

✅ Jane isn’t coming over tonight. Tell Sarah that we’ll see Jane next week.

### 9\. Avoid phrases with too many consecutive nouns.

Technical or compliance documentation often uses noun strings—a series of nouns used one after the other without any prepositions to describe complex concepts. For example, “employee performance evaluation procedure.” Together, these four nouns make up the name of a procedure used to evaluate employee performance. They’re intended to make things easier, but they usually end up doing just the opposite. When there are no prepositions, readers are forced to infer the relationship between the words. Whenever possible, break noun strings into smaller units, adding in prepositions and verbs as necessary.

❌Our employee relations improvement program is one of this year’s top priorities.

✅One of this year’s top priorities is to improve employee relations.

### 10\. Make a note of anything that needs to be adapted.

It’s not always possible to create content that’s 100% optimized for localization. Sometimes, you have to include things like currency, measurements, dates, times, temperatures, and phone numbers that have to be adapted for each language and country.

Depending on the course subject, you may even run into some larger differences related to local customs and etiquette (for example, how sales calls are done

Key takeaways

- Customer education reduces reliance on 1:1 CSM support, enabling teams to scale without adding headcount
- A structured education strategy streamlines onboarding and adoption, cutting time-to-value across your customer base

Table of Contents

As products and services become more complex, customers need support and guidance to explore their full capabilities and benefits. And as a company grows, providing this support on a one-to-one basis becomes harder.

The result? More pressure and workload on your Customer Success Managers (CSMs) and customer support teams.

For SaaS Customer Success Managers, customer education isn’t just about training customers. It’s the key to promoting product adoption, preventing churn, and deflecting support.

This blog post explores:

- benefits of customer education
- strategies for rolling out successful customer education initiatives
- best tools for the job

## Benefits of customer education

A customer education program bridges the gap between a product’s potential and its actual use. Through on-demand resources and real-time sessions, customers understand and use products effectively. The impact on your CSM team as well as your business is significant.

### Shorter time to value

[Onboarding](https://www.learnworlds.com/blog/customer-education/user-adoption-strategy-guide/) is the first stage of a customer education program, helping customers set up the product and get familiar with key functions. By reinforcing one-to-one onboarding with on-demand educational resources, like how-to guides and video tutorials, customers can start using their new asset faster without having to wait for a reply from your support agents or to book time with CSM.

### Enhanced customer retention

Ensuring satisfaction and customer retention are top priorities on a CSM’s job description. An effective customer education program makes this job a lot easier, especially in SaaS environments where customers expect self-service on top of personalized support.

When you educate customers, you help them enjoy the product without hiccups and experience more value. Beyond that, they are also more confident in using the product without feeling stuck or lost among features and buttons. Higher satisfaction directly impacts retention.

### Fewer support tickets

Providing self-service resources reduces the number of incoming support tickets. Customers can solve common issues independently, without relying on your team for basic guidance.

As a result, your support and customer success teams can **focus on more complex, high-impact issues** instead of repetitive questions. This not only improves response times but also helps you scale support without expanding headcount, reducing overall customer support costs while maintaining a high-quality customer experience.

Here’s what Katie Breaker, Director of Sales & Marketing at Birdieball said about her own experience:

“We discovered that our support team spent 60% of their time answering basic questions. Instead of hiring two more CSMs, we invested in creating comprehensive training modules to reduce repetitive inquiries.

Our CSMs went from answering the same questions day after day to identifying expansion opportunities. We closed three major partnerships because our team had more time for strategic relationship building instead of basic troubleshooting.”

Katie Breaker, Director of Sales & Marketing, [Birdieball](https://www.birdieball.com/)

### Improved customer engagement

A [well-planned customer education](https://www.learnworlds.com/blog/customer-education/) strategy includes opportunities for connection and interaction with other customers and your brand. This takes some of the burden off your CSMs who are traditionally in charge of customer relationship management.

Live webinars and community forums build a sense of community and increase brand loyalty, complementing and strengthening the relationship your CSMs have already built.

### Higher Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)

Offering a customer education program helps expand the customer lifecycle and therefore secure repeat sales and renewals. It also makes it easier to drive upsells and cross sells without manual push from your customer success and sales teams, increasing the CLV.

### Stronger brand reputation

Companies known for their customer education efforts gain a reputation for caring about their customers’ success, which enhances overall brand perception outside the limits of your exciting customer base.

Besides, customer education often goes beyond the typical [product training](https://www.learnworlds.com/blog/customer-education/product-knowledge-best-practices/). It’s often about sharing insights and thought leadership content about your industry, which helps you establish your brand as an authority in your field.

### Shorter sales cycle

Positive word-of-mouth, reviews and testimonials, and the mere existence of a customer training program are great benefits to showcase potential customers. This makes it easier for your salespeople to close sales deals and CS teams more efficient in presales support.

## Your 9-step guide to building effective customer education

Careful planning increases your chances of success. These nine steps will help you build a successful customer education strategy, from start to finish:

### Step 1: Identify customer needs

Start by understanding your customers’ needs and pain points. Your sources of truth:

- Surveys and feedback forms
- Interviews
- Product analytics tools
- Support tickets

All these can help pinpoint common issues and knowledge gaps among customers so you can prioritize content creation accordingly.

### Step 2: Segment your customers

Segment your customers to personalize customer education and offer them relevant content they will truly benefit from.

Common segmentation criteria to start with:

- **Job role:** A power user in BI will need different training than an exec who just wants high-level dashboards.
- **Stage in the customer journey:** New customers are onboarding while seasoned ones are expanding to new features.
- **Use case:** Are they using your product for sales enablement? Onboarding? Compliance training? That shapes what they care about.
- **Company size or tier:** Enterprise customers might get white-glove onboarding; smaller accounts might go through a self-serve path.
- **Location/time zone:** Useful for scheduling live sessions or localizing content to make it more relatable.

![](https://www.learnworlds.com/app/themes/learnworlds/assets/images/get-scale/light-bulb.svg)

### Pro tip

Use whatever data you already have in your CRM to build simple segments. Even basic tags like “Admin” vs. “End User” or “Onboarding” vs. “Expansion” go a long way.

### Step 3: Define clear objectives

The objectives of your education program should be tied to your business goals. Knowing what you want to achieve will help you shape your customer education strategy.

Use the SMART methodology to set goals:

- **Specific:** Clearly define what you want to achieve, without vague or broad terms
- **Measurable:** Make sure you can track progress and success with quantifiable metrics
- **Attainable:** Set goals that are realistic given your resources and constraints
- **Relevant:** Align your goals with broader business or learning objectives
- **Time-bound:** Assign a clear deadline to stay on track

![](https://www.learnworlds.com/app/themes/learnworlds/assets/images/get-scale/light-bulb.svg)

### SMART goal examples:

- Reduce intro-level support tickets by 20% within six months by launching an onboarding course that covers top support topics
- Increase the percentage of users who complete onboarding within 7 days from 40% to 70% in the next quarter by introducing a guided onboarding course

### Step 4: Choose your tools

Yet, the challenge is that video is expensive to produce and difficult to maintain—especially across multiple languages. Documentation and help center content can be updated in hours. Video updates often take days or weeks. As a result, multilingual videos are delayed, become outdated, or are skipped altogether.

To scale globally, you need a workflow where video evolves as quickly as your localized documentation.

## The “Video Production Tax”

Most video workflows are built around one-off production.

While text is flexible and easy to update across different languages, video has historically been treated as a static asset. When the product changes, the script and video drift away from the documentation, and updating them requires starting over. This creates a “video production tax”, a compounding cost in time and effort that appears every time you add a language or update your software.

At scale, this leads to:

- each language becomes a separate production effort
- small product changes trigger disproportionate rework
- terminology diverges between documentation and video
- videos fall behind documentation and UI updates

## The Foundation: Transifex as the Localization Engine

Product documentation needs to be localized in a way that supports constant change.

With Transifex, you connect the content you publish—documentation, help center articles, knowledge base entries, onboarding content—to a localization workflow designed for iteration.

When you update an article, you don’t restart localization. Only the new or modified text moves through translation and review. Existing translations are reused automatically, keeping turnaround fast and preventing unnecessary rework.

The outcome is multilingual documentation that always stays current as the product evolves.

## Turning Localized Text into Video Output

Once documentation is localized and kept up to date, [Video Creation Cloud (VCC)](https://xtm.ai/video-creation-cloud) is used to turn that content into video.

VCC is a video production engine built for repeatability. Instead of relying on one-off recordings and manual editing, it works with structured scripts derived from existing content.

You use original and localized documentation as the input for video scripts. Each language version is driven by approved localized text, not by separately written or translated scripts.

From there, VCC supports repeatable production steps for voice generation, timing, rendering, and versioning. Multilingual versions don’t require rebuilding the project per language, and updates don’t require starting from scratch.

When documentation changes, you update the source content, localize the change in Transifex, and regenerate the video output. The same knowledge is updated in two formats, without creating two separate sources of truth.

# How to Repurpose Video Content With AI (Without Drowning in Tools)

_Last updated: 2026-01-12_

If you already record or stream in StreamYard, start with our built‑in AI Clips to turn those recordings into vertical, captioned highlights without exporting files or adding more subscriptions. If you regularly repurpose videos from many different apps, you can pair StreamYard with a dedicated AI clipping tool for those edge cases.

### Summary

- Use StreamYard’s AI Clips to automatically generate vertical (9:16) captioned shorts from your live streams and recordings in a few clicks. ( [StreamYard Help Center](https://support.streamyard.com/hc/en-us/articles/44168907570964-AI-clips))
- Say “Clip that” during a show to mark moments as highlights, so AI can quickly turn them into suggested segments later. ( [StreamYard Help Center](https://support.streamyard.com/hc/en-us/articles/44168907570964-AI-clips))
- For most US creators, this integrated workflow is cheaper per processed minute than using separate tools that bill by credits or processing time.
- Consider Opus Clip or VEED only when you need multi‑platform imports, extra B‑roll, or team features that sit downstream of your main recording and streaming hub. ( [OpusClip](https://help.opus.pro/docs/article/plans-and-credits), [VEED](https://www.veed.io/tools/repurpose-video))

## What does “repurpose video content with AI” actually mean?

When people search for “repurpose video content AI,” they’re usually trying to do one thing: turn long videos (podcasts, webinars, live shows) into lots of short, social‑ready clips—without manual scrubbing through timelines.

In practice, AI repurposing usually covers:

- **Finding highlights automatically** – AI looks for engaging moments and suggests segments.
- **Reformatting for vertical** – cropping and reframing into 9:16 for Shorts, Reels, and TikTok.
- **Auto‑captioning** – adding subtitles so clips are watchable on mute.
- **Light cleanup** – trimming dead air or filler, fixing layout, maybe adding simple titles.

StreamYard’s AI Clips focuses exactly on this “highlight, reframe, caption, and go” workflow, optimized for creators who already host and record their shows with us. ( [StreamYard Help Center](https://support.streamyard.com/hc/en-us/articles/44168907570964-AI-clips))

## How does StreamYard’s AI Clips repurpose your videos?

Here’s the basic flow once your StreamYard stream or recording finishes processing:

1. **Open your recording** in your StreamYard video library.
2. Click **Generate clips**.
3. Our AI **analyzes your video and automatically produces vertical (9:16) captioned clips with a title** based on the most engaging moments. ( [StreamYard Help Center](https://support.streamyard.com/hc/en-us/articles/44168907570964-AI-clips))
4. You **review, trim, and tweak** the suggestions.
5. Download or publish those clips to your social channels.

A few important details for power users:

- You can generate AI clips from recordings **up to 6 hours long**, which is plenty for most live shows, summits, or webinars. ( [StreamYard Help Center](https://support.streamyard.com/hc/en-us/articles/44168907570964-AI-clips))
- Recordings **shorter than 30 seconds** aren’t supported, which keeps the system focused on material where highlights actually exist. ( [StreamYard Help Center](https://support.streamyard.com/hc/en-us/articles/44168907570964-AI-clips))
- AI Clips supports multiple languages including English, Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, French, German, Polish, Ukrainian, Japanese, Tagalog, Turkish, Hindi, Punjabi, Russian, and Thai. ( [StreamYard Help Center](https://support.streamyard.com/hc/en-us/articles/44168907570964-AI-clips))

Most importantly for US creators: you’re not shuttling giant video files between apps. Your recording lives where it was created, and AI repurposing happens in that same place.

## How do you mark the best moments while you’re live?

One of the biggest time‑sinks in repurposing is **remembering where the good stuff happened**.

With StreamYard, you can simply **say “Clip that” out loud during your live stream or recording**, and our system will mark that as a highlight and automatically take the previous 30 seconds as one of the suggested segments for clipping. ( [StreamYard Help Center](https://support.streamyard.com/hc/en-us/articles/44168907570964-AI-clips))

Imagine you’re interviewing a guest and they drop a perfect, quotable answer. Instead of jotting timestamps or scrubbing later, you just say, “Clip that,” keep the conversation flowing, and let AI do the heavy lifting after the show.

This is a big reason many creators treat StreamYard as their **repurposing capture point** even if they later send a few recordings into other tools for more complex edits.

## How much does AI repurposing really cost you per minute?

Cost per processed minute is where different tools behave very differently.

**Opus Clip** and similar platforms typically use **credit or processing‑minute models**. For example, Opus documents free access with **60 processing minutes per month** on the entry plan and hundreds of minutes on paid plans. ( [OpusClip](https://help.opus.pro/docs/article/plans-and-credits))

At StreamYard, we tie AI Clips to your plan using **batches, not minutes**:

- Each AI Clips generation is a **batch** that can cover a recording **up to 6 hours long**.
- On the Free plan, you get **2 clip batches per month**, so you can process up to **12 hours** of content if each batch uses a 6‑hour recording.
- On higher plans, the number of monthly generations increases; for example, 25 generations per month can cover up to **150 hours** of long‑form content in total if you maxed out every batch.

If you map that to tools that bill by processing minutes or credits, those same hours can be equivalent to **hundreds or even more than a thousand credits** in alternative platforms, which quickly becomes more expensive than StreamYard’s subscription for many creators.

Because you’re paying once for streaming, recording, and AI repurposing in the same place, you also avoid the “stack tax” of juggling multiple subscriptions just to get your content out the door.

**Opus Clip (multi‑source repurposing)**

- A separate web app built to **ingest videos from multiple platforms**, including YouTube, Google Drive, Zoom, Loom, Riverside, and StreamYard itself. ( [OpusClip](https://help.opus.pro/docs/article/plans-and-credits))
- Uses credits or processing minutes, with documented plans that scale from about 60 minutes per month on the free tier up into the hundreds of minutes on Pro. ( [OpusClip](https://help.opus.pro/docs/article/plans-and-credits))
- Adds extras like AI B‑roll, voice‑over, and audio enhancement, which can be valuable if you need heavy post‑production on top of your StreamYard recordings.

## Why SaaS video marketing is essential

Video is one of the most powerful growth levers for SaaS companies. It cuts through complexity, clearly explains your product, and gives prospects the information they need without overwhelming them.

With video, SaaS brands can engage, educate, and persuade in ways static content cannot, turning features into stories and use cases that feel tangible and relevant. Marketers increasingly treat video as a core channel because it streamlines communication, builds credibility, and lifts conversion rates across the funnel. For SaaS businesses, where clarity and trust drive adoption, video is critical for building confidence and nurturing stronger customer relationships at every stage of the journey.

**Key benefits of SaaS video marketing**

SaaS video marketing brings a range of benefits:

- **Lead generation:** Video can help convert the audience into leads. For instance, a call-to-action incorporated at the end of the video should lead users to the signup page.
- **Higher conversion rates:** Product videos help users understand value quickly—boosting trial signups and purchases.
- **Customer Retention:** Helpful videos guide users through key actions, improve product satisfaction, and keep customers engaged over time—reducing the risk of churn.
- **Better Engagement:** Engaging, shareable videos increase visibility and bring more traffic to your brand.

AI-powered face-to-face interaction is the next level of SaaS video marketing, driving higher lead generation, conversion rates, customer engagement, and retention. Bring your website to life with Kaltura’s real-time AI avatars.

## Tips on how to build an effective SaaS video marketing strategy

If you want to build a SaaS video marketing strategy blueprint that drives real business results, you need more than just a camera and good intentions. You need a structured SaaS video marketing plan—one that aligns with your business goals, speaks to your audience, and uses the right types of video content at the right time.

1. **Define clear SaaS video marketing goals**

Before you start creating videos, it is essential to define your goals. Ask yourself:

- What do I want to achieve with this video? Is it brand awareness, lead generation, or customer education?
- Which stage of the buyer journey does this video target? Awareness, consideration, or decision?
- Who is the target audience? Are you speaking to decision-makers, end-users, or both?

Specific objectives will, therefore, inform the script development, shooting, and even video distribution. For example, if you are focused on obtaining leads, your video might offer a free report or trial.

2. **Understanding the target audience**

To truly connect, you must know your viewers. Build detailed buyer personas that include pain points, industry context, and behavior patterns.

For instance, a guide to video marketing for SaaS in the healthcare industry would focus on compliance, time savings, and trust. If your audience works in fintech, focus on automation, data integrity, or fraud prevention.

When you align video content with audience priorities, you’re not just informing—you’re converting.

3. **Crafting the right message**

Great video content in SaaS focuses on clarity, not complexity. Avoid jargon and explain how your product improves your user’s workflow in plain language.

Instead of saying, “Our software uses advanced AI for optimization,” try, “Our software helps your team cut manual tasks by 60%.” Simple, direct messaging helps your audience retain information and engage.

Focus on benefits over features. Frame every point around the value to the customer, not the tech behind it.

4. **Choose the right SaaS marketing video types for each funnel stage**

Different videos serve different purposes — and the most effective SaaS video strategies use a mix.

- **Top of Funnel (Awareness)**: Use explainer videos, brand story videos, and short social clips to generate interest.
- **Middle of Funnel (Consideration)**: Deploy product demos, educational content, and customer testimonials to build trust and answer objections.
- **Bottom of Funnel (Decision)**: Use detailed case studies, feature comparison walkthroughs, and integration tutorials to push conversions.

Mapping your SaaS marketing video types to funnel stages ensures you’re guiding your audience, not overwhelming them.

## 7 types of SaaS video marketing examples that work

A strong SaaS video marketing strategy blueprint isn’t just about making videos—it’s about choosing the right types to support every stage of your customer journey. This guide to video marketing for SaaS companies outlines the most effective formats to help you educate, convert, and retain users.

If you’re aiming to build a SaaS video marketing strategy that drives results, these examples will give you a practical foundation.

_Pro Tip: To fully implement a SaaS video marketing strategy, optimize videos for search by adding captions, titles with relevant keywords, and a supporting text transcript. This improves SEO, accessibility, and viewer engagement._

1. **Demo and explainer videos**

Demo videos are essential for SaaS companies because they clearly show how the product works and why it matters. Keep them under two minutes, and focus on a single, specific problem your software solves—for instance, showing exactly how it streamlines project management.

These videos are closely related to explainer videos, which are often animated and work best at the top of the funnel to educate your audience and spark interest in your solution. Adding visuals such as diagrams or charts keeps viewers engaged and makes complex information easier to understand.

If you are working with a tight budget or small team, you can still produce polished demos by using real-time AI video avatar tools like Kaltura to create professional, on-brand videos at scale.

2. **Case studies and testimonial videos**

Customer success stories are powerful tools for building trust. A short video featuring a satisfied customer explaining how your product helped them can be more convincing than a written review. Include specific results, like “This software reduced our onboarding time by 50%.” Including metrics and before-and-after scenarios strengthens the impact of your testimonial videos.

Testimonial formats can also benefit from AI videos, allowing for scalable case study storytelling with high production value and low overhead.

3. **Social media videos**

Video marketing becomes even more effective on social media when adapted to each platform. Short, attention-grabbing clips should be tailored to the audience and tone of each channel—for example, more professional, insight-driven videos on LinkedIn and lighter, visually playful content on Instagram.

Always optimize for mobile first, since most viewers will watch on their phones. Use subtitles so the message is clear even with the sound off, ensuring users can quickly grasp your key points in any environment.

Looking for ideas on how to build a strong culture and make knowledge sharing easier within your remote team?

[Research](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/333107966_Impacts_of_knowledge_sharing_a_review_and_directions_for_future_research) shows that knowledge sharing is essential to business success and a team’s collective intelligence when shared with everyone leads to better outcomes. The old culture of remote work meant employees were often working in isolation without learning and collaboration. It's important to introduce good knowledge sharing strategies to support your remote teams to access the information they need to succeed.

Knowledge doesn’t always have to be lengthy text documents, in-person meetings, phone calls, or VoIP calls ( [What is a VoIP call?](https://www.8x8.com/s/what-is-a-voip-caller?locale=uk)). In remote teams, video-based knowledge sharing can replace in-person meetings and discussions. Internal video sharing, when done correctly, can be a top knowledge management strategy since it's engaging.

![Video-Based Knowledge For Your Remote Teams conceptual image of virtual meeting](https://media.clipchamp.com/clipchamp/2048/OMrjuamrmeusXY47kcVyV)

Let's explore why video-based knowledge is great for any [remote team](https://marutitech.com/distributed-scrum-team/).

## Facilitating knowledge sharing among employees

According to global market intelligence firm [IDC](https://www.idc.com/), Fortune 500 companies lose a minimum of $31.5 billion every year by failing to share knowledge. That’s the tangible impact knowledge sharing has on a company, an entire industry, and the business world. Here's how knowledge sharing impacts a business:

- Reduce L&D costs because knowledge sharing happens in a community setting.

- Create knowledge assets that can strengthen organizational culture by emphasizing the vision and key values.

- Develop employees, managers, and leaders faster and in a more efficient manner.


The right people receive the right information at the right time when an efficient sharing structure is in place. Here's why video-based knowledge sharing is effective and less time consuming. Who would prefer to read a twenty-page document when they can get the same information in a two-minute video?

Clever streaming and [distribution of information](https://distantjob.com/blog/best-remote-team-tools-file-sharing/) can maximise its value without overwhelming your employees.

## 3 Major benefits of using videos for knowledge sharing

### 1\. Improved retention

Video adds variety to training modules and can improve the viewer's ability to retain information. Video-based knowledge sharing can speed up and enhance your remote employees’ overall learning experience. You can experiment with short and long videos or a combination of both to see what works the best.

### 2\. Consistent internal messaging

Leveraging video for [internal knowledge sharing](https://klutch.app/blog/culture-of-knowledge-sharing-in-the-workplace/) ensures that all remote employees and teams receive information in the same way. A video with a [good script](https://clipchamp.com/en/blog/writing-video-scripts-guide/) can help avoid misinterpretation and miscommunication.

### 3\. Independent learning

Video-based knowledge sharing is a “pull” model that enables remote employees to search for the information they need. They’re free to learn about anything they want to and whenever they want to if you help them with a platform that offers all the informational videos.

## Video ideas for remote teams

What kind of videos should you create for your [remote teams](https://trackingtime.co/remote-work/7-best-practices-to-lead-and-manage-remote-teams.html)? Here are some video types to help you get started.

### 1\. Coaching new managers

Your in-house HR team can record training videos using a camera and [screen recorder](https://clipchamp.com/en/screen-recorder/) or you can always use animated [video-based training modules](https://breadnbeyond.com/explainer-videos/). This will help them understand the workflows, internal processes, and culture better.

![Sales prospecting example GIF](https://media.clipchamp.com/clipchamp/2048/crHEFGQR2POKc408SjkOm)

### 2\. Product training videos

Filming product [demo videos](https://clipchamp.com/en/demo-video/) helps your remote team to get a better idea about the product without even if they haven't physically experienced it.

A virtual video-based experience can help them understand the specifications and features better. To create a clear and compelling video, show the product in action.

### 3\. Sales training videos

Videos are a great way to impart training related to corporate [sales strategy](https://www.trywingman.com/blog-posts/sales-strategy-101-help-your-sales-team-prospect-better-and-win-more), closing tips, important product related information, selling points and rebuttals.

Remote sales teams can also learn some awesome remote networking tips from your videos. The best part is that they can access this information at any time which helps boost their motivation during low-yielding months.

### 4\. Meeting recap videos

A little bit of video editing magic can help save your employees from Zoom fatigue. Edit your lengthy Zoom or Microsoft Teams video meetings to highlight the most important parts of the meeting.

## Take internal videos to the next level

Here’s what you can do to make sure that your remote employees are receiving the information as they best can.

- Make your videos accessible and easily viewable by adding transcripts and captions.


![step 5c-Add subtitles to videos-5 benefits and easy hacks-Clipchamp Blog](https://media.clipchamp.com/clipchamp/2048/2vDAB3KUnNTz4BUzMY32WZ)

- Use interactive elements to gamify the learning experience. Interactive video elements like quizzes and polls can make it more fun for your remote employees. You can turn a compliance video into an engaging video by introducing elements like "spot the problems".

- Collect feedback on the video training content to let your employees know that they’re being heard. You can use constructive feedback to edit existing materials.

From ads to Instagram Reels, videos capture attention. It's no different in the world of remote work. Knowledge sharing shouldn't lay on the shoulders of only a couple of employees and should be accessible to all.

# How to Expand Your SaaS Business Into New Countries

Updated on:
November 12, 2025

Author:Marta Poprotska, Social Media Community Manager

Reviewed by:George Ploaie, Chief Operating Officer (COO)

[Download Now: FREE SaaS Global Expansion Checklist](https://payproglobal.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Global-SaaS-Expansion-Checklist.pdf)

To effectively expand your SaaS business into new countries, you use a strategy that tackles market dynamics, cultural nuances, and legal and regulatory compliance. This guide presents a range of considerations to assist businesses with the complexities associated with international expansion.

Step 1

## Conduct Thorough Market Research

Before entering a new market, it is essential to research the market. Companies that conduct marketing research tend to experience a faster rate of growth compared to those who do not, with an observed difference of [2-3 times](https://www.adience.com/blog/how-to/how-marketing-research-can-help-b2b-saas-companies/). This involves several factors:

**Economic indicators:** Assess the target country’s macroeconomic environment. Examine gross domestic product (GDP) growth rates, inflation rates, employment levels, and purchasing power parity to understand the market’s overall economic health and potential customer spending power.

**Customer behavior:** Research local customer behavior patterns, including cultural nuances, purchasing habits, price sensitivity, brand loyalty, and preferred channels of communication. Gather customer behavior details through surveys, focus groups, and by analyzing customer data via social media and other online platforms. This research is important for [defining your SaaS Ideal Customer Profile (ICP)](https://payproglobal.com/how-to/define-saas-ideal-customer-profile-icp/) accurately for each new international market.

**Market trends:** Identify and analyze current market trends, including emerging technologies, shifts in customer preferences, and societal changes. Understanding these trends is necessary for anticipating market needs and beating your competitors.

**Competitive landscape:** Do analysis of the competitive environment. Identify direct and indirect competitors and their market share, strengths, weaknesses, pricing strategies, and product offerings. This analysis is vital when determining [how to price your SaaS product](https://payproglobal.com/how-to/price-saas-product/) appropriately for the new market.

**Regulatory environment:** Familiarize yourself with the legal and regulatory framework of the target country, including trade policies, tariffs, import restrictions, and any industry-specific regulations. Compliance with local laws is a must to avoid fines and lawsuits and to ensure you can continue to do business in the country.

Step 2

## Identify and Secure Local Partners

Identifying and securing the right local partner is a key step for international expansion. A local partner could assist with understanding the market, navigate the entry process, and provide connections to existing networks. Here are some considerations when seeking a potential local partner:

- **Alignment of vision and values:** Make sure potential partners share your business’s vision, values, and objectives. Alignment between parties is necessary for mitigating conflict and building long-term collaboration. Execute due diligence on their business practices, reputation, and corporate culture.
- **Market knowledge and network:** A suitable partner should have deep knowledge of the local market and a robust network of contacts, that includes customers, suppliers, and regulatory bodies. While the network’s impact on [market entry and expansion](https://payproglobal.com/products/market-expansion/) initiatives is uncertain, it may lead to variations in the time and resources needed to develop new relationships.
- **Complementary strengths and capabilities:** Look for partners who offer complementary strengths and capabilities. This might include specific industry expertise, distribution networks, marketing capabilities, or technological know-how.
- **Financial stability and resources:** Assess the financial stability and resources of potential partners. Access to expansion funding and support for operational sustainability in the initial growth stages may be contingent upon the specific nature of a partnership and the availability of resources.
- **Legal and regulatory compliance:** Confirm your local partner has a clean track record of legal and regulatory compliance. While non-compliance has the potential to bring about disruptions to business activities and brand image, steps can be taken to decrease risks. Hire legal experts to review the partner’s compliance history.

Once you have identified a potential partner, craft an agreement and negotiate terms. This process should cover all aspects of the partnership, including roles and responsibilities, financial contributions, profit-sharing, intellectual property rights, dispute resolution mechanisms, and exit strategies. Before finalizing a partnership, consider engaging in pilot projects to test the working relationship and evaluate the partner’s effectiveness in real-world conditions.

Step 3

## Adapt to Cultural Differences

In every new market, consider the cultural differences that could affect areas such as product value, marketing strategies, and hiring decisions.

- **Cultural research:** Educate yourself about the target culture’s norms, values, communication styles, and business etiquette. This research focuses on enhancing understanding with local stakeholders and addressing potential communication gaps. Engage with local experts or consultants who can provide insights into cultural nuances and how they affect business operations. Their understanding of local preferences can be leveraged to shape appropriate adjustments to your business model.
- **Customization of products and marketing:** Adapt your products, services, and marketing messages to resonate with the local audience. This might involve changing product features, packaging, or promotional strategies to align with cultural preferences and sensitivities. # The Ultimate Guide to Video Marketing Strategy

Discover the ultimate video marketing strategy guide with tips on using diverse video content, prioritizing authenticity, optimizing video length, adding multilingual subtitles, and leveraging user-generated content for maximum engagement and reach.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/63b5c0982b18e6411f506075/668e48234a4b5ecbbfbd528c_667ed738c8853a8ecc2ff20e_Video-Marketing-Strategy.avif)

Table of Contents

[2\. Authentic Content > Perfect Video Content](https://localizejs.com/articles/the-ultimate-guide-to-video-marketing-strategy#2-authentic-content-greater-perfect-video-content)

[3\. Customize Length to the Platform](https://localizejs.com/articles/the-ultimate-guide-to-video-marketing-strategy#3-customize-length-to-the-platform)

[4\. Add Subtitles and Translate Them](https://localizejs.com/articles/the-ultimate-guide-to-video-marketing-strategy#4-add-subtitles-and-translate-them)

[5\. Scale with User-Generated Content (UGC)](https://localizejs.com/articles/the-ultimate-guide-to-video-marketing-strategy#5-scale-with-user-generated-content-ugc)

[Final Thoughts](https://localizejs.com/articles/the-ultimate-guide-to-video-marketing-strategy#final-thoughts)

[Talk to an Expert](https://localizejs.com/request-demo/demo)

Share

[email-share](https://localizejs.com/articles/the-ultimate-guide-to-video-marketing-strategy#)[twitter-share](https://localizejs.com/articles/the-ultimate-guide-to-video-marketing-strategy#)[linkedin-share](https://localizejs.com/articles/the-ultimate-guide-to-video-marketing-strategy#)[facebook-share](https://localizejs.com/articles/the-ultimate-guide-to-video-marketing-strategy#)

Video is a great way to connect with potential customers. The popularity of online videos has been skyrocketing for years.

And there’s good reason: according to Forbes, viewers retain 95% of a message when they watch it in a video compared to 10% when reading. Some additional video marketing statistics include:

- **Videos Get More Organic Shares:** Social videos get 1200% more shares than image and text content combined, according to [Wordstream](https://www.wordstream.com/blog/ws/2017/03/08/video-marketing-statistics).
- **Videos Have Better ROI and Conversion Rates:** [Studies show](https://optinmonster.com/video-marketing-statistics-what-you-must-know/) that videos produce 66% more qualified leads per year and a 54% increase in brand awareness.
- **Videos Provide SEO Advantages:** [Forbes](https://www.forbes.com/sites/tjmccue/2018/06/22/video-marketing-2018-trends-continues-to-explode-as-the-way-to-reach-customers/?sh=188ba8d598dc) found that the average user spends 88% more time on websites with video content, and time-on-page activity can have a positive impact on SEO. Google’s search algorithm, moreover, is increasingly emphasizing pages that contain video content.

![Image: video marketing statistics from 2022](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/63b5c0982b18e6411f506075/66bba510b4fe445d6097df66_667ed738c8853a8ecc2ff213_online-video-averages.png)

Ready to try video in your marketing efforts? Read on for our top 5 tips for an effective video marketing strategy.

### **Test All Types of Video Content**

Ask someone what “video content” means and you’ll get ten different responses, such as:

-     Playful TikTok videos
-     Homepage explainer videos
-     Product demos
-     YouTube reviews
-     Case studies

And once you create your video, what do you do with it? Do you share it on all social channels or just specific ones?

Our recommendation is to experiment with all types of video content - although obviously, your industry will have an impact here (SaaS companies may err toward explainer videos over short-form content). You may find, for instance, that product demos have great engagement rates on Twitter, but your TikTok strategy is falling flat. Once you know what works, you can then double down on that strategy.

Here are the formats and channels most recommended by expert [video marketers](https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/best-video-marketing-channels):

**Long-form videos:**

-     YouTube
-     Instagram

**Short-form videos:**

-     TikTok
-     Instagram Reels
-     YouTube Shorts

**Live streaming:**

-     YouTube Live
-     Instagram Live
-     Facebook Live

## 2\. Authentic Content > Perfect Video Content

[Low-fi videos with a DIY feel](https://business.instagram.com/blog/trends-low-production-video) are trending with businesses. For example, Dunkin uses Instagram Reels to share short product videos. With just a smartphone, they share eye-catching videos with fans on social media:

**View this post on Instagram**

[A post shared by Dunkin’ (@dunkin)](https://www.instagram.com/tv/Ch7L5gXsOEF/?utm_source=ig_embed&utm_campaign=loading)

Research from [HubSpot](https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/video-marketing) shows that most Internet users actually prefer imperfect, authentic content over high-quality videos that seem artificial.

Think about it this way—are you likely to trust a big-budget product review directly from a company’s website? Or would you rather trust a Youtuber who’s sharing their real thoughts?

## 3\. Customize Length to the Platform

The length of your videos is important - and should vary based on what platform(s) you’re sharing them on.

For instance, TikTok videos should be between 9 and 15 seconds, while YouTube videos offer more opportunity for long-form content.

![Image: Ideal content length for video marketing](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/63b5c0982b18e6411f506075/66bba510b4fe445d6097df5d_667ed738c8853a8ecc2ff216_video-length-for-content-marketing.png)

Here are video length suggestions by platform:

-     Instagram: 30 seconds
-     Twitter: 45 seconds
-     Facebook: 1 minute
-     TikTok: 15 seconds
-     YouTube: 2 minutes (or more)

## 4\. Add Subtitles and Translate Them

According to Verizon, [80%](https://www.streamingmedia.com/Articles/News/Online-Video-News/80-of-Video-Caption-Users-Arent-Hearing-Impaired-Finds-Verizon-131860.aspx) of people are more likely to watch an entire video when there are subtitles. Plus, about [69%](https://www.forbes.com/sites/tjmccue/2019/07/31/verizon-media-says-69-percent-of-consumers-watching-video-with-sound-off/?sh=2e736e0535d8) of people prefer to watch videos with the sound off.

English subtitles are an excellent start, but subtitles in additional languages can expand your reach even further. Doing this also highlights your commitment to delighting all customers and users - no matter where in the world they are.

(As a small side note, always remember to include multilingual metadata - like tags, hyperlinks, descriptions, and titles - when uploading to popular video-sharing platforms. This will improve your reach.)

Don’t know where to start with subtitle translation? We recommend [this subtitle translation tool](https://localizejs.com/products/mobile) to easily localize the text.

## 5\. Scale with User-Generated Content (UGC)

User-generated content ( [UGC](https://www.forbes.com/sites/forbescommunicationscouncil/2022/08/04/16-creative-ways-brands-can-leverage-youtube-in-2022/?sh=f0cb60549722)) is any content created by people (such as users or customers or reviewers), rather than by the brand itself. Studies show that consumers are [2.4](https://blog.hootsuite.com/user-generated-content-ugc/#Why_is_user-generated_content_important) times more likely to view user-genera

# HR’s Guide to Cultural Competence in a Global Workforce

**Erika Sandoval**
Global Senior Partner, HCM Strategic Advisory Group


Oct 2, 2024
Read time:
5
minutes


![A cross-functional team collaborating around a table, analyzing data.](https://www.ukg.com/sites/default/files/styles/teal_16x9_aspect_ratio_1000x563/public/2024-09/cultural%20competence.jpg.webp?h=a1637381&itok=-1IROSpM)

In dynamic and interconnected business environments, HR professionals are faced with both new challenges and opportunities. Cultural competence is more than just understanding different customs and practices—it is about fostering an inclusive organizational culture where diversity is valued and embraced. For HR leaders, cultural competence is essential for effective leadership, stronger global strategies, and an enhanced employee experience.

Having lived and worked across four continents, I have personally witnessed the profound impact of cultural competence in the workplace, which has shaped my commitment to making it a central pillar of my approach to people. I have seen how a lack of it can lead to miscommunication, missed opportunities, and disengagement. But I have also seen the opposite: how fostering cultural competence can transform organizations, strengthen teams, and drive innovation.

Cultural competence has a transformative power, and HR plays a vital role in cultivating it to promote an inclusive and globally successful workplace.

## Why Cultural Competence Matters

To understand the importance of cultural competence in HR, it is essential to consider the broader implications of diversity in the workplace. Businesses are more global than ever, meaning teams are not only culturally diverse but also often spread across various geographies. Whether you are an HR leader of a U.S. tech company expanding into Asian markets or a European manufacturer tapping into Latin American talent pools, the ability to navigate and manage cultural differences is essential for HR leaders.

When employees feel misunderstood or underrepresented due to their cultural background, it can erode trust and loyalty, leading to a decline in productivity and collaboration. On the other hand, fostering cultural competence creates a workplace where [**employees feel valued and respected**](https://www.ukg.com/blog/hr-leaders/aligning-shared-values-workplace-8-strategies-success "Aligning Shared Values in the Workplace: 8 Strategies for Success"). This leads to:

**Stronger collaboration**: Employees from diverse backgrounds who feel heard are more likely to work well together.

**Greater innovation**: Diverse teams bring diverse perspectives, which can spark creative solutions and drive innovation.

**Improved talent retention**: Employees who feel appreciated and respected are more likely to stay with an organization, reducing turnover costs.

**Global growth**: Companies that are culturally competent can better navigate global markets, manage international teams, and build strong customer relationships.

## The HR Role in Fostering Cultural Competence

For HR, cultural competence goes beyond recognizing different cultural norms. It is about creating an inclusive workplace regardless of an employee's background, and HR professionals are at the forefront of embedding cultural competence into the fabric of their organizations.

It’s not just about diversity training or complying with anti-discrimination laws—it’s about equipping teams with the tools they need to truly understand and manage cultural differences. Culturally competent HR teams can better:

**Navigate cross-cultural communication**: HR leaders need to [**understand different communication styles**](https://www.ukg.com/blog/hr-leaders/7-effective-ways-improve-your-communication-skills "7 Effective Ways to Improve Your Communication Skills"), whether direct or indirect, to ensure everyone is heard. During my time working for a British organization in Southeast Asia, I quickly realized that even though I had personal experience with indirect and direct communication styles, this region operated with a higher indirect style and people often use subtle, nonverbal cues. It was a valuable lesson in how cultural awareness can prevent miscommunication and collaboration.

**Foster inclusion**: Creating an environment where diverse perspectives are encouraged leads to innovative solutions. HR must ensure that policies and practices reflect inclusivity, from hiring processes to [**performance evaluations**](https://www.ukg.com/blog/hr-leaders/9-tips-boost-your-performance-review-process "9 Tips to Boost Your Performance Review Process").

**Reduce conflict**: Misunderstandings due to cultural differences can lead to workplace conflict. HR plays a critical role in resolving these issues through cultural awareness and mediation.

## Practical Strategies for Building Cultural Competence

Building cultural competence within an organization requires both commitment and action. Here are some effective strategies HR leaders can implement:

### Develop a cultural competence framework

Cultural competence should be embedded into the organization’s DNA. HR teams can create a framework that outlines the key competencies employees and leaders need—such as self-awareness of one’s own cultural worldview, attitudes toward cultural differences, and knowledge of other cultures’ practices. This framework should be integrated into leadership development programs, team-building activities, and employee evaluations.

### Leverage Employee Resource Groups (ERGs)

[**ERGs**](https://www.ukg.com/blog/hr-leaders/what-are-employee-resource-groups-ergs "What Are Employee Resource Groups (ERGs)?") offer employees from various backgrounds a platform to share experiences and insights into how their cultures influence their work and interactions. HR can support these groups by providing resources and encouraging active participation from all levels of the organization, including leadership.

### Encourage cross-cultural mentorship

Mentorship programs that connect employees from diverse cultural backgrounds can promote mutual learning and a deeper appreciation for diverse perspectives. HR can facilitate cross-cultural mentorship opportunities, breaking down cultural barriers and fostering a more inclusive workplace.

### Measure and track progress

It is important for HR to measure the impact of cultural competence initiatives. This can be done through employee surveys, feedback sessions, and diversity audits. By tracking progress, HR can celebrate successes, identify areas for improvement, and ensure that cultural competence remains a priority.

### Tailor approaches to local contexts

Global companies must recognize that a one-size-fits-all approach won’t work in every region. Having been raised biculturally and bilingually, I understood early in my career that cultural competence initiatives must be tailored to reflect the local context. What proves effective in one culture may not work in another. It is essential to avoid a U.S.-centric perspective when designing global strategies, ensuring that policies and practices are inclusive and relevant to the unique cultural dynamics of each region.

## The Role of Technology in Enhancing Cultural Competence

Technology has transformed HR in many ways, and when combined with cultural competence, it can be a powerful tool for driving inclusivity and innovation. With the rise of remote and hybrid workforces operating across different time zones, technology is essential for managing global teams effectively.

### Invest in HR tech platforms

![b2b video marketing](https://images.ctfassets.net/ddoznp8df51r/63Ma4tS5JRVNMgIgzded0E/611ab13e335db58c67678870988554f9/b2b-video-marketing.png?w=600&fm=jpg&q=100)

## 60-Second Summary

Since the first YouTube video in 2005, online video has become a dominant channel—people now watch over a billion hours of YouTube daily. For B2B marketers, video is no longer optional: it improves engagement, supports multi-stakeholder buying processes, and outperforms many traditional formats.

- **Key takeaways**: Video is highly effective for B2B—driving engagement, retention, SEO support, and conversions; 62% of B2B marketers rank video as their top format and studies show video outperforms print/direct mail by wide margins.

- **Standout strategies and tactics**: Keep top-of-funnel videos short, use customer stories for mid-funnel trust, repurpose long-form content into video, and leverage trends or self-deprecation (e.g., Hootsuite) to address objections and win buy-in.

- **Real-world lessons and frameworks**: Align video format and length to the buyer’s funnel stage, showcase customer outcomes (Salesforce/Barclays example), and use video to dismantle multi-stakeholder buy-in barriers with clear, concise demonstrations.

- **Practical implementation tips**: Start small with affordable tools, repurpose existing content, choose platforms based on buyer personas, measure views and conversions, and scale investment as video proves ROI.

# How to create product demo videos that engage and convert

Your product is amazing, and it deserves to be showcased in the best possible way to attract potential customers. And what better way to do that than by creating a stunning product demo video?

In today’s digital age, having product demo videos on your company’s website and social media channels is crucial. In fact, research shows that [84% of consumers](https://www.hubspot.com/marketing-statistics) have made a purchase decision after watching a brand’s video, and nearly [50% of internet users](https://www.thinkwithgoogle.com/consumer-insights/consumer-trends/video-searches-before-store-visits) look for product videos before visiting a company’s stores.

So, if you’re not already [demo videos for your software and products](https://screenpal.com/video-promo), you’re missing out on a significant opportunity to attract customers and increase sales. But don’t worry, this article will guide you on how to create engaging product videos and provide examples of some of the best demo videos in the market. Get ready to showcase your product like a pro!

- [**Where to promote your demo video**](https://screenpal.com/blog/product-demo-videos#where-to-promote-product-demo-videos)
- [**6 great product demo video examples**](https://screenpal.com/blog/product-demo-videos#product-demo-video-examples)

## **What is a product demo video?**

A product demo video goes beyond words by vividly illustrating the inner workings of your product, while simultaneously showcasing its unique benefits to captivate your audience. By harnessing the power of visual storytelling, product videos emerge as a highly impactful tool for engaging and converting website visitors. In just one minute of compelling visual content, you can convey a wealth of information about your product that surpasses the effectiveness of a traditional white paper or web page. Embrace the persuasive potential of product demo videos to leave a lasting impression on your viewers and drive meaningful connections with your audience.

## **How can demo videos benefit your business?**

- **Build awareness**: When your viewers watch your demo videos, they’re more likely to remember your brand. 63% of consumers report that [videos helped keep brands top of mind](https://motioncue.com/video-marketing-statistics/#Consumer-trends-2022). Your product demo video can also help you distinguish your brand from other similar companies.
- **Explain products**: Your demo video can guide potential users or customers through every aspect of the product’s functionality. Viewers are more likely to trust your products if they can see them working. 95% of all marketers say that [videos have helped users better understand their company’s products](https://www.hubspot.com/marketing-statistics) or services.
- **Increase traffic**: Video is increasingly important for SEO, and research shows that a website is [53 times more likely to reach the front page of Google](https://www.insivia.com/2023-saas-marketing-report/?) if it includes a video. Over [25% of searchers click on the first organic search result they see](https://www.searchenginejournal.com/google-first-page-clicks/374516), so getting on page one and as high as possible will dramatically increase your website traffic.
- **Increase sales**: When users trust your products because they’ve seen them working, they’re more likely to make a purchase. Including demo videos allows you to increase your product’s sales considerably.

Given the statistics and video trends we’ve noted, including a product demo video on your company’s landing page or marketing material is a no-brainer.

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

## **What should a product demo video include?**

Your [video promo](https://screenpal.com/video-promo) should include four basic elements: an overview of your product, the solution your product offers, how your product works, and a call-to-action.

Make sure that you have identified your product’s unique value proposition and can communicate it clearly and quickly in your demo video. Why did you make this product? Who is it for? What problems does it solve for your customers, and how does it solve them differently or better than your competition?

The following is a general overview of the structure of your demo video:

- **Description**: Start with an introduction to your product’s basic functionality and usefulness.
- **Solution**: Communicate your value proposition. What problems and pain points does your product solve, and how will your customer’s life be better with your product.
- **Demonstration**: Guide the viewer through your product’s functionality and capabilities.
- **Call-to-action**: Ask the viewer to act — buy the product, request more information, etc.

## **How long should a product demo video be?**

When creating a product demo video, strive for brevity and clarity. The goal is to effectively convey your product’s value proposition and functionality in two minutes or less. For more in-depth information, consider developing additional educational videos or extended demos to cater to a more engaged audience. However, the initial product demo video should be succinct and engaging, lasting no longer than 2 minutes. By maintaining a concise and focused approach, you can deliver a compelling overview of your product that captures the attention of viewers and drives interest in your offering.

## **How to create a product demo video**

### **Step 1: Establish the goal of your demo video**

A strategic approach is crucial when creating your demo video, beginning with clearly defining your goals. Are you seeking to increase sales or subscriptions, enhance brand awareness, or educate existing users? Pinpointing your video’s primary goal and des

# Global HR Management: 9 Strategies To Ensure International Success

[70% of cross-border collaborations fail](https://blogs.humansmart.com.mx/blog-how-does-crosscultural-communication-impact-knowledge-management-practices-54274) due to cultural differences, stalling knowledge sharing and teamwork. Global HR management can address these challenges and foster stronger collaboration, measurable growth, and lasting competitive advantage.

Written by Monique Verduyn

Reviewed by [Cheryl Marie Tay](https://www.aihr.com/editors/)

Global HR management (HRM) is the art and science of managing people across borders, ensuring organizational uniformity while adapting to local realities. Additionally, the global HRM market, recently valued at [$24 billion](https://contentsnare.com/hr-statistics), continues growing as more companies expand internationally.

Strong global HRM makes it possible to build one connected workforce without losing sight of regional differences. This article discusses what it entails, how it differs from domestic HRM, and nine strategies you can use to ensure global HRM success.

[![](https://www.aihr.com/wp-content/plugs/podcast-blog-ads-fade.jpg)](https://www.aihr.com/business/ai-for-hr-boot-camp/?il_id=banner&il_name=enterprise&il_creative=b2b-ai-bootcamp-2&il_position=slot2)

## What is global HR management?

Global HRM aims to deliver a consistent employee experience worldwide while respecting relevant local laws, customs, and expectations. This balance helps organizations stay competitive and attract top talent across diverse nations and regions.

### Functions

Global HR management handles core HR areas across multiple countries and regions. These areas include hiring, onboarding, payroll, compliance, training, and [performance management](https://www.aihr.com/blog/what-is-performance-management/). The main challenge is to make sure these functions run smoothly within different legal systems and cultural settings.

### Purpose

The purpose of global HRM is to deliver a consistent employee experience worldwide, while respecting relevant local laws, customs, and expectations. This balance helps organizations stay competitive and attract top talent across diverse nations and regions.

### Global vs. domestic HRM

Domestic HR management focuses on a single country, legal system, and cultural context. Global HR management, on the other hand, deals with multiple legal frameworks, languages, time zones, and employee expectations. This requires greater flexibility and coordination.

### Localization and cohesion

Because global [HR policies](https://www.aihr.com/blog/hr-policies/) must be unified but flexible, companies must set common standards that reflect their mission and values, then adapt them to fit local labor laws and cultural practices. This approach keeps organizations cohesive while ensuring employees everywhere feel supported.

## Global vs. domestic HR management: Key differences

Global HR management

Domestic HR management

**Scope of operations**

Spread across multiple countries, requires balancing global consistency with local adaptation.

Limited to one country, a single set of HR practices is required.

**Legal requirements**

Must comply with diverse labor laws, tax systems, social security, and data privacy rules.

One legal and regulatory framework to follow.

**Cultural context**

Operates across multiple cultural contexts with varied values, customs, and work expectations.

Works within a single national culture and set of norms.

**Workforce diversity**

Highly diverse workforce across nationalities, ethnicities, and time zones.

Less variation in employee backgrounds and work styles.

**Technology needs**

Requires integrated HR technology platforms to manage global data, payroll, and compliance.

Localized HR systems are usually sufficient.

**Comms**

Asynchronous communication is often necessary, with varying meeting etiquette and time zone challenges.

Fewer time zone barriers make it easier to maintain real-time communication.

**Policy design**

Needs a flexible global HR framework to adapt to local laws and practices.

Centralized, standardized policies across the organization.

**Talent management**

Must manage global mobility, expatriates, international recruitment, and cross-border training.

Focused on recruiting, training, and retention within a single local labor market.

**Strategic focus**

Aligned with global business strategy while adapting HR practices to local needs.

Focused on national business strategy.

**Risk management**

Higher exposure to political instability, currency fluctuations, global crises, and cultural misalignment.

Fewer external risks; mainly focused on local compliance and employee relations.

**Training and development**

Training must be globally consistent while accommodating local learning styles.

Standardized learning programs geared to local skills needs.

**Performance management**

Complex systems to fairly measure and compare employees across different cultures, markets, and legal systems.

One framework for evaluation and rewards.

**Mobility and relocation**

A major function; involves expat assignments, visas, relocation support, and repatriation programs.

Rarely an issue, except in the case of domestic transfers.

**Cost structure**

Fluctuates due to currency exchange rates, cost-of-living differences, and international benefits packages.

More predictable HR costs, based on one economy.

## Main global HRM challenges and how to overcome them

Managing workforces in different geographies and cultural contexts presents unique challenges for HR. Here are some of the most common challenges and how you can address them:

### Legal and regulatory compliance

- **Challenge:** Every country has its own employment laws, tax rules, and privacy regulations. For example, the EU’s GDPR has strict data protection standards, while U.S. laws take a differen
