### Do I need localization to go to market in a new country?

The short answer is yes. When launching a product or brand in a new country, you need to ensure it’s well-suited to the target market. However, the amount of work you need to do can vary greatly.

For some markets, this may be a case of translating key landing pages. If you share a language, it could be as simple as just changing the available payment options. For others, you may need to implement a complete transcreation strategy - updating and adapting all your content to better suit unique cultural touchstones.

Whatever the scope of the project, localization is key to ensuring the best possible launch.

### Differences for different businesses

To what extent you'll need to localize depends on business factors like target audience, competition, and culture.

For instance, if you’re targeting a market that’s very different from your existing ones, the best course of action might be to rebuild your GTM strategy from scratch.

Alternatively, if you’re targeting a market that’s quite similar, you may only need to translate your content and make minor alterations to your sales strategy.

Sometimes it can be even easier than that - for instance, if you’re a US company targeting the UK market, you might only need to find a local banking partner and start accepting orders

**Pros of localizing upon release**

By localizing your product release, you’ll eliminate barriers to a successful launch. These barriers include:

- Hard barriers (such as legal, consumption, and language)
- Soft barriers (such as sentiment, branding, and some use cases)

By ensuring you comply with local regulations, licenses, and legal requirements, you can ensure you hit the ground running and can be taking orders from day one. Plus, by speaking the local language, you’ll have a much broader reach and a greater chance of bringing in the customers you want.

Going beyond this and addressing soft barriers is a sign of truly a great localization GTM strategy. This goes beyond language, adapting your brand’s identity and messaging to local sentiment and likely use cases.

If you want to have a successful hard launch, it's best to serve users with the best possible version of your product, and that means localizing it.

**Cons of localizing upon release**

While it's best to localize your GTM strategy, you may need to overcome specific localization challenges. These include:

- **It’s expensive and time-consuming.** As we’ve mentioned, localization involves far more than translation. It also involves market research, international SEO, and more, which eat into your timeframe and budget.
- **It’s a nightmare to manage.** It can be challenging to manage all the different aspects of your localization strategy, especially if you lack in-depth knowledge of your target market.
- **It slows everything down.** Adding in the extra steps required to localize can make everything slower. In particular, if you have to make changes to accommodate a new alphabet or change your UX, you may cause delays.
- **It may require hiring someone.** If your target market is very different from your original market, you may need to hire a local subject matter expert. They can help you determine what to localize, but hiring them adds to your expenses.

### Localize less; building a localization MVP

One way to reduce the expenses and time frame associated with localization is to create a localization MVP (minimum viable product). That way, you can test the waters by focusing on a few key pieces of content and measuring the results. If the market reaction is positive, you can invest in a full localization一confident your investment won’t go to waste.

To build your localization MVP, ask yourself questions like:

- What are your goals for the new market?
- What do you need to localize to give users a good enough experience to decide to buy your product?
- What content do you need to localize to generate enough traffic and publicity?
- What legal material do you need to localize to ensure you meet local requirements?

### What constitutes a localization MVP?

A localization MVP involves identifying the key pieces of content to localize. You need to research the content your target audience is most interested in and assess your existing content’s popularity.

If your target audience prefers blog posts, localize your popular blog posts first. If they prefer solutions, concentrate on your FAQ pages, how-to guides, and manuals. For instance, Diana Georgieva, Senior Localization Manager at Pleo, says:

“I prioritized an MVP of localization based on what we had produced. So the apps, the website, the most popular landing pages, the most popular help center articles.”

By creating a localization MVP, Pleo was able to conquer 10 new markets in only 10 months! Check out our article on how they did it at Pleo to learn more.

![image1-Apr-06-2023-09-04-14-7447-AM](https://www.globalapptesting.com/hs-fs/hubfs/image1-Apr-06-2023-09-04-14-7447-AM.png?width=1946&height=1004&name=image1-Apr-06-2023-09-04-14-7447-AM.png)

### What is a localization MVP team?

Your localization MVP team is a group of people who'll carry out your localization GTM strategy. There are several options to choose from, each with pros and cons:

- **Asking someone to translate in-house**. In-house translators have detailed knowledge of your company and product. But they may only have expertise in a few languages and lack localization experience.

- **Using machine translation software.** This is a cost-effective option that’s much faster than manual translation. It's great for simple text like product descriptions but can struggle to accurately translate complex text. If you use this option, be sure to review the results.

- **Hiring a localization professional or team.** These professionals have hands-on localization experience and expertise. This is a more expensive option, though, and you’ll have to train them in your brand voice and terminology.

- **Using an LSP or external party**. This is the best option for multiple languages and specialized content. LSPs draw on a large pool of translators and localization experts, so have a short turnaround. The downside is the cost and the lack of intimate brand knowledge.

### **Avoiding embarrassing mistakes**

To avoid mistakes, you need to use localization testing and quality assurance before going to market. QA ensures your product works as expected and your localized content is up to scratch.

The tests you’ll need to run vary depending on your product and target market. But, at the very least, you need to ensure your product works in ter

Global Podcasting: Strategies for Translating Your Audio Content

# **Global Podcasting: Strategies for Translating Your Audio Content**

Podcasting has quickly become an influential means of storytelling, education, and brand development with millions of listeners around the world. Yet, as the podcasting world globalizes, language limitations can hinder audience reach.

Translating your audio content is a crucial strategy for expanding to international markets and making your podcast accessible to diverse audiences.

Let’s explore the most effective strategies for translating podcasts and ensuring that your message resonates globally.

![](https://www.theinvestorspodcast.com/?seraph_accel_gi=wp-content%2Fcache%2Fseraphinite-accelerator%2Fs%2Fm%2Fd%2Fimg%2F5faafa21557aa510debef197495398a8.23faa.jpeg&n=4qe0bDKpA5QfHig5DqDA)

## Why Translate Your Podcast?

The international podcasting market is thriving, with millions of shows on offer in various niches. However, most podcasts are created in English or other dominant languages, with little option for non-native speakers. Translating your podcast advantages:

- Enrich your audience base by reaching non-English-speaking markets.
- Increase interaction and brand recognition in global markets.
- Improve accessibility and inclusivity for multicultural communities.
- Maximize monetization potential by accessing international sponsorship and ad bases.

Since we now know why podcast translation matters, let us discuss the optimal ways to go about it successfully.

## How to Translate Podcasts for a Global Audience?

### Select the Suitable Translation Method

Podcast translation is not merely a matter of exchanging words in one language with another. Proper adaptation is called for to keep the tone, style, and cultural connotations of the source material intact. There are three main ways of doing this:

- AI-Based Translation Tools: Advanced AI technology, like a [voice translator](https://murf.ai/tools/audio-translator), can translate and synthesize speech into several languages automatically. Voice translator tools, for instance, can translate spoken content into another language without losing vocal traits.
- Professional Human Translators: For culturally rich, high-quality translations, using professional linguists guarantees your message is translated accurately.
- Hybrid Approach: A combination of AI technology and human checking guarantees efficiency while retaining natural-sounding translations.

Both methods have their advantages and disadvantages. AI translation is cheap and fast, but human translators offer more authenticity and cultural translation.

### Employ AI Voice Dubbing for Smooth Translations

Rather than offer subtitles or transcripts, AI voice dubbing enables you to produce multilingual versions of your podcast with natural-sounding voice synthesis. AI-based dubbing technologies can:

- Produce natural-sounding voices in various languages.
- Sync tone and emotional expressions.
- Facilitate quick turnaround times for dubbed episodes.

Through AI-powered dubbing, podcasters can quickly produce multilingual content without the necessity of manually re-recording every episode.

### Localize Your Content for Cultural Relevance

Translation is not just about word substitution; it’s about making content relevant to different cultures. A literal translation will not always be pleasing to international listeners, so the following should be considered.

- Idioms and Expressions: Some words do not have literal meanings and may require cultural translation.
- Regional Preferences: Content tailoring based on audience demographics increases engagement.
- Tone and Style: A formal tone is preferred by some cultures, whereas others like a conversational tone.

Collaborating with native speakers or localization specialists can help your podcast remain culturally authentic.

### Offer Multilingual Transcripts and Subtitles

Even if complete audio translation is not possible, transcribing and captioning in various languages can make your podcast more accessible. Transcripts have several advantages:

- Enhance [search engine optimization](https://searchengineland.com/guide/what-is-seo) (SEO) for easier discoverability.
- Enable non-native speakers to read along with the original audio.
- Make your podcast more accessible to the deaf or hard-of-hearing population.

There are automatic translation features that come with most transcription software, which helps make it easier to generate multilingual text versions of your podcast shows.

## Optimize Metadata for Worldwide Reach

Metadata also plays a significant role in how your podcast is discovered and where it is located. To gain more visibility, try:

- Translating episode titles, descriptions, and summary statements.
- Utilizing keywords that appeal to foreign audiences.
- Posting your podcast on directories that have listeners who do not speak English.

Optimizing metadata allows global audiences to find and interact with your translated show.

## Collaborate with Global Podcast Distributors

In order to maximize worldwide accessibility, release your podcast on platforms that support various audiences. Some worldwide podcast directories and platforms are:

- Apple Podcasts and Spotify (support multilingual options and global accessibility).
- Google Podcasts (enables worldwide discoverability with translated metadata).
- Regional Sites like Gaana (India) and Ximalaya (China), which support local markets.

By posting your podcast globally, you can gain international listeners of various linguistic bases.

## Utilize Multilingual Marketing Strategies

Once your podcast is translated, you’ll need an effective marketing strategy to promote it in global markets. Some key approaches include:

- Multilingual Social Media Campaigns: Reach global audiences by sharing translated content on Twitter, Instagram, and LinkedIn.
- Influencer Collaborations: Partner with influencers who speak the target language to promote your podcast.
- Paid Advertising: Run localized ad campaigns targeting specific regions.
- A successful marketing plan will make your translated podcast popular globally.

## Conclusion

Translation of your podcast is a good means of opening up your global audience, improving accessibility, and generating more engagement. Through the assistance of AI-based translation software, human translation expertise, voice dubbing, and focused marketing campaigns, you can successfully break language barriers and reach listeners across the world.

## Mastering Multilingual Video Marketing for Global Reach

To scale your e-commerce brand globally, you must speak the language of your customers—literally. Multilingual video marketing removes the friction of "foreignness," transforming a distant product into a local solution. When viewers see content in their native tongue, trust increases significantly, directly impacting your bottom line.

### The Strategic Advantage of Multilingual Content

- **Enhanced SEO:** Translated captions allow search engines to index your videos in multiple languages, increasing global search visibility.
- **Higher Engagement:** Subtitled videos are watched longer, especially in "sound-off" environments like public transit or offices.
- **Reduced Friction:** Clear product instructions in a local language reduce customer service inquiries and lower return rates.

### How to Localize Your Video in 3 Steps

You don't need a translation team to go global. Follow this workflow to adapt your content efficiently:

1. **Record Once:** Film your core product demo or brand story in your native language using a clear, concise script.
.
2. **Translate and Review:** Convert those captions into the languages of your target markets—such as Spanish, French, or Japanese—to ensure your message resonates across borders.

. This systematic approach allows you to work smarter, turning a single recording into a worldwide sales engine without the need for expensive equipment or large teams.

## Integrating Video into the E-commerce Experience to Convert More Customers

Once you have mastered global reach, the next step is turning that attention into transactions. Integrating video directly into your e-commerce store transforms a static shopping cart into an interactive experience. This transition is vital because customers are significantly more likely to purchase after watching a product video that demonstrates value and utility.

### Where to Embed Video for High Impact

- **Product Detail Pages (PDPs):** Use 30-second demos to answer common questions and reduce the "uncertainty gap" that leads to abandoned carts.
- **Post-Purchase Emails:** Send "How-to" videos to decrease return rates and build long-term loyalty by ensuring customers use the product correctly.
.

### How to Implement Video Without Production Burnout

Small businesses often struggle with the production bottleneck. To overcome this, focus on high-utility content rather than cinematic perfection. The goal is to be helpful, not just flashy.

1. **Audit Your Top Sellers:** Identify the products with the highest traffic but lowest conversion rates. These are your priority for video integration.
2. **Leverage BIGVU Captions:** Use the BIGVU captioning tool to ensure your value proposition is clear even when muted. This is essential for mobile shoppers who browse in public spaces and rely on text to follow the narrative.
.

By embedding video into the shopping journey, you solve the "visibility without clarity" pain point. You aren't just getting views; you are providing the specific information a customer needs to click "checkout" with confidence. This systematic approach ensures your video assets work as hard as your sales team.

## Mastering Viral Psychology and Platform Tactics to Capture Global Attention

To truly scale your global brand, your content must do more than exist—it must move. Virality isn't a stroke of luck; it is a result of tapping into human psychology. When you combine multilingual accessibility with the right emotional triggers, you create a self-sustaining marketing engine that captures attention across borders without requiring a massive advertising budget.

### The Psychology of Shareability

Understanding why people share is the key to reducing your production bottleneck. Focus on these three psychological pillars to make your videos "sticky":

- **Social Currency:** People share content that makes them look informed or helpful to their peers. Provide "insider" tips or unique product hacks.
- **High-Arousal Emotions:** Content that triggers awe, excitement, or amusement is far more likely to be shared than content that is merely informative.
- **Practical Value:** In the e-commerce space, "How-to" videos that solve a specific problem quickly are highly shareable because they offer immediate utility.

### How to Engineer a Viral Loop in 3 Steps

You don't need a Hollywood studio to go viral. Use this systematic approach to optimize your videos for maximum reach:

1. **Master the 3-Second Hook:** Start with a visual or verbal "pattern interrupt" that addresses a customer pain point immediately. This stops the scroll and signals relevance.
2. **Optimize for Silent Browsing:** Use the **BIGVU captioning tool** to ensure your message is clear without sound. Viral videos are often consumed on the go; if they can't read your value proposition, they won't share it.
3. **Include a Clear Social Call-to-Action:** Don't just ask for a sale. Ask viewers to "Tag a friend who needs this" to trigger the platform's recommendation algorithm.

By aligning your video strategy with these psychological triggers, you overcome the "visibility without clarity" trap. You aren't just posting content; you are creating assets that your audience actively wants to distribute for you, effectively multiplying your marketing efforts while keeping your resource investment low.

IN THIS ARTICLE

So you’re an American business. You sell digital products and your customer segments are all English speakers first. However, a recent addition to your product line has exploded in popularity in China, Taiwan, and Vietnam.

**Hooray?**

You can _hip-hip_ but hold the _hooray_ until you’ve addressed the new problem you’re presented with: how to deliver content marketing to an entirely new market that they can understand.

Multilingual video content has emerged as a powerful tool to overcome language barriers and connect with diverse cultural audiences.

This blog explores how multilingual strategies, supported by AI technologies like **digital avatars** and **AI video editors**, can enhance global reach and engagement.

## Benefits of multilingual video content

**Cultural relevance** Tailoring content to resonate with diverse cultural groups is key to building brand affinity. By offering videos in multiple languages, brands can ensure their messages are culturally relevant and respectful, fostering deeper connections with audiences.

**I understand, therefore I buy.** Audiences are more likely to engage with content presented in their native language. According to a study by Common Sense Advisory, _72.4% of consumers are more likely to buy a product with information in their own language._

Go figure.

If they engage, they’re moving through the funnel. If they can’t understand your videos, see you later, alligator.

**SEO advantages** Multilingual video content can improve search engine optimization by ranking in various language-specific search results. This increases visibility and attracts more traffic from global markets. This is particularly true now that AI is becoming increasingly efficient at determining key words presented in-video as opposed to video descriptions only.

**Brand equity** Providing multilingual content demonstrates a brand's commitment to inclusivity and diversity, enhancing brand equity. It shows that a brand values its global audience and is willing to invest in meeting their needs. Apart from that, you’re just hitting more people, meaning more opportunity to build affinity.

**Personalization** Creating personalized messages for different language speakers enhances the customer experience. AI tools, such as **text to video AI** and **AI ads generators,** can facilitate the creation of personalized multilingual content efficiently and at scale.

[Creatify](https://creatify.ai/blog) offers a suite of AI-powered tools that simplify the creation of bilingual video content:

- **Digital avatars**: Use digital avatars to present content in multiple languages. These avatars can be customized to deliver messages accurately and engagingly. They are available in a wide array of languages and personas.

- **URL-to-video:** Convert a simple product URL into engaging video formats in multiple languages with diverse avatars with Creatify's powerful tool. All you need is the URL from the product page! Here’s [a guide](https://creatify.ai/blog/how-to-create-video-ads-with-ai) on the process

- **Batch Mode**: Create bulks of ads or marketing content in various languages using the accelerative Batch Mode. This tool enables you to quickly develop multilingual ad campaigns that resonate with diverse audiences; theoretically, you can make _thousands_ in a day. Try it [here](https://creatify.ai/features/batch-mode).


By utilizing Creatify's AI tools, businesses can efficiently produce bilingual content that appeals to global audiences, enhancing their marketing strategy and expanding their reach.

## Conclusion

Multilingual video content is essential for brands looking to expand their global reach and engage with diverse audiences. By leveraging AI technologies like digital avatars and AI video editors, businesses can efficiently create personalized, culturally relevant content that resonates with global consumers. Whether you’re based in America or Andorra, AI makes translating your product or service globally, easy.

You invested time and resources to make a high-quality video and it worked! The view count kept rising and you sat back satisfied in a job well done. But now the watch count is slowing. Maybe it’s time to think about how you can repurpose video content.

With the right long-term distribution strategy, that first flare of interest could be just the beginning. You can repurpose video content to put it in front of more audiences and share it in new settings.

### Two Quick Quality Checks Before You Go Any Further

Before you think about ways to repurpose video content, make sure your video is ready for the big time. It should have transcriptions and captions ready to go. Both of these help improve your video’s reach and accessibility wherever you choose to share it.

Transcriptions allow search engine bots to better understand and categorize your video content. That means you’re more likely to [snag organic traffic](https://idearocketanimation.com/21837-first-page-google-search-results/). Even if your video [doesn’t include narration](https://idearocketanimation.com/4400-explainer-video-without-voiceover/), you can still provide a written walk-through of your visual content to break down key topics. [Translating your transcript](https://idearocketanimation.com/16934-guide-translating-video-content/) into different languages can help broaden your reach for international audiences.

Plus, transcription creates more interactive experiences by allowing you to link out to product pages or other content that provides more in-depth information. This will help increase time-on-site and help make it easier for people to get the information they need.

Like transcription, [adding captions](https://idearocketanimation.com/15527-video-captions-guide//) may not sound like a distribution tactic, but it can help improve your video’s reach. Captions can help make video more accessible to viewers who choose to watch without sound. Including captions in different languages allows you to reach international audiences as well.

## Ways to Repurpose Video Content

Maximize video ROI with a smart distribution strategy. You may already have used one or two of these techniques, but you haven’t fully distributed your video until you’ve tried all of them.

### 1\. Screen Video at Events

Bring your business video into the real world. Traveling to [a trade show](https://idearocketanimation.com/12142-trade-show-video-best-practices/)? Consider playing your explainer video on a loop at your booth.. Members of your sales team can also bring the video on a laptop or tablet to show at sales meetings. A strong explainer video can provide an overview of your product, and make it easier for your sales team to get right to tackling the more difficult follow-up questions or concerns.

### 2\. Engage Through Email

Include snippets of [video within your email campaigns](https://idearocketanimation.com/18062-video-email-marketing/) to provide specific tips, showcase products, or advertise events to customers. Brands in SaaS, real estate, healthcare, and the nonprofit industry may find special [value in Vmail](https://idearocketanimation.com/27221-what-is-vmail-marketing/).

Once you’ve captured your viewer’s attention through email, draw them to your website where they can learn more.

### 3\. Create A Social Media Campaign

Social media channels have become major video distribution platforms. Users spend an [average of 23.7 hours per month](https://www.socialmediatoday.com/news/new-research-looks-at-the-rising-online-video-trend-infographic/626914/) on YouTube and 19.6 hours on TikTok.

The optimal video length is [less than one minute](https://idearocketanimation.com/17591-best-video-length//). But that’s just an average. On social media, shorter is better. Consider how you might break your video into two or even three [short social media videos](https://idearocketanimation.com/27928-cut-down-short-social-media-videos/). When you slice your video into bite-size chunks you immediately get more options and opportunities for sharing your content.

So post snippets on your Instagram Reels, TikTok, or Facebook accounts. You can also use an entire collection of videos to create a series or playlist on your [YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/c/Idearocketanimation) or [Vimeo channel.](https://vimeo.com/idearocket)

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201200%20734'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

### 4\. Feature Video on Landing Pages

Dynamically engage audiences by incorporating video on your landing pages. [The best landing page videos](https://idearocketanimation.com/17896-5-interesting-landing-page-video/) tell a compelling story and provide value to the viewer. They can also be used to [promote](https://idearocketanimation.com/20755-ultimate-video-promotion-checklist/) your company culture, raise brand awareness, or shed light on specific campaigns.

Your video is a good candidate for landing page placement if it focuses on one thing your viewers want to see, learn, or do. Pair it with a strong CTA and success is almost assured.

### 5\. Place Video on Product Pages

Visitors who land on a product page are likely searching for more specific information, which signals that they may be closer to making a purchasing decision. This is where breaking up your explainer video into smaller sections can be especially powerful.

Tailored video clips are a quick way to give viewers the specific information they’re looking for while keeping the energy high.

### 6.  Educate Customers on A Webinar

Educate and engage with viewers by hosting a webinar and including snippets of your video within the presentation. This can help explain difficult concepts. Plus, it keeps viewers engaged and prevents you from becoming just another [talking head](https://idearocketanimation.com/11902-whiteboard-animation-talking-head-videos/).

As an added bonus, sharing your video on a webinar can help you gather immediate feedback from your audience. Pay attention to the questions they ask after they watch. Their response could help inform your next video project.

### 7\. Repackage Video into a Podcast

It seems like everyone has a podcast these days. Over [90 million Americans](https://www.buzzsprout.com/blog/podcast-statistics) are weekly listeners. And that number is [continuing to rise](https://www.demandsage.com/podcast-statistics/#:~:text=There%20are%20383.7%20million%20podcast,has%20the%20most%20podcast%20listeners.).

You might think starting a podcast is an overwhelming goal. Do you really have time to create more content? If you already have video, you’re more than half-way to a great podcast. Just split the audio off and, viola, instant podcast episode.

Or follow the newest trend and [start a video podcast](https://idearocketanimation.com/22275-start-video-podcast/). Then you’ll have tons of video content to distribute and repurpose.

### 8\. Grab Images and Imagine GIFs

A video is basically a series of images, so you can grab the most interesting stills to use in blog posts, website content, and as thumbnails. You might even print them on promotional material like flyers, [printed t-shirts](https://www.printful.com/t-shirt-printing), or brochures.

You can also skim through your videos in search of graphs, charts or relevant stats. Then repurpose these as infographics, social media images, or illustrations for blog posts.

Finally, pick a few frames to [turn into a gif](https://idearocketanimation.com/20809-gifs-promote-video/) that can be used on social media, in email, or anywhere else you want to grab viewer attention. The video below will show you how to make a gif using Adobe Photoshop.

![](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/omdfcGYEqPY/hqdefault.jpg)

# 2026 L&D Trends: How AI Is Reshaping Learning and Elevating the Role of L&D

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5b588875bbd7c1b993ace041/68f002cdcc7f72fff53407a0_RDgMI1PDcSwqi8sY-dAKPj1J9DwNgdnHI1tcPndO5kg.jpeg)

Shannon Howard

December 18, 2025

![L&D-4.png illustration in Black for 2026 L&D Trends: How AI Is Reshaping Learning and Elevating the Role of L&D](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5b588875bbd7c1b993ace041/688cb933db0b03b9dbd18edb_-3qJENK7dmWRK7KMXoufUQlPVx2i0Jx6AzrH8CDvZAg.jpeg)

With AI rapidly transforming L&D, how can learning leaders prepare for the next year?

In a [recent panel discussion](https://www.intellum.com/resources/webinar/2026-learning-and-development-trends), Intellum brought together experts from Talented Learning, EPAM, and Google to discuss L&D trends to watch as we head toward 2026.

Here are the key trends every L&D leader should be paying attention to:

## Trend \#1: The Shift from Destination Learning to Learning Ecosystem

One of the biggest changes shaping the future of L&D is the move away from standalone systems toward fully connected learning ecosystems.

For years, learning teams have worked within silos with separate tools for content creation, content management, learning delivery, and performance support. AI changes that equation. To work effectively, AI relies on clean, connected, enterprise-wide data. That means learning platforms must integrate with content management systems, data warehouses, HR systems, and even product and customer support tools.

When these systems work together, learning organizations can finally achieve goals they’ve talked about for decades: delivering the right content to the right person at the right time. With approaches like single-source authoring, updates can be made once and published everywhere—keeping learning accurate, current, and scalable across the organization.

## Trend \#2: AI as a Content Creation Multiplier

If there’s one area where AI is already delivering clear, measurable impact, it’s content creation.

Tasks that once took weeks—drafting outlines, writing learning objectives, producing videos, or localizing content—can now be done in hours or even minutes with AI assistance. Learning teams are using AI to summarize complex documents, generate structured course frameworks, and create professional-quality videos without studios, crews, or lengthy post-production cycles.

But the biggest win isn’t just speed. It’s what L&D teams can do with the time they get back.

Instead of operating as reactive content factories, learning professionals can focus on higher-value work: [aligning programs to business outcomes](https://intellum.com/resources/blog/9-examples-of-skills-gaps-in-the-workplace-and-how-to-close-them), partnering with stakeholders, and measuring real performance impact.

## Trend \#3: Hyper-Personalized Learner Experiences at Scale

Personalization has long been a goal in L&D, and we’ve made incremental improvements over the years, but AI is what finally makes it achievable at scale.

Modern AI-driven learning experiences can adapt based on role, preferences, performance data, peer behavior, and real-time context. Instead of static learning paths manually configured by administrators or recommended next steps based on predetermined rules, learners receive customized recommendations that evolve as they do.

AI agents and copilots are also becoming part of the learning experience itself. Learners can ask questions, explore topics more deeply, and receive guidance directly within courses or during their daily work. After a course is complete, AI can identify [skill gaps](https://intellum.com/resources/blog/9-examples-of-skills-gaps-in-the-workplace-and-how-to-close-them) and deliver targeted microlearning to reinforce understanding and improve performance.

The result is learning that feels less like a one-time event and more like an ongoing, personalized journey.

## Trend \#4: Learning in the Flow of Work Becomes Reality

# The Not-So-Ultimate Guide to Creating a Global Content Strategy

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/62c1fa6dc46ace67f7f60440/6421c29a3db6e1400971e6f4_1663099876997.jpg)

Cierra Loflin

July 25, 2022

How do you create a content strategy to reflect your company's global presence? It's a big question, but ultimately the [content strategy](https://superpath.co/blog/ama-with-ellen-schwartze-and-david-brown-from-knotch) should sharply reflect the company's overall go-to-market (GTM) strategy. This will determine the choices you make when serving new regions.

Full disclaimer—this is not the ultimate guide to creating a global content strategy. Instead, this is a skeleton to help you get started. We can’t possibly cover all the nuance that goes into ‘making the sausage,’ but here’s a blueprint to get you started.

Before creating a global strategy, talk to your CMO or CEO to get aligned on the new market, target audience, their pain points, and your budget. Since GCS can get complex, having a firm grasp on your business strategy will keep you grounded. [Thinking like the CMO](https://superpath.co/blog/episode-14-ryan-bonnici-on-leading-a-70-person-marketing-team) will help any content team understand content’s place in the broader organization.

So although we could write a book on global strategy, we’ve condensed it down to a basic framework, along with a few choices you can make based on your resources.

## Is your product distinctly different or universal?

A global business is one where people all around the world use your product. Think Apple, Adidas, or Adobe. Whether your business is global or going global soon, one of the first things to do is determine if your product is:

- **Distinctly different in each region like Eventbrite.** Each market has different events, compliance considerations, and requires a local presence and authority.
- **Fairly universal across the board like Asana.** The product features and updates remain the same whether in France or Japan—the main thing that changes is the language.

![regional vs universal products](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/62c1fa6dc46ace67f7f60440/6309e29ac93c97873a9e469b_regional%2Bv%2Buniversal%2B%25281%2529.png)

A company with a universal product can translate and localize its content for the different regions it serves. Other companies whose products and delivery change in each country will need a truly local approach. Put another way, are you creating a global strategy? Or developing many local strategies that will be united under a global umbrella?

### Products that are different in each region = more complex approach

Since Eventbrite runs local events and processes payments locally, each region has a different business strategy. [Ronnie Higgins](https://www.linkedin.com/in/ronniehiggins/), the former Head of Global Content Strategy at Eventbrite, explains that ticketing, for example, is very different in Brazil since everything is paid for in cash.

Getting people to buy tickets online is a different business model than in the United States, where people are used to purchasing with credit cards.

This complex business strategy requires regional legal, sales, and marketing teams, in addition to different content for each local version of the site. At Eventbrite, local teams on the ground create unique content for their region, but collaborate with the United States team to create a centralized global operation.

### Products that don’t change in each region = more simple strategy

If your product is universal, like Asana, a global content strategy could mean trans-localizing your main pieces of content and publishing them on another version of your site. For example, Asana serves customers in 190 countries but the product doesn’t change. For example, when they [released a product feature called ‘Workload’ across countries](https://blog.asana.com/2019/06/managing-content-programs-asana/#close), they promoted it with the same blog announcement in six languages.

The [original blog](https://blog.asana.com/2019/06/new-workload-resource-management/#close) is in English and a copy has been created in Spanish, French, German, Portuguese, and Japanese. Everything is the same but translated, from the subject matter expert quotes to the animated graphics.

There’s still plenty of nuance in organizing a global content strategy at Asana, but we can reason that it takes less manpower than an operation like Eventbrite’s.

## Do you understand your new target markets?

Once the leaders at your company choose the new market, it’s up to the content team to understand the new region. Whether the new customers are in Japan or Colombia, the team should ensure they’re respectful and tactful when translating and localizing.

A simple way to avoid this is by using professional translators, not machine translators, and considering the customer experience in the new market.

For example, as told in “ [Going Global: Multi-Market Expansion Lessons from Airbnb](https://www.getblend.com/blog/global-multi-market-expansion-lessons-from-airbnb/),” Airbnb expanded by first creating a “universal product without location-specific elements in the design or code.” Then, they localized this “location-neutral” product for each market. Like Asana, Airbnb has made a carbon copy of their site that looks exactly the same for any region, whether in the United States, Philippines, or Germany.

The content has been translated and localized by a “localization vendor, a QA vendor, and in-house localization experts.” But it’s not just translation—they also considered how people make purchases in each market, the local currency, and which social media platforms are most popular. This helped AirBnB create a great customer experience in each region.

As you're growing out into different markets, there are a lot of complexities. David Bell, SEO Consultant and Co-founder of [Previsible.io](https://previsible.io/), says, “Go slow and pick your battles–I'd start in one country and just slowly roll it out.”

## What size is your budget?

Next, check your budget. Do you have the resources to build a team in those regions? Or will you have to manage everything from the original flagship location? Going global can get expensive super fast with all the technicalities involved–it’s best to start lean, find mentors along the way, and dip your toes in the water. You’re bound to make mistakes in your first market, which you can learn from before moving to the next.

If you’re working with a small budget, your team can start by building out a top-level domain (TLD), for example, [https://www.eventbrite.ch/](https://www.eventbrite.ch/). Then create a localized and translated version of the blog and website, plus a system for publishing translated content to the TLD.

For example, Altitude Sports, a Canadian Outdoor Apparel Company, has just two markets and versions of their website in [English](https://altitude-blog.com/en/) and [French](https://altitude-blog.com/fr/). When freelancing for them, we used a project management software to organize this small-scale global organization in the US and Canada. They have recurring approval stages like “Brief created→ Assigned to writer → Editor approved → Translation & localization → Upload to CMS” for every piece of content.

Like Altitude, your company can start simple but implement strong, sustainable processes.

### Translate and localize

# The B2B Expansion Marketing Playbook for Rapid, Hyper-Localized Market Entry

- ![Picture of Lea Amiri](https://directiveconsulting.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/T0BTHTL6S-U08E3B6FEDN-795f2ed42698-512-150x150.png)

Expanding into a new market used to feel like trying to start a fire with wet wood — slow, expensive, and full of guesswork. You’d spend months translating your website, tweaking your ads, and praying your global message kind of worked in a region you barely understood. By the time you realized it didn’t? The budget was gone, the timeline had slipped, and the board was asking why pipeline still looked cold.

But 2025 changed the game.

The companies winning global expansion marketing today aren’t scaling a generic international go-to-market playbook across borders. They’re building regional experiences that feel native on day one. They’re not trying to convince buyers they belong — they’re showing up already speaking the language (literally and figuratively). And when you enter a market with that level of cultural fluency, everything changes. Buyers trust you faster. They understand you sooner. And your funnel warms before your SDRs ever make the first touch.

This is the shift toward hyper-localized expansion marketing, a form of B2B localization that ensures your messaging, proof, channels, and compliance all match the local market’s reality. When that happens, market entry stops feeling like a risk. It starts feeling like market fit.

This guide is built for operators who need to enter markets quickly but can’t afford the waste that comes from guesswork. By combining regional psychology, compliant data capture, country-specific messaging, and a 30/60/90 execution model, you’ll learn how to turn hyper-localization into a predictable revenue engine,  one that lowers CAC, improves SQL quality, and accelerates deal velocity across every market you enter.

## How Hyper-Localization Drives Faster Pipeline (and Makes Expansion Marketing Work)

Most companies don’t fail at market expansion strategy because their product is wrong. They fail because their assumptions are wrong. Teams take the same global messaging, the same proof points, the same creative — and expect it to land in markets that think, speak, buy, and trust completely differently. It’s the fastest way to burn budget, waste time, and stall pipeline.

Because here’s the truth: Buyers don’t evaluate solutions globally. They evaluate them locally.

They lean on the brands they already know. They trust language that feels native. They respond to channels they use every day. And they interpret value through cultural signals that global GTM teams often overlook.

[CSA Research’s 2025 study](https://csa-research.com/Blogs-Events/CSA-in-the-Media/Press-Releases/businesses-prefer-local-language-purchasing) makes it undeniable: over 90% of businesses prefer buying from brands that adapt to their language and market norms. That’s not a “nice to have.” That’s a buying requirement.

This is why hyper-localization works. The moment a prospect sees a message written the way _they_ talk, a case study featuring a company down the street, pricing in their currency, and a CTA that connects through their preferred communication platform—the entire experience snaps into place. It stops feeling foreign. It starts feeling familiar. And familiarity is the ultimate accelerator of trust.

Hyper-localization isn’t about adding layers of complexity. It’s about stripping out the friction that slows buyers down. When you remove the invisible blockers, the awkward language, the irrelevant proof, the mismatched channels, you create a funnel that flows naturally. CTR climbs. Landing-page conversion jumps. Demo acceptance increases. Sales velocity speeds up.

It’s why modern regional demand generation teams treat expansion as an operational discipline, not a hopeful experiment — and why leading companies lean on principles similar to those in our [revenue acceleration playbook](https://directiveconsulting.com/blog/revenue-acceleration-playbook-how-to-scale-hidden-growth-engines-fast/).

### Refactor Your Messaging for Local Buyer Psychology

The first real test of any international expansion services motion has nothing to do with channels or budgets. It starts with your value proposition, and whether it actually makes sense to the people you’re trying to reach.

Most teams begin by translating. The smartest teams begin by transcreation.

Literal translation fails because it ignores how different markets interpret value, risk, urgency, and differentiation. You can’t simply convert English copy and expect buyers to internalize it. You need local market research B2B buyers would recognize as reflective of their world.

**The DACH region—Germany, Austria, and Switzerland** is defined by a commercial culture that prioritizes quality, certification, compliance, and operational excellence. Stability matters. Credibility matters. Messaging that emphasizes proven performance and risk reduction wins here far more often than “disruptive” or innovation-first narratives.

**Japan** values predictability, reputation, and collective decision-making. Buyers want solutions that feel reliable, established, and safe to champion internally. Messaging that aligns to group consensus and long-term consistency consistently outperforms bolder, experimental positioning.

**LATAM—Latin America, including markets like Mexico, Brazil, Colombia, Chile, and Argentina** operates with a completely different rhythm. Buyers prioritize responsiveness, communication accessibility, and flexibility. WhatsApp is a primary business channel. Payments often require local methods. Messaging that reinforces speed, support, and seamless communication earns trust early.

These aren’t subtle differences. These are core buying behaviors—and your messaging either resonates with them or works against them.

When messaging is transcreated by native or near-native experts, it aligns with local cultural norms, industry vocabulary, idioms, and expectations. That alignment is what improves performance, not the translation itself. It’s why localized campaigns often show a 20–40% lift in CTR within the first 60 days compared to global baselines.

This step isn’t about creativity, it’s about precision. The question is never “How do we say our message in German?” It’s “How do German buyers _talk about_ this pain point, this opportunity, and this solution?” When messaging reflects how buyers already think, it accelerates trust from the start.

For deeper cultural frameworks, explore our [hyper-local marketing](https://directiveconsulting.com/blog/regional-business-growth-with-cultural-customization/).

### Localize proof and offers (case studies, guarantees, payment)

Messaging earns attention, but proof earns belief. And in new regions, global proof rarely carries weight. A big US logo may mean nothing to a buyer in the UK, Germany, or Singapore.

This is where B2B localization becomes revenue-critical.

Localizing proof means adapting:

- Case studies with regionally relevant logos
- KPIs in local units or currencies
- Guarantees + offers that match regional expectations
- Payment methods (e.g., PIX, boleto) where required

When LATAM buyers see local payment options or European buyers see € pricing, confidence rises immediately. Teams often see 25%+ LP-to-SQL improvements once localized proof enters the funnel.

# How to Translate Subtitles: Complete Guide to Video Subtitle Translation

[Twitter](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=How%20to%20Translate%20Subtitles:%20Complete%20Guide%20to%20Video%20Subtitle%20Translation&url=https://trtc.io/blog/details/how-to-translate-subtitles?uid=bbb319b6-eda4-4808-b2f5-e905cd53482b)[Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://trtc.io/blog/details/how-to-translate-subtitles?uid=bbb319b6-eda4-4808-b2f5-e905cd53482b)[LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https://trtc.io/blog/details/how-to-translate-subtitles?uid=bbb319b6-eda4-4808-b2f5-e905cd53482b)

10 min read

Apr 2, 2026

![poster_24a4ad516252db35600f4ed36f5f9543.png](https://trtc.io/media/0f640c15-2a4d-4f84-a314-b2b9f8cfc53d.png)

Every minute, over 500 hours of video are uploaded to YouTube alone. With audiences spanning 100+ countries, the demand for multilingual video content has never been higher. Whether you're a video creator expanding to new markets, a localization team managing enterprise content, or a subtitle team working on film and TV projects, knowing how to **translate subtitles** accurately and efficiently is a critical skill.

But subtitle translation isn't as simple as running text through Google Translate. Timing, context, cultural nuance, character limits, and format compatibility all play a role. A poorly translated subtitle can confuse viewers, damage your brand, or even change the meaning of your content entirely.

This guide walks you through everything you need to know — from understanding subtitle file formats to choosing the right **subtitle translator**, implementing AI-powered workflows, and delivering broadcast-quality translated subtitles at scale.

## Understanding Subtitle File Formats

Before you can **translate subtitles**, you need to understand the file formats you're working with. Each format has unique characteristics that affect your translation workflow.

#### SRT (SubRip Text) — The Universal Standard

SRT is the most widely used subtitle format across platforms. It's a plain-text file with a simple structure:

```plaintext
1
00:00:01,000 --> 00:00:04,500
Welcome to today's presentation
on global marketing strategies.

2
00:00:05,000 --> 00:00:08,200
We'll cover three key topics
that every team should know.
```

**Why it matters for translation:** SRT files are easy to edit in any text editor. However, translators must be careful not to alter the timestamp lines. Translated text often differs in length from the source — German text, for example, is typically 30% longer than English — so you may need to adjust timing or split lines after translation.

#### VTT (WebVTT) — The Web Standard

WebVTT is the native subtitle format for HTML5 video and is required by many web-based players:

```plaintext
WEBVTT

00:00:01.000 --> 00:00:04.500
Welcome to today's presentation
on global marketing strategies.

00:00:05.000 --> 00:00:08.200
We'll cover three key topics
that every team should know.
```

**Key differences from SRT:** VTT uses periods instead of commas for milliseconds, supports CSS styling, and includes a `WEBVTT` header. Many **subtitle translation** tools can convert between SRT and VTT seamlessly.

#### ASS/SSA (Advanced SubStation Alpha)

ASS files support rich formatting — fonts, colors, positioning, animations, and karaoke effects. They're popular in anime fansubbing and professional video production:

```plaintext
[V4+ Styles]
Style: Default,Arial,20,&H00FFFFFF,...

[Events]
Dialogue: 0,0:00:01.00,0:00:04.50,Default,,0,0,0,,Welcome to today's presentation.
```

**Translation consideration:** ASS files contain complex metadata. When translating, preserve the style definitions and only modify the dialogue text. Some languages may require different fonts to display characters correctly (e.g., CJK characters need compatible fonts).

#### SUB/IDX (VobSub) and Other Formats

SUB/IDX files are bitmap-based subtitle formats used in DVDs. Unlike text-based formats, they contain images of the subtitle text, which means they cannot be directly translated — you must first use OCR (optical character recognition) to extract the text before translating.

Other formats you may encounter include:

| Format | Use Case | Text-Based? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| SRT | Universal / most platforms | ✅ Yes |
| VTT | Web / HTML5 video | ✅ Yes |
| ASS/SSA | Anime / styled subtitles | ✅ Yes |
| SBV | YouTube uploads | ✅ Yes |
| TTML/DFXP | Broadcast / streaming | ✅ Yes |
| SUB/IDX | DVD | ❌ Bitmap |
| PGS (.sup) | Blu-ray | ❌ Bitmap |

**Pro tip:** Always work with text-based formats when possible. If you receive bitmap subtitles, convert them to SRT first using tools like Subtitle Edit or SubRip before starting your **subtitle translation** workflow.

## Step-by-Step Subtitle Translation Workflow

Whether you're translating subtitles for a single YouTube video or managing a 50-language localization project, the workflow follows the same core steps.

#### Step 1: Extract or Obtain the Source Subtitle File

If you already have an SRT or VTT file, you're ready to go. If not, you'll need to generate subtitles first:

- **From video/audio:** Use automatic speech recognition (ASR) to transcribe the spoken content and generate timed subtitles.
- **From hardcoded subtitles:** Use OCR tools to extract burned-in text (this is less accurate and should be a last resort).
- **From platform exports:** Download existing subtitles from YouTube (via YouTube Studio), Vimeo, or other platforms.

For teams looking for a streamlined solution, [**Tencent RTC's video transcription software**](https://trtc.io/video-transcription-software) provides real-time speech-to-text capabilities that can automatically generate accurate, timed subtitle files from any video or live stream — supporting 50+ languages out of the box.

#### Step 2: Prepare the File for Translation

Before translating, clean up the source file:

- **Fix timing errors:** Ensure subtitles appear and disappear at natural speech boundaries.
- **Correct transcription mistakes:** Fix any ASR errors in the source text.
- **Split long lines:** Keep each subtitle to a maximum of two lines and roughly 42 characters per line (broadcast standard).
- **Add context notes:** For professional translators, provide a glossary and context document explaining brand terms, character names, and tone.

#### Step 3: Translate the Subtitle Text

This is where you choose your translation method:

1. **Fully manual translation:** A human translator works through each subtitle entry, translating while considering timing constraints.
2. **AI auto-translation with human review:** Use an AI subtitle translator to generate a first draft, then have a human linguist review and refine the output.
3. **Fully automated AI translation:** For high-volume, lower-stakes content (e.g., internal training videos), AI-only translation may be acceptable.

We'll compare these methods in detail in the sections below.

#### Step 4: Review and Quality Assurance

After translation, perform these quality checks:

- **Reading speed check:** Ensure viewers have enough time to read each subtitle (standard: 15–20 characters per second).
- **Line length check:** Verify no line exceeds the character limit for the target language.
- **Timing sync:** Watch the translated subtitles against the video to ensure they match the visual and audio cues.
- **Linguistic review:** Check for grammar, spelling, cultural appropriateness, and consistency.

#### Step 5: Export and Embed

Save the translated subtitles in the required format and either:

- Upload as a separate subtitle track (soft subtitles)
- Burn them into the video (hard subtitles)

From ultra-fast conversational AI to studio-quality narration, compare 12 text-to-speech APIs — including ElevenLabs, Google Cloud, Amazon Polly and Speechmatics — to find the voice that matches your use case and budget.

![Text to speech providers-header](https://www.speechmatics.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fyze1aysi0225%2F648V1IXGjYSfJgEDRhT0TP%2F3c98b1594a987a16dc4c6ec17fb39738%2FTT-preview-1200x900_1_5x.webp&w=3840&q=75)

[![Tom Young](https://www.speechmatics.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fyze1aysi0225%2F4N6l1JRA9OmEc7iwEMoOIn%2Fd8d25b36d580ba3bb901c1e8fc301335%2FTom_Young.jfif&w=256&q=75)](https://www.speechmatics.com/author/tom-young)

[Tom Young](https://www.speechmatics.com/author/tom-young)Digital Specialist

The text-to-speech market is currently very loud and very human.

What was once the domain of robotic, stilted voices has transformed into [a competitive landscape](https://artificialanalysis.ai/text-to-speech/models) of neural networks producing uncannily human speech.

Having just released our own natural, low-latency [text-to-speech API](https://www.speechmatics.com/text-to-speech) (at a fraction of the cost of established vendors like ElevenLabs, Amazon Polly and Google Cloud TTS), we thought it worth sharing our findings on what's out there.

From our research and work, one thing is very clear; each vendor has unique benefits and differentiators, and blindly following the biggest names could mean you're paying for features you'll never use or missing capabilities you desperately need.

Below, we examine twelve of the best TTS APIs in 2026, from ultra-fast conversational models to emotionally intelligent narrators, to help you match your use case, resources, and priorities to the right voice for you.

## What is a Text-to-Speech API?

A Text-to-Speech (TTS) API is a cloud-based or on-premises service that converts written text into spoken audio.

Rather than recording human voices for every possible utterance, modern TTS systems use deep learning models: typically neural networks trained on vast datasets of human speech, to synthesize natural-sounding voices in real time.

Developers integrate these APIs into applications via HTTP requests, receiving audio files or streaming audio in return. The technology underpins everything from accessibility tools for visually impaired users to voice assistants, interactive voice response (IVR) systems, and increasingly, [Voice AI agents](https://www.speechmatics.com/use-cases/ai-voice-agents) that respond instantly and naturally.

The best TTS APIs offer more than just basic speech synthesis. They provide multiple voices across dozens of languages, fine-grained control over pronunciation and prosody through [Speech Synthesis Markup Language](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speech_Synthesis_Markup_Language) (SSML), [real-time streaming](https://www.speechmatics.com/product/real-time) for low-latency applications, and in some cases, the ability to clone or customize voices entirely.

The devil, as ever, is in the details, and the pricing.

## Comparative overview: TTS APIs at a glance

To help you navigate the noisy TTS landscape, here's a rapid comparison of twelve leading services, organized by their core strengths and ideal use cases:

## The contenders: Who's who in TTS

# Level Up Your Video Marketing Strategy: The Ultimate Guide

By:

Ashley Levesque

Share:

[![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61967dbb50eec57a4e7fde97/61ce03b9a0d65d0a0d7cd1ef_blog-share-fb%402x.png)](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=&t= "Share on Facebook")[![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61967dbb50eec57a4e7fde97/65b3c8f1de9a98341cbf103a_x%20logo%20(1).png)](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet? "Tweet")[![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61967dbb50eec57a4e7fde97/61ce03b93bb75406388555fa_blog-share-li%402x.png)](http://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=&title=&summary=&source= "Share on LinkedIn")

Video marketing isn't just flashy YouTube ads or viral TikTok sensations. It’s also not only about top-of-the-funnel content designed to build brand awareness.

**Done right, video is an incredibly versatile medium that can transform how you educate, engage, and convert your audience at every stage of their buying journey.** In fact, according to recent research, 96% of B2B organizations say video positively impacts their performance.\*

That might mean creating viral videos for social media, grabbing a prospect's attention with a dynamic demo reel on your homepage, or using personalized video messages in email nurturing campaigns. But it might also mean interactive self-guided product explorations, webinars with thought leaders in your industry, Q&A recordings, and video case studies.

The applications are endless.

However, to actually leverage the potential of video across the customer journey, you need more than creative ideas. You need a well-designed, flexible, and effective video marketing strategy.

This complete guide will give you a proven blueprint for video marketing that gets results. We’ll walk you through all the key steps to building — or improving — your video strategy, so you can boost thought leadership, pipeline, conversions, retention, and more.

[![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61a529b176c3293ae1c9a93d/660c63ea22b3eb5bb784efde_CTA_HA%20Demo%20(4).png)](https://www.banzai.io/downloads/buying-guide-for-webinar-platforms)

## How Video Marketing Can Boost Your ROI

Let's face it: marketing is tougher than ever. Between budget constraints, limited resources, and all the competition, captivating audiences feels nearly impossible some days.

A strategic video marketing approach can help you get heard through the noise, connect with customers in moments that matter, and drive lead generation and conversions.

### What Is Video Marketing?

Video marketing refers to any video content created and distributed to promote a product, service, brand, or organization. This encompasses a wide variety of video types and use cases, from social media posts and ads to testimonial interviews, product explainer reels, onboarding tutorials, webinars, virtual events, and more.

What’s most important is that all of these video types are designed to achieve marketing objectives, whether that’s building awareness, generating pipeline, educating users, or retaining customers.

### Benefits of Video Marketing

The proof is in the numbers when it comes to the immense potential of video marketing.

According to the 2023 Gartner Tech Marketing Benchmarks Survey, video is the top asset type included in 2023 marketing plans as well as the third best performing asset for generating marketing qualified leads (MQLs) for technology marketers’ organizations with between $10 million and $50 million in annual revenue.

Key [plus points of video marketing](https://www.banzai.io/blog/video-marketing-benefits) include:

- Increased brand awareness and affinity
- Improved lead generation and pipeline
- Conversions — Wyzowl research shows that including video on landing pages can increase conversion rates by over 80%
- Higher user engagement and retention
- Improved SEO rankings and site traffic
- Greater thought leadership and trust
- Increased sharing and engagement through social media
- Opportunity to build emotional connections

With a strong strategy in place, you can bring all the right elements together for video marketing magic.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61a529b176c3293ae1c9a93d/660c678fd184ccd33b82a92e_SchittsCreekSometimesClicksCompressed.gif)

## 8 Steps to Build An Effective Video Marketing Strategy

By following our tried-and-tested steps, you can create a stellar strategy for videos that engage your audience and accelerate pipeline at every stage.

While these steps are outlined sequentially, video marketing requires constant testing and optimization over time. Expect to iterate continuously based on performance data and audience feedback.

Not every aspect will apply to every business or use case either. This framework helps ensure you cover all the key elements for video marketing success — but you should adapt the steps based on your unique goals, target segments, and resources.

### 1\. Define Your Goals and Audience

The first step in developing an impactful video marketing strategy is getting crystal clear on your goals and target audience. This focus will inform every subsequent decision from video types to distribution channels.

Start by outlining your overall objectives. Do you want to increase brand awareness among a new buyer persona? Generate more demo requests? Reduce customer churn? Drive event registrations?

Then, map your video strategy directly to those KPIs. For demand generation, you may want to focus on product explainer and demo videos, while if you want to reduce churn, you could prioritize educational video tutorials that support customers post-purchase.

Next, drill down on your target audience, buyer personas, and customer segments. Make sure you understand their demographics, values, personality, pain points, role in the buying process, and preferred engagement with content.

Getting this level of insight will let you tailor your video positioning, messaging, length, visuals, and distribution channels so they resonate across the whole customer journey.

### 2\. Decide On Your Video Types

Different types of video content tap into the needs and emotions prospects experience at different moments.

For instance, leads who are at the beginning of their journey, and feeling frustrated with current solutions not meeting their needs, may respond best to short videos that center on introducing your brand and solving their problem.

Key [video marketing examples](https://www.banzai.io/blog/video-marketing-examples) at this stage include:

- Short how-to videos optimized for social media platforms
- Explainer videos
- 30-second video ads
- Results-focused homepage or landing page videos

As they move towards curiosity, optimism, and confidence, you may want to focus on:

- Longer product demo videos
- Q&A sessions that address any doubts
- Webinars
- Customer testimonials
- Case studies that show _exactly_ how your solution helps
- Videos giving extra insights into your brand and culture behind the scenes

By tailoring your video strategy to emotional drivers, you can influence every touchpoint on the path to conversion.

Once customers have converted, you’ll want to make sure your video strategy includes content that drives retention and turns them into true partners.

# 10 Expert Tips for Repurposing Video Content

Share on social media

[![Linkedin logo](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/63b9c43cf803b0007440fa38/6592bbf7d0c4400af2662643_linkedIn.png)](https://www.castmagic.io/post/repurposing-video-content#)[![X(Twitter) icon](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/63b9c43cf803b0007440fa38/6848715d4aa6383691614b3e_icons8-x-logo-60.svg)](https://www.castmagic.io/post/repurposing-video-content#)

What if we told you that repurposed content delivered better engagement, leads, and conversions compared to creating entirely new content?

We hope that made every content creator who stumbled upon this blog post sit up and take notice!

Look, repurposing video content has become one of the most effective strategies for maximizing your return on investment while reaching diverse audiences across multiple platforms.

The truth is [video content](https://www.castmagic.io/post/video-for-content) reigns supreme, but creating fresh videos for every platform and campaign can be exhausting and expensive.

That's where the art of repurposing comes in. When we master the skill of transforming our existing video assets into multiple formats, we unlock incredible potential for extending our reach, boosting engagement, and saving precious time and resources.

Below, we're sharing 10 expert strategies that will revolutionize how you approach repurposing video content. From creating shorter clips for social media to developing entire podcast episodes from a single longer video, these techniques will help you squeeze every ounce of value from your video investments.

## Develop a Strategic Framework for Repurposing Video Content

Before we dive into the tactical aspects of repurposing, we need to establish a solid foundation. Creating a strategic framework isn't just about randomly chopping up videos. It's about developing a systematic approach that aligns with our content goals and audience needs.

The first step in our framework involves mapping content goals to platform requirements. We must identify the primary objectives for each piece of video content and understand how different platforms serve different purposes in our marketing funnel.

For instance, a comprehensive longer video might serve as lead generation content on our website, while shorter clips from the same content could drive brand awareness on [Instagram Reels](https://www.castmagic.io/post/how-to-make-an-instagram-reel) or YouTube.

Establishing a workflow to repurpose content efficiently is equally crucial. We need to design a systematic process that includes timelines, responsibilities, and quality checkpoints.

This means creating templates for consistent branding and developing standard operating procedures that our team can follow.

Castmagic's AI-powered platform can help automate much of this workflow, from [automatic transcription](https://www.castmagic.io/post/ai-transcription-revolutionizing-the-future-of-audio-content-conversion) to content generation, making it easier to reuse your existing video content across multiple formats.

## Extract Maximum Value by Creating Shorter Clips from Long-Form Content

One of the most effective ways to repurpose video content is by transforming lengthy videos into shorter clips that pack a punch. The beauty of this lies in its simplicity and immediate impact on engagement rates across platforms like [YouTube, Instagram Reels, and TikTok](https://www.castmagic.io/post/tiktok-vs-reels-vs-shorts).

We should focus on identifying high-impact moments within our longer video content. These are the quotable segments, key insights, and emotional peaks that can stand alone as compelling clips. Using engagement metrics from our original videos helps us pinpoint where viewers showed the most interest or where retention spiked dramatically.

The goal is to extract 30-60 second clips that capture the essence of our message while being perfectly shareable across social platforms.

Advanced transcription tools can help identify these golden moments by analyzing the content and highlighting key quotes, timestamps, and engaging segments automatically. When you transcribe your longer video content, these platforms can suggest the best segments for repurposing into shorter clips.

Educational content presents particularly rich opportunities for this type of repurposing.

We can break complex tutorials into step-by-step short clips, create "quick tip" series from comprehensive guides, and develop FAQ-style video content that addresses common questions our audience asks. These repurposed clips work exceptionally well for Instagram Reels and YouTube videos.

## Optimize Your Video Content for YouTube Shorts and Vertical Formats

The rise of YouTube Shorts has fundamentally changed how we think about video content. With the platform's algorithm favoring short-form vertical content, mastering this format has become essential for maximizing our reach when we repurpose longer video content.

Transforming horizontal [YouTube videos](https://www.castmagic.io/post/types-of-youtube-videos) for vertical viewing requires more than simply cropping our content. We need to reframe our clips thoughtfully, using dynamic text overlays and graphics to maintain engagement in the mobile-first viewing experience that defines YouTube Shorts and Instagram Reels.

The key to success with YouTube Shorts lies in understanding the algorithm's preferences. Content should be structured for optimal lengths between 15-60 seconds, implement trending hashtags and sounds strategically, and create looping clips that encourage rewatches.

[AI-powered clip generation tools](https://www.castmagic.io/tools/youtube-clip-generator) can analyze your longer video and automatically suggest the best segments for YouTube Shorts, complete with optimal formatting and engaging elements.
