# 5 ways video can support your sales enablement strategy

**October 31, 2019**

Michael Monette

![5 ways video can support your sales enablement strategy image](https://www.epiphan.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/10/Sales-enablement-strategy_Main.jpg)

Sales enablement is about giving your salespeople the knowledge and tools they need to sell more effectively. That makes video a vital addition to any sales enablement strategy. Used to its full potential, video can help your salespeople get up to speed more quickly and engage buyers at every stage of the sales cycle.

Like so many corporate buzzwords, [“sales enablement”](https://blog.topohq.com/sales-enablement-who-what-how-when-why/) is a murky term. [Gartner](https://www.gartner.com/en/information-technology/glossary/sales-enablement) defines it as, “The activities, systems, processes and information that support and promote knowledge-based sales interactions with clients and prospects.” In other words, sales enablement is anything a business does or puts in place to help its sellers better connect with buyers and close sales.

Sales are the lifeblood of any business, so it’s well worth the time it takes to hammer out an effective sales enablement strategy. [Unpreparedness accounts for a third of all lost sales](https://www.panopto.com/blog/what-is-sales-enablement/), according to global market intelligence firm IDC. At the same time, IDC estimates that [salespeople spend seven hours each week](https://www.enablix.com/sales-enablement-industry-growth/) searching for content in preparation for sales opportunities.

## Make video a pillar of your sales enablement strategy

Where does video fit in? Here are five powerful ways video can help your sales team sell:

1. [Better salesperson onboarding](https://www.epiphan.com/blog/sales-enablement-strategy/#onboarding)
2. [More accessible training](https://www.epiphan.com/blog/sales-enablement-strategy/#training)
3. [Easier remote collaboration](https://www.epiphan.com/blog/sales-enablement-strategy/#collaboration)
4. [Enhanced prospecting and engagement](https://www.epiphan.com/blog/sales-enablement-strategy/#engagement)
5. [More engaging sales calls and remote demos](https://www.epiphan.com/blog/sales-enablement-strategy/#calls)

### 1\. Better salesperson onboarding

A new hire means another person to chase leads, nurture customer and client relationships, and run sales demos – but not straight away. It could be months before a fresh employee is familiar enough with your business to sell effectively.

A lot of employers count on senior staff to orient recruits to the company culture, sales tactics, and products and services. But tying up seasoned sellers this way can actually _hurt_ sales capacity for a time. Businesses also often send new employees to offsite classrooms to get the knowledge they need. This can be expensive for the company and not always effective for learners, who may suffer from information overload.

It’s in the best interest of any business to ensure onboarding is as undisruptive and cost-effective as possible. With a library of standardized, high-quality [onboarding and training videos](https://www.epiphan.com/blog/six-steps-create-effective-training-videos), new sales personnel can learn the ins and outs of your products and services without drawing on long-standing staff. This is especially valuable if your company has multiple offices or sellers who work remotely.

Video learning empowers new staff to learn at their own pace. Plus, newbies and veterans alike can rewatch onboarding content at any time to reinforce their knowledge. With the average employee forgetting [35 percent of their training](https://apttus.com/resources/7-ways-to-increase-sales-productivity-with-sales-enablement/) within a month – and up to 90 percent after six – the value of this is clear.

### 2\. More accessible training

Video training isn’t just for new hires, of course. Video is a great way to build your entire sales team’s knowledge and skills and to share around the latest information about your products and services. It may even be employees’ preferred way to learn. According to one [Forrester Research poll](https://www.forrester.com/report/Enterprise+Videos+Future+Aligns+With+Customer+Obsession/-/E-RES99802#figure3), 75 percent of employees would rather tune into a training video than read a document, email, or online article with the same information.

But is video learning effective? Absolutely. In fact, video is an ideal match for two proven concepts in professional learning and development: [just-in-time learning](https://myelearningworld.com/what-is-just-in-time-learning/) and [microlearning](https://insights.learnlight.com/en/articles/microlearning-examples-innovative-new-way-deliver-training/). These approaches enable employees to grow their knowledge at their own pace. What results are learning experiences far more conducive to comprehension and retention. It certainly beats packing everyone into a classroom for a day or two of wall-to-wall training.

Video on-demand (VOD) content can also make it easier for employees to locate information. With a video content management system like [Kaltura](https://corp.kaltura.com/solutions/video-for-business/sales/) or [Panopto](https://www.panopto.com/panopto-for-business/sales-enablement/), an organization can index transcripts and on-screen text to make their video content library highly searchable. Say a salesperson needs to clarify a product feature. All they have to do is enter a fitting keyword or phrase to bring up a list of videos that include it, then select one to skip ahead to their answer.

### 3\. Easier remote collaboration

When it comes down to it, [sales is a team sport](https://www.salesforce.com/blog/2018/03/collaborative-selling-better-customer-relationships.html). Making sure your sellers have opportunities and tools to collaborate is an important part of any sales enablement strategy. It can be tricky to keep the lines of communication open when your sales team is distributed or includes salespeople who are mobile. Phone conferencing and instant messaging are always options, but video is the next best thing to having everyone together in the same room.

A  [video conferencing platform](https://www.epiphan.com/blog/best-video-conferencing-software/) can give your salespeople an efficient and reliable line to anyone else in the company. Many offer mobile versions that let users make video calls or join video conferencing sessions from anywhere. Features like file transfer, screen share, and virtual white boards can also boost productivity.

Enterprises can use video to promote collaboration in other ways. For instance, most video content management systems let employees across an organization shoot and upload instructional videos or announcements for on-demand viewing. This makes it easy to share around updates, best practices, and other important information.

### 4\. Enhanced prospecting and engagement

No one ever said cold outreach has to be cold. Video can add warmth to communications with potential customers or clients. When sales sends an email, chances are it’ll land in an inbox full of similar messages from other companies also vying for the account owner’s attention. Add a personalized video to your pitch and it’s a lot more likely to stand out. Case in point: embedding a video in an email can [boost click rates by 300 percent](https://www.martechadvisor.com/articles/marketing-automation-2/personalization-and-optimization-will-be-the-focus-for-email-marketers-in-2017/).

The same principle applies when it comes to engaging inbound leads and building customer relationships after an initial sale. Sellers can record and attach personalized videos to messages whenever they follow up with a customer or client. This simple touch could help influence a renewal, upsell, or cross-sell.

# How to make a good explainer video

Est. reading time: 8 minutes

Published on: December 13, 2018

LanguageWire

![Man sitting in cafe wearing headphones using his laptop](https://www.languagewire.com/media/eqznbg3t/man-using-headphones-for-virtual-shopping-support.webp?width=1136&height=460&format=webp&quality=80&v=1dbe5e41adda880)

## How to make a good explainer video

We're all looking for attention. And brands are the world's biggest attention seekers. Unfortunately, attention is a zero-sum game. We live in a time of information overload. Modern consumers are bombarded with marketing messages during every waking minute. So, how do win the race for attention?

## Not all content is equal

It's not enough to create content that is valuable and interesting, you also have to consider how you deliver it to your target audience. Some forms of communication are more effective than others. It's important to offer content that's easy to digest.

Creating short videos can be one of the best ways to boost your online presence.

In the battle for eyeballs, videos stand tall above text, still images and audio content. In fact, people spend [2.6x more time on pages](https://wistia.com/learn/marketing/video-time-on-page "wistia.com") with video than without. Trying to sell a product? Well, your potential customers can be [convinced to buy a product or service](https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/state-of-video-marketing-new-data "blog.hubspot.com") by watching a video.

## What is an explainer video?

Explainer videos are short marketing videos that explain a product or service. They are often animated, but this is by no means the rule. For them to work, they should be compelling, concise and clear. And this is where it gets tricky, **how to make a good explainer video?**

## A good process

Making video content can be daunting. You may find it easier to get an agency to do the heavy lifting and guide you through the process. But even with recent drops in production costs, agency prices can be exorbitant for something that isn't all that hard to make. So here are some tips on how to create an engaging video for your brand without breaking the bank.

### 1\. Research

This is where you lay the foundation for everything to come. If you do your research right, you will find every step along the way a lot easier. First, make sure you understand your product or service. Use the information in your product brief (such as USPs, target audience and competitive landscape) to know what you need to create.

Secondly, dig into what problems your target audience faces. If you understand their pains correctly, you’ll be able to create a video that is valuable to them by showing them the solutions to one or more of the problems they are facing. This is a great time to make use of the buyer personas you have stashed in a drawer somewhere.

Finally, use all of this information you’ve collected to create your explainer video brief. This will be the reference point for your internal team as well as an external assist you need along the way.

### 2\. Allocate resources

You have your brief together now, so what’s next to get this video made?

You’ll need a scriptwriter, a project manager, a graphic designer, a video production specialist, an editor, models, a voiceover artist,

A ‘good job’ nowadays is no longer only a high-paying one. Going by employer branding statistics, employees look for flexibility, purpose, and inclusiveness to commit to their jobs.

Here’s what numbers say about the importance of global employer branding:

- According to the Universum Global Report, 2021, employer branding is a priority for [60% of all companies](https://www.myshortlister.com/insights/employer-branding-statistics).
- [82% of people](https://www.careerarc.com/blog/future-of-recruiting-study/) consider a company’s reputation and branding before applying for a job.
- A [strong employer brand](https://www.clinchtalent.com/eu/) reduces employee turnover by 28% and costs by 50% per hire.

International employer branding talks about the company’s personality. It works as the core of how to attract the world’s best talents, hire, and retain them.

It communicates your visions, values, and messages to your target audience to make them collaborate with you over your competitors.

To know more about International employer branding, stay with us!

## What are the Global Specifics of Employer Branding?

Employer branding involves certain factors. In other words, a few key factors influence employer branding. These are;

- Job security
- Compensation
- Employee benefits
- Positive work ethics and good work culture
- Pleasant working atmosphere
- Work-life balance
- Career advancement opportunities

For positive International employer branding, you must consider all of these factors.

Besides taking care of the above-mentioned factors, there are certain international employer brand specifics that you must maintain.

### Think globally, act locally

This slogan fits employer branding perfectly, and it is indeed one of the best practices of global employer branding.

Going by this strategy, companies need to create a unified brand. However, when you hire employees globally, you may need to adapt to certain changes in your branding.

For instance, you may need to change your language or images to adjust the local sensitivities and preferences. For example, if you create a video in one language, you may want to add voiceovers in different languages. Another scenario is that you may promote your company’s diverse and inclusive culture in countries where it is a more sensitive subject.

### Incorporate your company culture into branding

Maintaining a high [employee retention](https://www.usemultiplier.com/blog/employee-retention-strategies) rate is the first step toward building your global brand. A great way to do that is by building a comfortable organizational culture.

. Your company culture can be your employees’ conduct – the way they dress and behave, their work ethics and values.

Bring your company culture to the spotlight in all your communications. Be it your social media channels, emails, website, or internal memos, ensure the promotion of your culture.

This will help make your company culture a part of everything you do. Thus, you can incorporate it easily into your global employer branding strategy.

### Single recruitment strategy

Though your local recruitment managers will do the actual hiring, ensure that you have a centralized recruitment strategy. The hiring needs or vacant job positions will differ by location. Ensure that all your candidates experience the same process at every location.

Further, you can also put a standardized feedback method in place to gather the applicants’ feedback on the recruitment process. Moreover, use a standard communication method to interact with candidates.

However, if there is a specific local communication preference, you can adapt to that to ensure the implementation of the best practice.

### Leverage your current top performers

Several global companies use their existing high-performers as an advantage to fill entry-level job roles.

Many of these roles have low pay, long work hours, low-quality work conditions, etc. These roles and responsibilities usually lead to dissatisfaction among the employees who then share negative experiences and grudges to the world outside. As a result, these hamper the employer’s brand image.

Although you cannot prevent these negative reviews, you can use your top performers to leave positive comments, reviews, and testimonials. Employers can request the existing employees through emails, weekly newsletters and other forums to leave positive comments on various review portals.

Also, try to improve the quality of entry-level jobs to the highest possible extent.

## Ensuring Employee Onboarding and Retention through Global Employer Branding

International employer branding plays a huge role in marketing and recruitment. If companies cannot attract, engage, and retain talents, they are unlikely to achieve their business goals.

Hence, the best players in global business are trying their best to attract and retain employees. Here are four main areas they focus on:

### Target the right talent

Attracting the right talent is important. Hence, your hiring managers must create talent profiles defining the skills, qualifications, and qualities for different job positions.

Along with qualities and qualifications, hiring managers must also mention a few leading attractions that can drive the right applicants. For instance, if you want to hire engineers, you can emphasize innovation as an opportunity. Similarly, for marketing or finance executives, you can mention leadership opportunities.

Also, for every job position, you can mention a few attractive career progression opportunities applicants would get if hired.

Companies can also market their global employer branding to attract the right talent. For example, L’Oreal mentions its brand characteristics on the global careers website homepage to attract the ideal candidates.

Similarly, you can leverage your International employer branding to attract the right talent for the right position.

### Try to stand-out

To stand out from the crowd, you need to find out the response – _What makes your company culture and employment opportunities different from your competitors?_

Most companies are striving to be ‘good’ employers. Hence, you must become better than the ‘good’ or simply _great to attract superstar performers_.

Creating an Employee Value Proposition is one way to communicate what makes you stand out in the crowd. This value proposition lists the qualities that make you great and special to your employees.

One of the best examples of global employer branding when it comes to being different from the rest is Apple Inc. Apple is one of the leading destinations for tech talents. Former Apple employees market themselves by mentioning ex-Apple employees in their portfolios.

But why? What makes employees feel so great working with Apple Inc.?

A major part of the brand’s success is its branding. Their employee value proposition emphasizes being different. Apple’s ‘Think Differently’ campaign calls for the _misfits_, _rebels_, and _troublemakers_ who see things differently. The brand talks about how it considers the ‘ _crazy ones’_ as genius.

Apple’s branding talks about its search for innovators. Also, the brand highlights its quality of treating its employees as

The Language Technology Landscape in 2026: An Enterprise Buyer’s Guide

What is the lowest-risk path to scaling multilingual access across your organisation without blowing up security, budget, or user experience?

This question is asked by thousands of leaders every day. The first thing to consider is that global communication has never been more complex. Enterprises and institutions now operate across markets and time zones, manage remote teams and virtual events, and engage audiences who speak dozens of different languages.

With this global reach comes a growing responsibility: to ensure that every stakeholder can understand, contribute, and feel included. For decades, this responsibility was handled by human interpreters working on site with hardware booths and headsets. Today, artificial intelligence can translate speech in real time and generate captions at a fraction of the cost, while hybrid platforms offer the flexibility to combine both approaches.

But choice brings confusion. Leaders, teams, and event organisers must select the right solution for their needs while balancing accuracy, scale, security, and cost. To help you navigate this decision, we have gathered up-to-date information on the language solutions available in 2026, including human, AI, and hybrid systems, leading providers, and the key factors to consider when going multilingual.

# Is Your Marketing Localization Strategy Ready for What’s Next? Trends for Global Teams

Global marketing is entering a new era. Explore these trends for global teams and how to prepare for the future.

March 23, 2026

5 Minutes

- [Blog Post](https://www.weloglobal.com/resources/category/blog-post/)

![](https://www.weloglobal.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/11/2026/03/2026-Trends-for-Global-Teams_Blog-Header_Option-1-scaled-1-1024x312.webp)

Global marketing is entering a new era. Organizations are now judged not only by the speed and quality of their content, but by their ability to deliver meaningful engagement across every language, channel, and market.

For global teams, this demands a strategic, scalable localization approach that aligns technology, talent, and operational excellence.

## Why Global Marketing is Evolving

As global markets expand and digital channels multiply, marketing teams must deliver more campaigns, in more formats, to more audiences than ever before. Traditional workflows struggle with this, prompting organizations to rethink how they create and deliver multilingual content.

Localization is also becoming a [clear competitive advantage](https://www.welocalize.com/insights/the-importance-of-localization-in-multilingual-content-marketing-2/). Audiences expect messaging shaped by cultural insight, making local relevance essential for engagement in growth markets.

Meanwhile, [the buyer journey is no longer linear](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2019-11-13-gartner-says-the-traditional-linear-sales-process-no-). Customers move across platforms and markets unpredictably, requiring consistent, culturally credible experiences at every touchpoint.

To meet these expectations, organizations are shifting from region-specific processes to centralized localization models that support consistency, efficiency, and speed. This operational evolution is defining the future of global marketing teams.

## Trends Reshaping Global Marketing

Several trends are setting the trajectory for global marketing localization. Together, they signal a need for stronger operations, deeper cultural alignment, and more advanced use of AI.

### Content Velocity Overwhelm Legacy Workflows

Marketing teams must support more formats, markets, and channels than ever. Manual or regionalized processes simply can’t handle the growing demands of global content production.

### Customers Expect Consistent Multilingual Experiences

Quality can no longer vary by geography. Users expect the same clarity, tone, performance, and brand integrity across all languages and digital environments.

### Multilingual SEO Drives Global Discoverability

As search behavior diversifies across markets, localized keywords, metadata, URL structures, and schema become essential for [ensuring content can be found and understood](https://www.welocalize.com/insights/maximizing-your-websites-global-reach-with-multilingual-seo-welocalize/) by regional audiences.

### Regulation Elevates Expectations for Global Content

Legislations such as the [EU Accessibili](https://www.welocalize.com/insights/the-european-accessibility-act-building-a-more-inclusive-digital-future/) [t](https://www.welocalize.com/insights/the-european-accessibility-act-building-a-more-inclusive-digital-future/) [y Act](https://www.welocalize.com/insights/the-european-accessibility-act-building-a-more-inclusive-digital-future/) and EU AI Act is pushing organizations to adopt transparent AI usage, accessible digital experiences, and secure multilingual data practices. Compliance is becoming a strategic advantage.

### Inclusive and Accessible Content Shapes Engagement

Localization now requires [content that works for everyone](https://www.welocalize.com/insights/multilingual-accessibility-in-a-post-eaa-world/)—screen-reader compatibility, culturally relevant visuals, multilingual captions, and accessible experiences across languages and formats.

### Language Gaps Create New Growth Opportunities

Underserved languages or [low-resource languages, such as Arabic](https://welodata.ai/2025/09/03/bridging-the-arabic-ai-gap/), highlight uneven AI performance. Investing in linguistic diversity helps brands expand market reach and improve multilingual model accuracy.

### Specialized Localization Accelerates

Demand is growing for advanced services in regulated and technical sectors. This requires transcreation, domain expertise, and precision-driven workflows to meet strict industry standards.

### Video and Audio Localization Becomes Essential

With global video consumption accelerating, [localized multimedia](https://www.welocalize.com/multimedia/) is now a core expectation. Brands are expanding their use of subtitles, captions, and market-specific dubbing to meet audience preferences.

## The Role of AI

AI is redefining how global marketing teams deliver multilingual content. Its role is shifting from automation to intelligence—helping teams manage complexity while maintaining quality and cultural relevance.

Modern AI systems now go beyond surface-level translation. [New reasoning and agentic models](https://www.welocalize.com/insights/building-trustworthy-agentic-ai/) can interpret context, sentiment, idioms, and domain-specific terminology, supporting brand voice and consistency before human review. These capabilities rely on diverse, high-quality, [ethically sourced data](https://welodata.ai/2025/09/25/ethical-ai-fair-work/) to ensure accuracy and fairness across languages.

To support this shift, enterprises are consolidating their technology stack. Integrated platforms that combine AI, workflow automation, quality management, and human expertise are replacing fragmented, region-specific tools. This strengthens governance, accelerates delivery, and aligns global teams under shared processes.

As AI becomes more embedded in multilingual workflows, trust and accountability are essential. Transparent data practices, ethical sourcing, and rigorous annotation standards [ensure AI systems remain reliable, unbiased, and culturally aware](https://welodata.ai/2024/10/15/building-trust-in-ai-key-strategies-for-ensuring-ai-data-integrity-and-quality/) as they scale.

### How Adapt Studio Powered by Opal Transforms Global Marketing

Organizations need a platform that can orchestrate automation, maintain quality, and adapt to diverse content demands. Through [Adapt Studio](https://www.welocalize.com/platform/adapt-studio/), a marketing localization platform powered by [Welocalize’s Opal](https://www.welocalize.com/platform/), global marketing teams can launch and manage multilingual campaigns with the speed and structure modern marketing demands.

[Adapt Studio](https://www.adaptworldwide.com/adapt-studio/) is built by marketers for marketers, combining generative AI, performance-focused workflows, and in-market experts to deliver translation that drives real marketing results.

- **Configurable workflow** s tailored to creative, technical, and regulated content
- **Experience apps** that allow creators to request translations from their native tools
- **Workflow apps** governing translation, review, and approvals
- **Pre-built connectors** for seamless integration with existing enterprise systems
- **Modular AI components** powered by leading foundational models
- **A digital foundation** of data pipelines, rules engines, and AI-enabled agents
- **A global expert network** ensuring accuracy, nuance, and compliance

## Preparing Your Team

Preparing for the future requires building the operational discipline to deliver localized content reliably and at scale. Teams need clarity in how work moves, confidence in core systems, and structures that enable AI to enhance—not disrupt—the way they operate.

Use these foundational steps to build readiness:

- Move away from manual

# How to Localize eLearning Videos: A Comprehensive Guide

![Image showing article author](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f1840665928895a221fb731/6627a167feab2200a6f14776_Milos.jpeg)

Milos Milosevic

May 9, 2025

![Understanding the importance of video localisation to boost E-learning](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f1840665928895a221fb731/66279e6360c6f6fcad78f1e1_6.png)

## Summary

This guide provides you with a comprehensive overview of the e-learning video localization process. It outlines the significance of e-learning video localization and the key elements to consider when embarking on the project. Moreover, we provide you with the necessary tools to ensure a successful e-learning video localization project.

[READ IT](https://www.vidjet.com/blog/understanding-the-importance-of-video-localisation-to-boost-e-learning#)

Are you eager to extend the impact of your eLearning content to a global audience? Localizing your eLearning videos can be a powerful strategy, unlocking new markets and revenue streams. However, the process of eLearning localization extends far beyond mere translation.

It involves comprehensively adapting your content to align with diverse cultural, linguistic, and educational preferences across different regions.

In this detailed guide, we will explore the intricate world of eLearning video localization, emphasizing its significance, the step-by-step process involved, and the transformative impact it can have on the learning experience for students worldwide.

‍

## **Understanding eLearning Video Localization**

At its core, eLearning video localization represents a strategic [marketing](https://www.vidjet.com/blog/powerful-ecommerce-on-site-marketing-strategy-to-boost-sales-for-your-brand) initiative that tailors educational video content to resonate with specific geographical locations' cultural, linguistic, and educational nuances.

Imagine delivering your eLearning video series to students in different countries. They may speak varied languages, adhere to distinct measurement systems, have diverse cultural references, and possess unique educational backgrounds.

For instance, while learners in the United States might be familiar with the Imperial measurement system, which utilizes units like feet and inches, counterparts in Europe often rely on the metric system, which measures distances in meters and centimeters.

Localizing eLearning videos involves translating spoken content and adapting visual elements, cultural references, and instructional methodologies to connect with your target audience deeply.

![Image highlighting the cultural importance of localisation.](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f1840665928895a221fb731/66279cdd95ab5301c6713388_T4CK0Wg4GX3XVqLAR4IMfWyinfGD4g4zc8ywwo5WCaEChBq3wZoOJJGCRmhBzKg-IBdS9zNuftY2mKE2K_ze04yCCBOHyvwdB8Vh5C3q6I3kR630q3xbBbmEaIf9r7k3gIxYJBk98pz3.png)

Image highlighting the cultural importance of localisation.

‍

## **The Significance of eLearning Video Localization**

Why is eLearning video localization important in today's globalized educational landscape? Let's delve into some key reasons that underscore its significance:

### **1\. Enhancing Relevance and Engagement**

Localizing your eLearning videos ensures the content becomes inherently relevant to students by incorporating familiar concepts, language nuances, and cultural references. This heightened relevance fosters deeper engagement and forges a stronger emotional connection between learners and the presented material.

### **2\. Facilitating Comprehension and Retention**

Localization significantly improves comprehension and retention rates. When presented in their native language and cultural context, learners can more effectively grasp complex concepts. This leads to a more profound understanding of the subject matter and enhances the learning experience.

### **3\. Promoting Inclusivity and Accessibility**

By offering localized video content, you make your eLearning courses accessible to a diverse range of learners worldwide, thereby promoting inclusivity and [marketing for education](https://www.inbeat.co/articles/education-marketing-agencies/) that highlights the value of diversity in learning.

## **Key Elements to Localize in Your eLearning Videos**

When embarking on the eLearning localization journey for your videos, several critical elements warrant careful consideration and adaptation:

**Spoken Content: Translating and Localizing Voiceovers**

The spoken content in your eLearning videos, including voiceovers, narration, and dialogue, should be accurately translated and localized into the target language(s). This involves linguistic translation and cultural adaptation to ensure that the delivery resonates authentically with the target audience.

**Visual Elements: Adapting Graphics and Visuals**

Visual elements such as on-screen graphics, text overlays, animations, and illustrations convey information to the target audience. These visual components must be adapted to suit the cultural preferences, sensitivities, and aesthetics of the target audience.

**Cultural Context: Incorporating Local Examples and Contextual References**

Integrating local examples, case studies, and contextual references into your eLearning videos enhances relatability and engagement for learners in specific regions. This cultural contextualization of the course material ensures the content remains relevant and impactful across diverse cultural norms and settings.

**Subtitles and Closed Captions: Ensuring Accessibility and Clarity**

Providing accurate and localized subtitles or closed captions in the target language(s) is essential for learners with hearing impairments or those who prefer subtitles. This ensures accessibility and clarity of content, accommodating a wider audience and enhancing learning outcomes. It is good to know that today [closed captions can be done by humans or AI-generated](https://dayinterpreting.com/blog/investigating-the-accuracy-of-ai-generated-captions-a-comparative-analysis-with-human-captioning-services/).

![Image showing localised reels content.](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f1840665928895a221fb731/66279cdd247d9f65f945e5f8_Kt6UhHEWf9dyJf7SXosbEsTjRraUp36lyJp9cnA4Rqp9pbun4ENuLZqPvxePfpz7t15EJqxnZ5rpBcLlqVpGCs7o_nRSAASlrH0hb2GdG5YguBvXRQ7vmufSsgANUmMKwF-_0zAdSs1C.png)

Image showing localised reels content.

**Interactive Elements: Adapting Interactivity and Assessments**

Suppose your e-learning course features interactive elements such as quizzes, assessments, or interactive simulations. These elements should be localized to align with the target audience's language, cultural nuances, educational standards, and cultural expectations. This localization of interactivity enhances learner engagement and comprehension.

‍

## **Understanding the eLearning Localization Process**

Navigating the intricate process of [eLearning localization](https://www.daytranslations.com/localization-services/elearning-localization-services/) requires a systematic approach and attention to detail. Here's a step-by-step guide to effectively localize your eLearning video content:

**1\. Comprehensive Audience Research**

Begin by conducting thorough research to understand your target audience's cultural, linguistic, and educational preferences. Consider language dialects, cultural sensitivities, preferred learning styles, and technological infrastructure.

**2\. Translation and Cultural Adaptation**

Utilize professional translation services or localization tools to translate and culturally adapt your e-learning course videos' spoken content, visual elements, subtitles, and interactive components. Ensure linguistic accuracy, cultural relevance, and contextual appropriateness in all translations.

**3\. Visual Localization

# Engage all families with translated video captions

![translated video caption press release blog header](https://talkingpts.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/Blog_VideoCaptions-766x262.png)

Written by [Paige DeLozier](https://talkingpts.org/sme-paige-delozier/)

We are so excited to announce the availability of our latest feature making TalkingPoints more accessible than ever before.

TalkingPoints translated video captioning makes it super easy for teachers to record and send video messages in over 100 languages. This new feature was designed specifically to further our mission of connecting educators and families–particularly those that are hardest to reach–and fosters a  more effective home-school connection to fuel student success. Read the full release below.

![Full logo tp small](https://talkingpts.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/TP-Full-Logo-Small-300x42.png)

**ED-TECH NONPROFIT, TALKINGPOINTS LAUNCHES MULTILINGUAL TRANSLATED**

**VIDEO CAPTIONS SUPPORTED IN MORE THAN 100 LANGUAGES**

_New feature expands options to foster better, more personalized, more effective_

_school-parent communication and collaboration to fuel student success_

February 24, 2021, San Francisco, California — Today, education technology nonprofit

[TalkingPoints](http://35.90.209.29/) announced that it has launched a new video translation feature designed to facilitate more effective communication between teachers and families–in more than 100 different languages — delivered to mobile devices via text message. TalkingPoints translated video caption feature was specifically designed to further the organization’s mission to connect educators and families–particularly those in under-resourced and multilingual communities–in support of student success.

TalkingPoints translated video captioning makes it easy for teachers to record and send

messages and for parents to view and respond. Teachers simply record a video message in the TalkingPoints app and send. Families can then view and read along in their own home languages, making TalkingPoints more accessible than ever before.

“TalkingPoints mission is to facilitate stronger connections between teachers and parents to make a positive impact on student success. We know that video messaging is an increasingly popular and effective way for teachers to share information with

parents–particularly those that may be the hardest to reach– and helps them build more meaningful connections, so it was natural to add translatable video captioning to

TalkingPoints,” said [Heejae Lim](https://talkingpts.org/ceo-heejae-lim/), Founder and CEO of TalkingPoints.

The school-home connection is more important than ever and TalkingPoints helps fuel

those relationships by removing language barriers and:

- **Powering consistent engagement with families:** With TalkingPoints, teachers


don’t have to wait for school or district translators to communicate with parents.
- **Enabling teachers to meet families where they are:** One in four students in U.S.


public schools speak a language other than English at home, and many families are most comfortable communicating via text. TalkingPoints makes it easier for both teachers and family members to connect quickly and effectively across language and technology barriers
- **Encouraging collaboration and community:** Research shows that family


engagement plays a critical role in student success, yet many families — particularly those in under-resourced areas — may be at a disadvantage due to language barriers, internet access and time constraints. TalkingPoints removes those barriers so families can engage in a manner that works best for them.

TalkingPoints is free to all teachers and families. Visit [www.talkingpts.org](http://35.90.209.29/) for more information.

**About TalkingPoints**

Founded to help teachers connect with families–particularly those of under-resourced and non-English speaking backgrounds, TalkingPoints multilingual platform uses human and AI-powered, two-way translated communication to deliver information and personalized content in more than 100 languages–via web and mobile apps and through text messages. The platform also delivers scaffolded, guided content and eliminates language barriers, fostering strong collaboration to improve students’ academic success. TalkingPoints currently serves more than 3 million teachers and families across the country.

There is an old saying that an image is worth a thousand words. Moreover, medical science confirms that the human brain is wired to retain visual information better. Therefore, videos have some distinct benefits in spreading the message.

Videos have gained even more significance with the advancements in technology. Unlike several decades ago, videos are now easily shareable, surpassing text-based content in popularity and engagement. Any video material is [1200% more likely to be shared](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/social-video-generates-1200-more-shares-than-text-image-moroles/) than a text article.

Furthermore, [video translation](https://ulatus.com/audio-video-translation/) has played a crucial role in expanding the reach of various industries, and the medical field is no exception. Along with [medical translation](https://www.ulatus.com/medical-translation-service/) and localization, video translation has revolutionized patient care and research, providing enhanced healthcare services to patients who are geographically distant. Through a video call, effective communication between patients and doctors can be achieved.

In this article, we will delve into why video translation services are essential for the healthcare industry

## Impact of Language Barriers on Health Outcomes

- **Inability to make informed decisions**: Language barriers can have a detrimental effect on health outcomes as effective communication is crucial in healthcare. Without understanding health information, individuals may struggle to make informed decisions about their well-being, leading to potential gaps in care and compromised health outcomes.
- **Lack of healthcare resources**: In certain nations or specific ethnic groups, poor health outcomes can be attributed to the lack of health information available in the language spoken by those communities. This language barrier creates a significant obstacle, hindering access to vital healthcare resources and preventing individuals from fully understanding and managing their health conditions.
- **Poor communication:** Language barriers pose challenges not only in developing nations but also in countries like the United States, where a significant portion of the population prefers languages other than English. For example, a considerable number of individuals in the US communicate in Spanish. This underscores the importance of providing accurate and accessible healthcare information in multiple languages to meet diverse linguistic needs and [facilitate effective healthcare delivery](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7201401/).

Be it text or video, any communication is of little use if not clearly understood by the audience. One of the issues related to poor health outcomes in certain nations or specific ethnic groups is the lack of health information in the language that people understand.

This problem is not only widespread in developing nations. Just take the example of the US, which is better known as an English-speaking country. However, a majority of the population prefers to communicate in Spanish.

# Best AI Translation Tools: Choosing the Right Platform for Global Communication

We've shifted into an era where leveraging AI for translation is essential for global business communication.

![LILT Team](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/w2y5ha8x/production/6ab0c35dffe21923329d72bc28b3598a314f1092-40x40.svg)

LILT Team

![Best AI Translation Tools: Choosing the Right Platform for Global Communication](https://lilt.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fcdn.sanity.io%2Fimages%2Fw2y5ha8x%2Fproduction%2F6a3604c78c66bf406e1c7db88f6cea73061c4b5a-4256x2837.jpg&w=828&q=75)

It wasn't long ago that artificial intelligence was a novelty in the world of language services. But technology has changed. We've shifted into an era where leveraging AI for translation is essential for global business communication. As organizations expand across borders, the sheer volume of content demands solutions that move much faster than traditional workflows.

Speed alone is no longer the benchmark, however. Businesses today need a mix of rapid output and enterprise-grade translation accuracy. It isn't enough to simply generate words. The output must be contextually precise and culturally relevant.

This guide compares the top tools available today and explains what to look for when evaluating them. Whether you are a [government agency](https://lilt.com/blog/government-efficiency) dealing with sensitive intelligence or a corporation launching a new product, understanding the nuances of these platforms is the first step toward building a successful localization strategy.

## What to Look for in Platforms Using AI for Translation

Selecting the right AI-powered translation technology is about more than just comparing feature lists. You need a solution that aligns with your specific operational goals. Here are the critical factors to consider during your evaluation:

**Accuracy and model adaptability:** The most effective tools don't just translate. They learn. Look for platforms that offer adaptive modeling where the system improves over time based on specific domain data and feedback.

**Integration capabilities:** Your AI translation tool should not exist in a silo. It needs to connect seamlessly with your existing content management systems, translation management systems, and customer support platforms to automate content flow.

**Human-in-the-loop workflows:** While AI handles the heavy lifting, human expertise ensures nuance. The best solutions incorporate workflows where human linguists verify and fine-tune AI outputs, which in turn retrains the model for better future performance.

**Security and compliance:** For regulated industries, data privacy is non-negotiable. Ensure the platform provides robust security measures to comply with [regulations like GDPR](https://gdpr.eu/what-is-gdpr/) and safeguard customer information.

**Scalability and pricing transparency**: As your content volume grows, your solution must scale without exponential cost increases. Look for transparent pricing models that accommodate increasing data volumes and user needs.

## How to Choose the Best AI Translation Tools for Your Needs

The "best" tool often depends on your specific constraints regarding scale, content type, and regulatory needs. Use this brief decision guide to orient your search:

- **If you need enterprise-grade security and adaptive quality:** Look for platforms that combine AI efficiency with human verification, such as LILT.
- **If you need raw speed for massive amounts of generic text:** Consider large-scale cloud engines like Google Cloud Translation.
- **If you need fluency for European languages in casual contexts:** Tools like DeepL are often a strong choice for ad-hoc needs.

## Top 8 AI Translation Tools, Ranked

Given the above considerations, we've analyzed the leading players in the AI-powered translation market to help you find the right tools for your business. If you're interested in leveraging the power of AI for translation in 2026 and beyond, here are the best platforms:

### \#1\. LILT

LILT is the leading solution for enterprise localization. It provides a practical approach to managing multilingual intelligence at scale.

Unlike standard machine translation engines, LILT offers a complete platform utilizing AI for translation that integrates adaptive neural models with a [human-in-the-loop](https://lilt.com/products/expert-human-verifiers) workflow. This ensures that the AI learns from professional linguists in real time and constantly improves accuracy for your specific domain.

**Key strengths:** LILT excels in "Contextual AI" where the system understands strategy, purpose, and domain. It significantly accelerates timelines and achieves a 6-8x improvement in throughput compared to traditional methods. Additionally, it supports diverse modalities including text, speech, and video.

**Ideal use case:** LILT is ideal for government agencies and global enterprises that require high-quality, verified translations for mission-critical content. It is particularly strong for organizations dealing with high volumes of data that require strict security and domain adaptation.

**Note on limitations:** As a comprehensive enterprise platform, LILT is designed for organizational use rather than for individuals seeking free or ad-hoc translations for personal text.

### \#2\. DeepL

DeepL is widely recognized for producing translations that sound natural and fluid. It is particularly strong when handling European languages. It uses advanced neural networks to capture nuances that other engines sometimes miss.

**Key strengths:** The primary strength of DeepL is its linguistic fluency. It often produces text that requires less post-editing for general business communication. It also offers a user-friendly interface that is easy for teams to adopt quickly.

**Ideal use case:** DeepL is a great option for businesses needing quick, high-quality translations for emails, internal documents, or customer support tickets where "good enough" fluency is the priority over strict terminology control.

**Note on limitations:** DeepL has a smaller language portfolio compared to giants like Google. Its free version also has limitations regarding data privacy, so enterprises must ensure they opt for the Pro version to secure their data.

### \#3\. Google Cloud Translation

Google Cloud Translation offers massive scale and support for over 100 languages. It leverages Google’s vast data ecosystem to provide robust API access for developers.

**Key strengths:** The sheer breadth of language support and integration into the Google Cloud ecosystem makes it a top choice for developers. It offers " [Adaptive Translation](https://blog.modernmt.com/understanding-adaptive-machine-translation/)" features that allow users to customize models with their own data.

**Ideal use case:** This tool is best for developers building multilingual applications or companies that need to translate enormous volumes of user-generated content, like reviews or comments, where speed and coverage are more critical than perfect nuance.

**Note on limitations:** While powerful, the generic output can lack the specific domain accuracy required for technical or brand-sensitive content without significant customization and training.

### \#4\. Microsoft Azure AI Translator

Part of the Azure Cognitive Services suite, this tool is deeply integrated into the Microsoft ecosystem. This makes it a natural choice for organizations already heavily invested in Microsoft 365.

**Key strengths:** Integration is the key differentiator here. It works seamlessly within Office, Teams, and SharePoint. It also offers strong document translation capabilities that preserve original formatting.

**Ideal use case:** Large enterprises using the Microsoft stack will find it easiest to deploy Azure AI Translator for internal documentation, knowledge bases, and corporate communication.

# How to prepare your SaaS company for global expansion

As SaaS companies grow, it becomes increasingly important to identify expansion opportunities and develop strategies and scalable processes to support them. Some companies move upmarket and start selling to the enterprise where they’d previously sold to SMBs. Some take the opposite approach and move downmarket. Others move into new verticals.

And then there’s international expansion. According to [Paddle](https://info.paddle.com/report-saas-commerce-trends)’s CEO Christian Owens, expanding internationally can boost revenue growth by 13%.

But with many cultural, financial and practical differences to consider, there’s no one correct way to expand internationally. Should you master local and regional markets first, then grow internationally, or go global from day one?

This blog post looks at the most important aspects to consider when planning to expand to foreign markets, pulling in expert advice from SaaS leaders around the world.

## Is your SaaS ready to expand internationally?

The first question to consider is: is your SaaS company ready to expand internationally?

Today, SaaS businesses are built on modern cloud platforms that can be accessed by, and scale to users, all over the world. So it can be tempting to assume that you should be a global business, right from day one.

But while it may be easier than ever before to build a global audience, that doesn’t necessarily mean your business or your product is ready to go global.

According to [Stephen Carpenter writing for TechCrunch in 2015](https://techcrunch.com/2015/12/23/a-startups-guide-to-international-expansion/):

> “When 25 percent or more of your business is coming from international markets, it’s time to scale outside your home country.”

This is a useful benchmark to keep in mind. More recently, at SaaStock EMEA earlier this year, Matthew O’Riordan (CEO & Co-Founder at Ably) shared a global readiness checklist to help you understand if your SaaS is ready to expand internationally. He recommended asking your business these three questions:

1. Are you solving a global problem? Is there a global pull for your solution?
2. Are you a product-led repeatable business in all markets?
3. Do you need feet on the ground or professional services to complete the sales process?

[![](https://www.saastock.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/Screenshot-2020-11-09-at-16.39.12-1-1024x527.png)](https://youtu.be/8RsZm_vanRA)

[Watch his keynote with Jonno Southam (Senior VC Business Development at AWS) here.](https://youtu.be/8RsZm_vanRA)

Of course, there will be other things to consider depending on what region you’re looking to expand into, and where you already have an established presence (your home region). But these three questions are a great starting point to help you understand if your business is ready to expand internationally, or if you still need to lay the foundations in your home region first.

## What are the biggest challenges facing SaaS founders expanding internationally?

Internationalisation is a real growth opportunity for SaaS companies, but it comes at a cost.

At SaaStock EMEA, Paddle CEO Christian Owens delivered a keynote on setting yourself up for sustainable growth in new markets. He shared some of the biggest challenges that SaaS founders face when branching out into new markets:

- Regional cultural differences
- Language differences
- Currency differences
- Accepting international and cross-border payments
- Tax liabilities and regional regulations.

[Watch his keynote from SaaStock EMEA here.](https://saastock.tv/programs/scale-weds-think-glocal-set-yourself-up-for-sustainable-growth-in-new-markets-christian-owensmp4-01ac61?categoryId=60449)

To successfully scale your SaaS company internationally, it’s a balancing act of operational logistics (such as accepting payments) and cultural alignment.

Even if you’re expanding into a region that on the surface seems like it’ll be quite similar to your home region (for example from Western Europe into the US, or from the US into Europe), it’s important not to underestimate the less clear-cut cultural differences.

If you’re expanding into a region that’s less similar to your own (for example, from Asia into North America, or from North America into Latin America) then it’s even more important to take the time to understand these differences, so you can build empathy with new team members, customers and users in the region.

[![New call-to-action](https://www.saastock.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/6fe8aeb7-b033-459a-aaa4-9008793a5990.png)](https://cta-redirect.hubspot.com/cta/redirect/6471807/6fe8aeb7-b033-459a-aaa4-9008793a5990)

## Different approaches to expanding internationally

There are several different routes to market in new regions:

1. **Direct: setting up a new regional HQ.** For example, [Salesforce](https://www.salesforce.com/news/stories/the-history-of-salesforce/) was founded in 1999, and in April 2001 it announced its expansion into the worldwide marketplace with new regional headquarters in Dublin and Tokyo.
2. **Acquisition: acquire a business in the region to become your ‘feet on the ground’.** Workday is a good example here: it [acquired Cape Clear](https://www.workday.com/en-us/company/latest/newsroom/press-releases/press-release-details.html?id=818281?&_rda=/company/news/press_archive/workday_launches_integration_on-demand.php) in 2008 to create its EMEA HQ.
3. **Reseller partnership: joining with a trusted partner to bring your SaaS to market in the new region.** In certain regions (like the Nordics, Latin America and Japan), companies really prefer to have a relationship with local software companies rather than international ones.
4. **Remotely: keep your team in your home region but expand sales and marketing activity to target new regions.** There are many ways to roll this out, but one approach is to hire for different specialisms in different regions. For example, having your customer success team in the USA, or your engineering team in Europe. Having specific business functions in specific geographies helps overcome the challenges associated with localizing salaries, and avoid pay disparity between team members.

Whatever route to market you choose, when it comes to expanding internationally, there are two main approaches that we see companies take:

1. If you’re in a fairly disparate region where markets vary greatly between countries (for example: Asia or Latin America), then companies expand within their own region as the first stage of internationalisation.
2. Alternatively, if your home region is quite cohesive, with less variation within the region (for example: North America), then companies will look further afield and make the leap into new global regions when expanding internationally.

## Advantages of expanding in-region first

There are several practical advantages to expanding within your home region first, before jumping into new global regions:

- **Time zones** – broadly speaking, there’s likely to be less variance in time zones within a region than if you jumped straight to expanding worldwide. If this is your first step in expanding internationally, doing so closer to home may be easier to manage from a logistical, day-to-day working standpoint than making a bigger leap overseas.
- **Cultural alignment** – if you’re in a more cohesive region, geographical proximity may help you build cultural alignment within the new market and with your new international team.
- **Local laws and regulations** – you may already have a good understanding of the local laws and regulations that will affect the day-to-day running of your business, compared with if you were expanding into a completely new region.

# Why multilingual learning is essential for a global workforce

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/689340825669a734519bc571/6978b5e35a843f7dc9ef0489_roberta.webp)

Roberta Bettanin

Global organisations are increasingly diverse.

Teams span continents, languages, and cultures.

From a project manager in Amsterdam to a customer service team in Lisbon, from warehouse staff in Warsaw to engineers in Munich.

It's the reality of modern business, and it's brilliant.

Yet many training programmes remain rooted in English-only or generic approaches that assume everyone learns the same way.

The cost?

Lower engagement.

Weaker knowledge transfer.

And training investments that deliver **half** the impact they should.

For HR and L&D leaders managing multinational workforces, this creates urgent questions:

- Does English-only training truly work everywhere?

- What does effective localisation actually involve?

- How can we design learning that drives real impact across cultures and languages?

This article answers those questions with evidence, practical guidance, and frameworks you can apply today.

## Why language and culture matter in learning

To kick off, let’s dive into why localised learning is crucial.

### Training effectiveness and learner engagement

Research from the [Association for Talent Development (ATD)](https://www.td.org/content/press-release/atd-research-localizing-training-makes-learning-more-effective) agree that localisation improves training effectiveness.

Localised content leads to deeper engagement, faster completion, and better knowledge retention, ultimately enhancing on-the-job performance.

**Put simply: learners engage more strongly when training reflects their language and context.**

### The cognitive reality: learning in a second language

Here's something most L&D leaders already suspect but rarely quantify: learning in a second language fundamentally changes what people can absorb and retain.

Is it even possible to quantify?

The answer’s yes.

In that same report from the ADT, 311 talent development professionals were surveyed, more than 80% reported that localising content enabled learners to retain more information.

When someone processes training content in a non-native language, their working memory is split between two tasks: translating the content _and_ understanding it.

This cognitive load doesn't just slow learning down; it reduces the brain's capacity for deep processing, critical thinking, and long-term retention.

Imagine trying to grasp a complex leadership concept - say, managing conflict with psychological safety - whilst simultaneously translating terminology like "accountability," "boundaries," or "constructive challenge."

The mental effort required for translation crowds out the space needed for genuine learning.

### Beyond words: why cultural context shapes understanding

But language is only half the story.

Cultural context shapes how we interpret concepts, examples, and even learning itself.

As [**Marìa Rosales**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/mariarosalesfernandez/), Product Manager at GoodHabitz, explains:

_“If I'm not understanding, the reference is not going to be relevant for me and therefore I'm going to stop paying attention and probably the learning outcome is going to be worse.”_

Take a seemingly straightforward topic like giving feedback.

In Dutch workplace culture, directness is expected, even valued.

A manager offering unfiltered observations would be seen as honest and helpful. But in a different context, that same directness might feel the opposite.

Or consider accountability.

In some cultures, this centres on individual ownership and transparency.  In others, it's framed through collective responsibility and preserving dignity. Same word, but an entirely different meaning.

Localisation recognises these differences and adjusts accordingly.

## What localisation really means

**Localisation** goes well beyond literal translation.

Because it's not about dumbing down or softening content.

It’s about translating the _meaning_ of a point, not just the words.

For example:

- **Language nuance**: idioms, tone, grammar, clarity that feels natural, not stilted or overly formal.

- **Cultural relevance**: examples, scenarios, and references that learners actually recognise and relate to.

- **Context adaptation**: regulatory specifics, regional norms, workplace realities, and social cues that match the learner's environment.

The bottom line?

**Translation ≠ localisation.**

Translation swaps words. Localisation adapts meaning, context, and cultural resonance. It aligns training with the learner's world, and that alignment drives behaviour change, not just comprehension.

**Practical takeaway for HR & L&D:**

Audit your training programmes to identify where language or cultural context is limiting learner engagement. Start by mapping regions and languages where performance or completion metrics trail the global average. That gap is your localisation opportunity.

## The hidden cost of “English only **”** training

An English-only approach might seem efficient, but it carries hidden costs that undermine your L&D investment, and ultimately lead to:

- **Lower engagement and completion:** non-native speakers expend cognitive effort translating rather than learning.

- **Inconsistent knowledge transfer:** cultural cues, humour, and references can be lost or misunderstood.

- **Exclusion of key groups:** frontline staff or non-office workers may be less fluent in English, widening the development gap.

Here's the uncomfortable truth: english-only training creates a two-tier workforce where development opportunities correlate with language privilege, not potential.

According to [ATD data](https://www.td.org/content/atd-blog/why-localized-e-learning-is-essential-for-global-workforce-success), while 68% of organisations have localised some training, **32% have not yet done so despite recognising the need**, highlighting a major opportunity for improved impact.

# Video SEO: Ranking Your Videos on YouTube and Google Search

By

ALM Corp

The digital landscape has fundamentally transformed. With over 2.7 billion active users on YouTube and video content dominating search engine results pages, mastering video SEO isn’t just an advantage—it’s a necessity for brands and creators who want to stay visible in 2025 and beyond.

Consider this: YouTube is the second-largest search engine in the world, processing over 3 billion searches per month. Meanwhile, Google increasingly prioritizes video content in its search results, with video carousels, featured videos, and key moments dominating prime real estate on the first page. If your content strategy doesn’t include optimized video, you’re essentially invisible to billions of potential viewers.

This comprehensive guide will walk you through every aspect of video SEO, from understanding how algorithms work to implementing advanced optimization techniques that will help your videos rank #1 on both YouTube and Google Search.

## Understanding the Video SEO Ecosystem in 2025

Video SEO has evolved dramatically from simply uploading content and hoping for the best. Today’s video optimization requires a sophisticated understanding of multiple platforms, algorithms, and user behaviors.

### Why Video SEO Matters More Than Ever

The statistics are staggering: videos are 53 times more likely to rank on Google’s first page than traditional text content. Additionally, 88% of video marketers report that video gives them a positive ROI, and websites with video content experience a 157% increase in organic traffic from search engines.

But beyond the numbers, video SEO matters because of how people consume information. Modern searchers prefer visual, engaging content that delivers information quickly and memorably. Whether they’re searching for “how to fix a leaky faucet” or “best project management software,” users increasingly click on video results over text-only listings.

### The Dual-Platform Approach: YouTube and Google

One of the most powerful aspects of video SEO is the synergy between YouTube and Google Search. When you optimize correctly, a single video can rank in multiple locations:

- **YouTube search results** – capturing the 3+ billion monthly searches on the platform
- **Google video carousels** – appearing at the top of traditional search results
- **Google AI Overviews** – becoming source material for AI-generated answers
- **Featured video snippets** – securing premium placement in Google’s answer boxes
- **Traditional organic results** – ranking your video page alongside text content

Brands like HubSpot have mastered this dual-platform strategy, often ranking their videos 3-4 times on a single search results page through different SERP features. This multi-channel visibility exponentially increases click-through rates and brand exposure.

### How YouTube’s Algorithm Works

YouTube’s recommendation system is powered by sophisticated machine learning that analyzes hundreds of signals to determine which videos to promote. Understanding these core ranking factors is essential for optimization success.

**Primary Ranking Signals:**

1. **Watch Time** – The total minutes viewers spend watching your videos. YouTube’s algorithm heavily weights this metric because longer watch times indicate valuable content that keeps users on the platform.

2. **Audience Retention** – The percentage of your video that viewers actually watch. A video with 70% average retention will outrank one with 30% retention, even if the lower-retention video has more total views.

3. **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** – The percentage of impressions that result in clicks. YouTube compares your CTR against similar videos to determine if your thumbnail and title are compelling.

4. **Engagement Signals** – Likes, comments, shares, and subscribes all indicate that viewers found your content valuable enough to interact with it.

5. **Session Time** – How long viewers stay on YouTube after watching your video. Videos that lead to longer viewing sessions get promoted more heavily.

6. **Video Velocity** – How quickly your video accumulates views and engagement in the first 24-48 hours after publication.


### How Google Ranks Video Content

Google’s approach to video ranking differs from YouTube’s but shares some core principles. Google analyzes both the video itself and the page hosting it to determine relevance and quality.

**Google’s Video Ranking Factors:**

1. **Structured Data (Schema Markup)** – Properly implemented VideoObject schema tells Google exactly what your video contains, dramatically improving visibility.

2. **Page Content Relevance** – The text surrounding your embedded video must align with and support the video’s topic.

3. **Technical Requirements** – Page speed, mobile-friendliness, and Core Web Vitals all impact video rankings.

4. **User Experience Signals** – Bounce rate, time on page, and interaction metrics influence rankings.

5. **Domain Authority** – Websites with strong backlink profiles and established authority have an advantage in video rankings.

6. **Video Quality Indicators** – Resolution, length, production value, and accessibility features (captions, transcripts) all matter.


## Comprehensive Keyword Research for Video SEO

Successful video SEO begins with strategic keyword research that identifies what your audience is actually searching for across both YouTube and Google.

### The Multi-Platform Keyword Research Strategy

The most effective approach combines insights from both YouTube and Google to find keywords that perform well on both platforms:

**Step 1: Generate Seed Keywords**

Start by brainstorming core topics related to your niche. For example, if you’re in digital marketing, your seed keywords might include:

- SEO strategies
- Content marketing
- Email marketing automation
- Social media advertising

**Step 2: Use YouTube’s Autocomplete**

Type your seed keywords into YouTube’s search bar and observe the autocomplete suggestions. These suggestions represent actual searches from real users, making them invaluable for understanding search intent.

For “SEO strategies,” YouTube might suggest:

- SEO strategies 2025
- SEO strategies for beginners
- SEO strategies for small business
- SEO strategies that work

**Step 3: Analyze Competitor Videos**

Examine the top-performing videos in your niche by sorting competing channels by view count. Look at their:

- Video titles and the keywords they target
- Description optimization and keyword placement
- Tags they’re using (viewable with browser extensions like VidIQ or TubeBuddy)
- Topics that generate the most engagement

**Step 4: Leverage Keyword Research Tools**

Professional tools provide data that manual research can’t match:

- **Semrush Keyword Analytics for YouTube** – Shows search volume, competition level, and related keywords
- **TubeBuddy** – Provides keyword scoring based on search volume and competition
- **Keywords Everywhere** – Displays search volume data directly in YouTube search results
- **Google Keyword Planner** – Essential for understanding Google search volume

**Step 5: Identify Video-Friendly Keywords in Google**

Not all keywords trigger video results in Google. To maximize visibility, target keywords where Google already shows video content:

Search your target keywords in Google and look for:

- Video carousels at the top of results
- Individual video results within organic listings
- Featured video snippets
- “Videos” tabs with significant content

# Creating inclusive job aids with microlearning

#

Job aids

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6074616a98ce7877b6bfdbe4/62a210a5faa382c4c45578a5_unicef.png)

Dr. Dawn DiPeri

Learning Passport Global Content Management Specialist

I work for the Learning Passport, launched in 24 countries and counting. It’s an educational technology solution created to help bridge disparity gaps, particularly with continuity of instruction for children, teachers, and families displaced by war, climate change, and the COVID-19 pandemic. We use 7taps to provide guidance and supporting materials to country offices around the world.

![thumbnail-image](https://yt3.ggpht.com/RSCeEFPCZ_ru5BKL4OXKwXeuumag3sbTtmAT3xfCVJm02WoblOGBupBTm7ntg1TVt49cms2H=s68-c-k-c0x00ffffff-no-rj)

7taps Microlearning871 subscribers

_Powered by MicrolearningConf_

> “I read others raving about 7taps. It fits the learning theories I learned in my doctoral program. I knew a rapid authoring tool would be a good fit for my faculty and corporate training needs.”

I create job aids on finding the right digital assets and learning resources, tips for their evaluation, curriculum mapping basics, and other related topics. After working with dense reports and PDFs, 7taps micro-courses are so refreshing. They help to deliver information in a quick easy way that is digestible and engaging. I also love the AI programmable characters and the ability to translate easily —that's important for our organization.

## Tips for effective and inclusive microlearning

### 1\. No jargon

Think about industry-specific terms and try to avoid them.

#### Prioritize clear language over jargon

Learn how to speak and write in clear, more concise language. When we're working with employees where English is not their first language, we want to ensure we get rid of the jargon, acronyms, and speak clearly.

#### If you have to use acronyms, explain what they are

The UN has so many acronyms and phrases I had a hard time learning. Luckily, I had a little glossary cheat sheet that someone had given me that I could quickly review to figure it all out.

**💡** If your industry or org _does_ need to use acronyms, make them a 7taps “cheatsheet” so they always have easy access to that reference wherever they are.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6074616a98ce7877b6bfdbe4/675194b39f0a58c192dcdce0_66507c7dfc910937089bdbe5_1.webp)

### 2\. Use inclusive imagery

> "We tend to design around what we are familiar with, what's comfortable to us, instead of realizing, ‘Well, okay, but that may not be everyone's comfort zone."

We want to make sure we have representation. Imagine you need an image of an engineer and you typed in engineer to a stock photography website, what would you find? Would you find a white male that's working on a computer? But who’s to say if this engineer is in a wheelchair, or is a different age, or a different gender? Think about including a wide range of representative images next to inclusivity, rather than always defaulting to using the same type of images for content.

We want children to be able to see aspects of themselves in the images—to feel like they can be anything they want to be.

**💡** Don’t forget about using gender-inclusive language. Here’s [a quick course](https://app.7taps.com/yYxrHZwRtpy) to help you with that.

### 3\. Consider language & ability

#### Leverage AI but support it with a human touch

In 7taps, AI avatars can be programmed to speak in multiple languages. Be sure to have a native speaker review the content for accuracy and adjust as needed. Be aware that certain words or phrases do not translate appropriately.

#### Source educational materials in different languages

Most of the open educational resources are created either from a Westernized or Americanized point of view. I try to source and advocate for more open educational resources that can resonate with our children in underserved populations. One example of this is advocating for more content in different languages, not just in English.

Our most requested languages are English, French, Spanish, and Arabic. And the Learning Passport platform currently supports learning in 46 languages—with more to come.

![ inclusive design for job aids should include resources in different languages](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6074616a98ce7877b6bfdbe4/675194b39f0a58c192dcdcd6_665080d605788287d87e3399_2.webp)

#### Ensure digital accessibility

Creating content accessible to all, including those with disabilities, is not just ethical—it's effective. But digital accessibility goes beyond just using closed captions for any audio or video content you create. For example, ensuring high color contrast in our digital content enabled students with visual impairment to engage more fully with the material.

### 4\. Think about the context

In many places around the world, girls aren't able to attend school past a certain grade. Many times you see teen pregnancies and child marriages. We want to try to fix those problems, starting with the educational content children see.

We want to ensure that the images we select and the words we say are encouraging more gender parity in the world. This often comes back to using inclusive images—ones that aren’t racist, ableist, or gender-biased.

💡When you're looking for imagery, stop and evaluate, “Is there a better way to show this?”

![avoid stereotypes and biases when designing job aids - e.g. female construction worker](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6074616a98ce7877b6bfdbe4/675194b39f0a58c192dcdce3_66507c9217fcc71666dfe5b9_3.webp)

### 5\. Avoid stereotypes

#### Start challenging your biases

If you close your eyes and picture a chef in your head. Is it a male? Is it a female? What do they look like? We have to challenge our biases and think of different ways to portray things—ways that focus on parity, representation, and inclusivity.

#### The truth about positive stereotypes

What if we said all Asians are good at school? It could still be harmful. It sets up a level of perfection and can make people feel like they have to live up to that standard. So we want to be aware of so-called “positive stereotypes”.

💡Check your biases with [this assessment created by Project Implicit](https://implicit.harvard.edu/implicit/takeatest.html) at Harvard.

#### Source locally relevant content

In Ghana, we collaborated with local authors and illustrators to develop educational content that echoed the children’s cultural background and experiences.

## Top 5 keys 🔑 to unlock inclusive learning

1. Inclusivity in education technology is key to bridging global disparities.
2. Jargon can be a barrier to learning. Make clarity a priority!
3. Inclusive images reflect the diverse world we live in. Ensure that everyone feels represented in educational materials.
4. Make education an equal playing field for non-native speakers and differently-abled individuals via digital accessibility.
5. While stereotypes might seem positive, they are inherently limiting. Challenge biases to paint a complete, diverse human experience.

#### Enhance your learning journey
