eLearning courses are one of the marvels of our digital world. No longer is learning confined to classrooms and auditoriums; people worldwide can tune in to experts in every field to access eLearning materials with a click of a button. But to reach an international audience, your eLearning courses must be translated. By including [eLearning translation services](https://jrlanguage.com/business-technical-translations/elearning-translation-services/ "eLearning translation services"), educational institutions, corporations, and content creators can tap into a global audience ready for more knowledge.

Multilingual eLearning is democratizing access to education, making it accessible to individuals with language obstacles. eLearning translation services and internet access supply students in different countries and professionals seeking better skills with the gateway for improvement and advancement.

Creating eLearning content requires knowledge and a clear strategy. In addition, another set of considerations and understanding is required when thinking about embarking on [translation services](https://jrlanguage.com/ "translation services") for your learning courses.

## The Benefits of eLearning and Translation

There is an application for eLearning content in nearly every industry. You can use it for onboarding new employees taking them through company policies, role expectations, and essential HR information. eLearning can be used to roll out new software, provide customer service, share instructions, and much more. Maybe you want to launch an education series for your customers to use a new product, or you need to create a series of Human Resources videos to use during sexual harassment training – the possibilities are endless.

The eLearning market is projected to be worth [$325 billion by 2025](https://techjury.net/industry-analysis/elearning-statistics/) – so if this isn’t something you had your eye on before, it certainly should be. This growth is partly due to the COVID-19 pandemic, which significantly increased the adoption of eLearning courses across every industry and is a worldwide practice now.

Still not convinced your organization might benefit from eLearning tools? Consider this: a survey of 2,500 companies found those who used a “comprehensive training program” had 24% higher profit margins and a staggering 218% higher revenue per employee. Having an e-learning course has increased income for 42% of U.S. organizations.

Providing eLearning content can also increase information retention in your team, increase policy compliance, and furnish an inclusive way to access information.

![eLearning-content-translation](https://jrlanguage.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/eLearning-content-translation.webp)

### Breaking the Linguistic Blocks

Language is a vessel for knowledge and can also be a barrier stopping the seamless transfer of information across cultures. English is a global lingua franca and the lingua franca in the digital world. However, despite English dominance, it is a reality that learners across the globe come from diverse places and have different linguistic backgrounds. Some students can feel more comfortable acquiring information in their native language or may have limited English proficiency.

eLearning translation services solve the language barrier by rendering educational content in a language that resonates with students, enhancing their comprehension and engagement with the training materials.

#### Types of eLearning content

- **Corporate Training.** Employee training materials, including onboarding, human resource training, software training, and development of new skills.
- **Customer Focus Material**. Customer training materials, including instructional videos for new products and processes.
- **Educational Content**. Student learning, including higher academic courses, niche classes, summer school, vocational training, online courses, and more.
- **Compliance Training Modules**. Including rules, regulations, policies, and procedures. Quality assurance and training for special certifications

![eLearning translation company](https://jrlanguage.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/10.webp)

## Best Practices to Create eLearning Content that Will Need Translation Services

If you know from the get-go that your Elearning course is going to need translation, you should design it with the translation process in mind and consider the best practices that we are presenting below:

### Start your Project with a Multilingual Audience in Mind

If you know your eLearning course will be multilingual or might need translation into another language, that should be considered in the design stage to follow best practices. Challenges arise whenever content translation is an afterthought. Planning for translation in advance helps you design how to write your content, examples, images, videos, and text placement in your videos.

Examples of things to look into if you need to translate your learning course:

#### 1) eLearning Tools, Authoring tools, and LMS Platforms

What authoring tools are going to be used? Are you going to use a Learning Management System (LMS platform)? What other content creation tools are you going to use? You need to work with a translation company to help ease the intricacy of managing eLearning content across different LMS platforms, authoring, and content creation tools.

Your team must also determine each tool’s workflow and capabilities to extract the original language and import translated content in the new languages.

#### 2) Prepare for Content Expansion

In translation services projects, [text expansion](https://jrlanguage.com/blog/managing-text-expansion-and-contraction-in-translation/ "text expansion blog post") is expected (as it is the contraction of the space occupied by the content). In languages like German and Spanish, translation produces growth in the space occupied by the words. German translation services can have up to a 50% text expansion over English. On the contrary, other languages, like Chinese and Japanese, might take less space. Both expansion and contraction affect the display of your courses on the screen, so a review process to validate how the new content shows has to be added to the workflow. Also, content variations must be considered during the course design for easy adaptation.

#### 3) Considerations for Video Production

Videos are heavily used in eLearning. Maintaining the inventory of assets to translate and source elements like the final script will make the project move faster. Prepare to edit the English video with enough transition time to leave room for the translation expansion.

If your videos are long and your speakers go fast, that might be a problem with the expansion of the text during translation. You may have to return to the editing drawing board, wasting valuable time and resources.

Planning ahead for eLearning translation also helps you plan the audio for your videos. Your audio should be specific to the subject you’re talking about, without using cultural idioms, humor, or niche references, as these don’t translate well and will need to be localized for each audience. If they have localization in mind, your team will produce a video and audio that is easier to internationalize for different languages, so you don’t need a separate script for each translated version. The more audio tracts you have, the more budget you need for translation services.

![eLearning translation](https://jrlanguage.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/fdshfdh.webp)

# Patient Education Materials: Ensuring Accessibility for LEP Populations

![](https://globalinterpreting.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/patient-education-materials.png)

#### Table of Contents

Patient education resources are your key tools for helping people make smart health decisions. From brochures and pamphlets to videos and digital guides, these materials explain medical conditions, treatments, prevention steps, and healthy choices. However, patients with Limited English Proficiency (LEP) often struggle to understand this vital information.

Language barriers prevent many patients with limited English proficiency from understanding critical medical information. This gap leads directly to worse health outcomes, more hospital stays, and dangerous miscommunications between patients and their care teams. Every patient deserves access to health education materials they can fully understand, regardless of what language they speak.

### Understanding Limited English Speakers in the US

[LEP populations—about 9% of the US](https://www.migrationpolicy.org/research/limited-english-proficient-individuals-united-states-number-share-growth-and-linguistic)—bring rich diversity to our country. They speak [Spanish, Chinese (Mandarin and Cantonese), Vietnamese, Tagalog, Arabic, Korean,](https://www.census.gov/topics/population/language-use.html) and many other languages. Each language community carries unique cultural norms, values, and healthcare practices that matter when creating patient education materials.

#### **Barriers for Limited English Proficient (LEP) Patients**

LEP patients [struggle to navigate healthcare information due to language barriers](https://www.ahrq.gov/health-literacy/improve/precautions/index.html). They often face challenges like:

- **Language Barriers**

When English isn’t your first language, medical terms and care instructions blur together. [More from HHS](https://www.hhs.gov/civil-rights/for-individuals/special-topics/limited-english-proficiency/index.html).

- **Cultural Differences**

Your culture shapes what illness means; when advice clashes with those beliefs, even clear words can miss their mark.

- **Health Literacy**

Health information lands only if you have the basics; without them, translations still feel dense and technical. [More from the CDC](https://www.cdc.gov/health-literacy/php/about/understanding.html).

- **Access to Resources**

When nearby clinics lack materials in your language, treatment gaps widen and recovery slows. [More from Kaiser Family Foundation](https://www.kff.org/racial-equity-and-health-policy/issue-brief/disparities-in-health-and-health-care-5-key-question-and-answers/).


#### **Statistics Highlighting the Need**

Language barriers cost real healthcare outcomes. [Studies show](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15009776/) Limited English Proficient (LEP) patients face higher hospital readmission rates, experience more medical errors, and report lower satisfaction. These patients struggle to understand their diagnoses and treatment plans compared to English speakers. Without proper interpretation, their health literally suffers.

### **Legal and Ethical Considerations**

Healthcare providers face both legal and ethical duties to make patient education materials accessible for people with Limited English Proficiency (LEP). These requirements stem from federal and state laws and ethical standards designed to ensure health equity and patient safety.

#### **Federal Laws Requiring Language Help for LEP Individuals**

Several federal laws mandate the provision of language assistance services to LEP individuals:

- [**Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964:**](https://www.justice.gov/crt/fcs/T6manual5) This landmark legislation prohibits discrimination on the basis of race, color, or national origin in programs and activities receiving federal financial assistance. Under Title VI, healthcare providers receiving federal funds are required to take reasonable steps to ensure meaningful access to their services for LEP individuals. This includes providing free language assistance services such as interpreters and translated materials.
- **Affordable Care Act (ACA):** [Section 1557](https://www.hhs.gov/civil-rights/for-individuals/section-1557/fs-sex-discrimination/index.html) of the ACA extends the non-discrimination protections of Title VI to all health programs and activities that receive federal funding. It explicitly includes language access requirements, reinforcing the obligation of healthcare providers to offer language assistance to LEP patients.
- [Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA)](https://www.healthcare.gov/accessibility/): While primarily focused on individuals with disabilities, the ADA also requires healthcare providers to ensure effective communication with patients, which can include providing language services to LEP individuals who have communication disabilities.

#### **Ethical Obligations**

Beyond legal requirements, healthcare providers face [an ethical duty](https://code-medical-ethics.ama-assn.org/) to make health information accessible to all patients, regardless of language. This ethical obligation rests on several key principles:

- **Respect for Autonomy**

Every patient deserves to steer their own care decisions. When you give people with limited English proficiency (LEP) clear facts on diagnoses, options, and likely results, you honor that choice.

- **Beneficence & Non‑Maleficence**

Your first duty: help and never harm. Sharing accurate, plain‑spoken information with LEP patients closes gaps that could otherwise trigger risky mistakes.

- **Justice**

Fairness demands equal access. Language support puts LEP patients on the same footing as English speakers, ensuring everyone receives the knowledge and tools to stay healthy.


#### **Informed Consent**

For limited English proficient (LEP) patients, [informed consent only works when they truly understand their options](https://www.hhs.gov/ohrp/regulations-and-policy/guidance/faq/informed-consent/index.html). This means providing all consent forms, education materials, and explanations in their language. This translation isn’t just good practice—it’s a legal and ethical requirement that ensures patients grasp treatment choices, risks, and benefits before making decisions.

#### Upholding Legal and Ethical Standards

Healthcare providers can meet their legal and ethical obligations with these key steps:

- **[Professional Translation Services](https://globalinterpreting.com/)**: Utilize certified medical translators to ensure that written materials are accurately translated and culturally appropriate.
- **Qualified Interpreters**: Provide access to trained medical interpreters for verbal communication, whether in-person or via telehealth platforms.
- **Staff Training**: Educate healthcare staff about the importance of language access services and how to effectively work with interpreters and translated materials.
- **Patient Feedback**: Regularly solicit feedback from LEP patients to assess the effectiveness of language services and identify areas for improvement.

### Culturally and Linguistically Tailored Materials – More than Just Translating

Creating patient education materials that LEP patients can actually understand demands both cultural and linguistic precision. When Limited English Proficient (LEP) populations receive materials tailored to their language and cultural framework, they can better access, comprehend, and apply health information. These customized resources bridge communication gaps, boost understanding, and ultimately lead to improved health outcomes.

#### **Impact on Patient Understanding and Health Results**

Providing health information in a patient’s native language [drastically improves their grasp of medical conditions and treatments](https://www.ahrq.g

Reading Time:  7minutes

Video is the most powerful tool for activating your employer brand. It brings your employee value proposition (EVP) to life, creates an emotional connection with candidates, and sets you apart in a crowded talent market. But not just any video will do. The best employer branding videos feature real employee stories.

## **Why Video is Essential for Employer Brand Success**

At Stories Inc., we’ve been tracking the impact of video over time and the numbers continue to prove its effectiveness. Today:

- A landing page with video increases conversion rates by over 80%.
- Including “video” in an email subject line boosts open rates by 20%.
- Job ads with embedded video generate 800% more engagement.

Your competitors know this, too. Eighty-eight percent of talent pros say video generates more responses from passive candidates, and 85% of talent marketers confirm video delivers strong ROI.

## **Why Now is the Time to Invest in Employer Branding Videos**

Candidates’ priorities have changed dramatically in recent years. While big-name brands once had a recruiting advantage, today’s top talent is looking for more: purpose, passion, inclusion, flexibility, and meaningful work.

The best way to prove your company offers these things? Real employee stories.

Story-driven employer branding videos go beyond [employee testimonials](https://storiesincorporated.com/whats-the-difference-between-employee-testimonials-and-employee-stories/). They provide authentic, firsthand proof of what it’s like to work at your company, making your culture tangible and relatable. When employees share their experiences in their own words, your audience can see themselves in your workplace. That’s what makes these videos so compelling—and why they’re essential for [employer brand success](https://storiesincorporated.com/work/employer-brand/).

## **The 8 Best Employer Branding Videos**

Now, let’s dive into some of the best employer branding videos.

This diverse and inspiring collection showcases how real employee stories bring company culture to life. Each video captures the authentic experiences that make workplaces unique and compelling. These aren’t just well-produced videos, they’re powerful storytelling tools that activate employer brands and connect with talent in meaningful ways.

### **1\. Bringing an EVP tagline to life**

In this video, AARP captures the essence of their culture: Work Happy.

Through authentic storytelling, we see how AARP supports its team members—whether it’s caregiving hours, hybrid work flexibility, or a strong sense of belonging through ERGs and diverse perspectives. And we don’t only hear about it from storytellers, we see it. From the group events to the smaller moments, this video shows “Work Happy” in action.

### **2\. Uncovering purpose and passion**

n this Sodexo video, we see how one act of care can transform a life. A patient who refused to eat, talk, or engage finally found hope—all because of one person’s persistence and a peach cobbler.

This story isn’t just about food; it’s about compassion, connection, and Sodexo’s commitment to Act with Purpose.

### **3\. Showing off a caring culture**

At The Knot Worldwide, support extends beyond work and into the personal lives of employees. This video highlights a culture where colleagues celebrate milestones together, leaders offer personal support during difficult times, and employees feel empowered to lead authentically.

Through real stories, we see a workplace built on strong relationships, mutual respect, and a true sense of belonging.

### **4\. Spotlighting opportunities to grow**

At BAE Systems Inc., career growth is fueled by mentorship, hands-on experience, and a culture that embraces learning. This video tells the story of an employee who started on the manufacturing floor and, with the support of colleagues and continuous learning, became a software engineer.

Through real experiences, we see how employees are empowered to innovate, embrace challenges, and develop skills that shape the future of technology.

### **5\. Inside the team**

Team storytelling is as important as general culture content. Take this video from Demonware. Behind every massive game launch is a team solving complex challenges at an incredible scale. This video dives into the team responsible for bringing crossplay and cross-title progression to Call of Duty, re-engineering systems to handle millions of players seamlessly.

Their story highlights the innovation, dedication, and high-stakes problem-solving required to create a seamless gaming experience for players worldwide.

### **6\. Innovation in action**

Not many people can say their job takes them inside a wind tunnel, but for this product development specialist at automaker Stellantis, it’s just another day at work. This video highlights a one-of-a-kind role that blends precision, creativity, and problem-solving to improve vehicle aerodynamics.

From designing custom testing equipment to fine-tuning airflow for better performance, we see how unique work environments and hands-on innovation fuel passion and drive.

### **7\. Content for specific audiences**

For women in STEM disciplines, seeing leaders who share their experiences can be the spark that fuels a career. This video shares the story of a cybersecurity systems engineer who has thrived at Lockheed Martin for 19 years, thanks to a culture that values flexibility, integrity, and growth.

Her journey highlights the company’s commitment to supporting working parents, increasing diversity in leadership, and empowering the next generation of women in tech.

### **8\. Customer stories are great employer branding stories**

This employer branding video from legal tech company, Relativity, highlights how their technology empowers changemakers like Andrew to secure justice for the wrongfully convicted.

By featuring both a customer testimonial and the team behind the initiative, the video moves beyond the traditional scope of employer brand and shows how a career at Relativity can help drive systematic change.

## **Your Employer Branding Videos Need Employee Stories**

The most compelling employer branding videos don’t just tell candidates why your company is a great place to work—they **show them** through the authentic experiences of your employees. Real stories create emotional connections, build trust, and ultimately drive talent attraction and engagement.

Ready to create employer branding videos that make an impact? Schedule an [intro call](https://storiesincorporated.com/contact/) with our team today to see how Stories Inc. can help you uncover and showcase the stories that set your company apart.

# Navigating DEI Changes: What it Means for Language Services

[Toni Andrews](https://ititranslates.com/author/tandrews/)\|February 27, 2025

Reading Time: ~3 mins

![A picture of a woman in a board room standing in front of an audience with a tv behind her that says diversity.](https://ititranslates.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Navigating-DEI-changes-banner.png)

The landscape of Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) policies is shifting rapidly. As companies navigate these changes, the implications for language services and corporate communication are profound. This article explores the current trends, the impact on the language services industry, and what it means for professionals in the field.

# The Changing Face of DEI Policies

## Companies Reducing DEI Policies

Several major companies have announced reductions in their DEI initiatives. Citigroup, for instance, has rolled back its diversity hiring goals and renamed its DEI team to “Talent Management and Engagement” [\[1\]](https://buildremote.co/companies/keeping-dei/). Similarly, PepsiCo has shifted its focus from representation goals to broader employee development [\[1\]](https://buildremote.co/companies/keeping-dei/). These changes reflect a broader trend of companies re-evaluating their DEI commitments in response to external pressures.

## Companies Prioritizing DEI![A picture of a meeting of a group of diverse business people.](https://ititranslates.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Promoting-Diverse-Workplaces-300x300.png)

On the other hand, some companies remain steadfast in their commitment to DEI. Procter & Gamble has publicly stated that equality and inclusion are integral to their business strategy [\[2\]](https://www.forbes.com/sites/conormurray/2025/02/06/war-on-dei-ag-pam-bondi-targets-dei-at-private-companies-and-universities/). Similarly, Delta Air Lines continues to emphasize DEI as critical to their operations [\[3\]](https://www.theroot.com/a-list-of-companies-that-continue-to-support-dei-1851755249). These companies recognize the long-term benefits of maintaining diverse and inclusive workplaces.

## Rebranding DEI

In response to the political climate, some companies are rebranding their DEI efforts. The Society for Human Resource Management (SHRM), for example, has dropped the term “equity” from its strategy, opting for terms like “belonging” and “community” instead [\[4\]](https://afrotech.com/where-do-these-companies-stand-with-dei). This rebranding allows companies to continue their DEI initiatives without drawing unwanted attention.

# Impact on Government-Funded Organizations

Organizations that rely on government funding are particularly vulnerable to changes in DEI policies. Recent executive orders have mandated the removal of DEI programs from federal agencies, which could lead to increased scrutiny and potential loss of funding for non-compliant organizations [\[5\]](https://blog.obws.com/list-of-corporations-ending-dei-initiatives/). Nonprofits and educational institutions must navigate these changes carefully to maintain their funding and continue their DEI efforts.

# Implications for the Language Services Industry

## Challenges for Interpreters and Translators

The rollback of DEI policies poses significant challenges for the language services industry. [Interpreters](https://ititranslates.com/interpreting/) and translators, who often work with diverse populations, may find fewer opportunities as companies reduce their DEI commitments. Additionally, the shift towards skills-based hiring could impact those who have relied on DEI initiatives for career advancement [\[6\]](https://www.forbes.com/sites/conormurray/2025/02/07/amazon-removes-diversity-references-from-annual-report-here-are-all-the-companies-rolling-back-dei/).

## Opportunities for Growth

Despite these challenges, there are opportunities for growth. Companies that continue to prioritize DEI will need robust language services to support their inclusive communication strategies. This includes translating employee handbooks, training materials, and other critical documents to ensure all employees have equal access to information [\[7\]](https://www.axios.com/2025/01/16/dei-rollback-companies-amazon-meta-mcdonalds).

The evolving landscape of DEI policies presents both challenges and opportunities for the language services industry. By staying informed and adaptable, interpreters and translators can navigate these changes and continue to thrive in their careers.

![Team collaborating on AI translation tools in modern office](https://csuxjmfbwmkxiegfpljm.supabase.co/storage/v1/object/public/blog-images/organization-9644/1768878863167_image_1768878862879.png)

Translating words from one language to another used to be slow and tiring. Now smart tools can turn entire conversations or pages into new languages in seconds. # Learning and Development: How Video Transforms Workforce Training

by [**Ali Rind**](https://enterprisetube.com/blog/author/ali-rind), Last updated: April 6, 2026, ref:

![Learning and Development: How Video Transforms Workforce Training](https://enterprisetube.com/hubfs/Learning%20and%20Development%20How%20Video%20Transforms%20Workforce%20Training.webp)

Play

Learning and Development: Video-Based Workforce Training Guide

AI-generated audio

24:03

Learning and development (L&D) is the structured process of building employee skills, knowledge, and competencies through training programs, mentoring, and continuous education. For organizations with hundreds or thousands of employees spread across offices, factories, and remote locations, delivering consistent L&D at scale is one of the hardest operational problems to solve well.

The gap between what L&D teams want to achieve and what they actually deliver keeps growing. According to the [LinkedIn Workplace Learning Report 2024](https://www.linkedin.com/business/talent/blog/learning-and-development/2024-workplace-learning-report), 90% of organizations are concerned about employee retention, and providing learning opportunities is the number one strategy they are using to address it. Yet most training programs still rely on slide decks, PDFs, and occasional in-person workshops that employees forget within weeks.

Video has changed this equation. When employees can watch, pause, rewatch, and interact with training content on their own schedule, completion rates climb and knowledge actually sticks. But dropping a few recordings into a shared drive is not a strategy. Building an effective video-based L&D program requires the right structure, tools, and measurement framework.

> **Key Takeaways**
>
> - Video-based L&D programs improve knowledge retention by up to 65% compared to text-only training, according to research from the Association for Talent Development.
> - Effective learning and development requires structured learning paths, interactive assessments, and completion tracking, not just a library of recorded content.
> - SCORM and LTI integration lets video training platforms work alongside existing LMS investments rather than replacing them.
> - Organizations with distributed or multilingual workforces need AI-powered transcription and adaptive delivery to reach every learner consistently.
> - Measuring L&D success means tracking engagement at the video level (heat maps, rewatch rates) alongside quiz scores and certification completion.

## What Is Learning and Development in the Workplace?

Learning and development encompasses every formal and informal effort an organization makes to improve employee capabilities. This includes onboarding programs for new hires, compliance training mandated by regulations, leadership development for high-potential employees, and technical skills training for specific roles.

L&D differs from one-time training events in a critical way: it is continuous. A strong L&D program creates ongoing pathways for employees to grow, not just checkboxes to clear during their first week. According to the Association for Talent Development's 2024 State of the Industry report, organizations spend an average of $1,283 per employee annually on learning, yet many struggle to measure whether that investment produces real skill improvement.

The core components of any L&D program include needs assessment, content creation, delivery, engagement, and measurement. Where most programs break down is in the last three. Content gets created but sits unused. Delivery is inconsistent across locations. And measurement, if it exists at all, tracks completion rather than comprehension.

## Why Does Video Outperform Traditional Training Methods?

The human brain is wired for visual learning. Research from McKinsey's organizational performance practice shows that multimodal instruction, which combines visuals, narration, and demonstration, consistently outperforms text-only training in both comprehension and long-term recall. That is not an abstract finding. It has real implications for how adults learn and retain workplace skills.

Consider the difference between reading a ten-page safety manual and watching a three-minute video showing the actual procedures. The video communicates tone, demonstrates physical actions, and provides visual context that text simply cannot replicate. For complex procedures like equipment maintenance, software workflows, or patient care protocols, video closes the gap between knowing and doing.

Video also solves the scheduling problem. In-person training requires everyone to be in the same place at the same time. For organizations with multiple locations, shift workers, or remote employees, that is expensive and often impossible. [On-demand video](https://enterprisetube.com/blog/video-on-demand) lets employees train when it fits their schedule, pause and resume as needed, and rewatch sections they did not fully grasp the first time.

### The Retention Advantage

Research on the [Ebbinghaus forgetting curve](https://www.td.org/content/atd-blog/updating-the-four-levels-for-the-new-world) shows that learners forget roughly 70% of new information within 24 hours without reinforcement. Video-based microlearning combats this by breaking content into short, focused segments of three to seven minutes, making it easy for employees to revisit specific topics whenever they need a refresher.

Interactive video takes retention further. When learners answer quiz questions embedded within the video itself, they engage in active recall. That act of retrieving information strengthens memory formation far more than passive watching. Studies in cognitive science consistently show that testing during learning, not just after, improves long-term retention by 25% to 65%.

## What Should a Video-Based L&D Program Include?

### Structured Learning Paths

Learners should not have to figure out what to watch next. Effective programs organize content into sequenced paths where each module builds on the last. Prerequisites ensure that employees master foundational topics before advancing. A new hire onboarding path, for example, might require completing the company culture module before moving to role-specific technical training. The [EnterpriseTube training and learning platform](https://enterprisetube.com/training-and-learning) supports exactly this model, letting administrators build prerequisite-enforced learning plans directly within the platform.

### Interactive Assessments

Quizzes, surveys, and knowledge checks embedded directly within video content serve two purposes. They test comprehension in real time, and they keep learners engaged. A ten-minute video with no interaction is easy to play in a background tab. A video that pauses to ask a question every few minutes demands active participation.

### Completion Tracking and Certification

For compliance-driven industries like healthcare, financial services, energy, and government, proving that employees completed training is not optional. L&D platforms need to track who watched what, when they completed it, and what scores they earned on assessments. Automated certificate issuance eliminates manual paperwork and gives employees a tangible record of their progress.

### LMS Integration

Most large organizations already have a Learning Management System (LMS). The video training platform should not replace it; it should work alongside it. Standards like [SCORM 1.2/2004 and LTI 1.3](https://www.td.org/content/press-release/new-atd-book-updates-kirkpatricks-four-levels-of-training-evaluation) allow video platforms to pass quiz scores, completion data, and learner activity back to the LMS. ## For YouTube Creators

If you're a content creator, translating your videos is like giving them a passport to travel the world. Imagine your content being enjoyed by viewers in Paris, Tokyo, Rio de Janeiro, and beyond! Here's how to make it happen:

### **YouTube Studio: Your Built-in Translation Toolkit**

YouTube Studio is a powerful tool for creators. It offers several features that make managing and optimizing your content easier. Translating your videos within YouTube Studio is straightforward and can be done without much hassle. Here's how you can do it:

1. **Log in to YouTube Studio**: Access your YouTube Studio dashboard by logging in to your YouTube account and clicking on your profile picture at the top right corner, then selecting "YouTube Studio."

2. **Select a Video:** From the left-hand menu, click on "Content" to see all your uploaded videos. Choose the video you want to translate, and click on the “Details” option.

![how to translate youtube videos with YouTube Studio](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6474e7207c23ed3a6036fe99/6680231b446804e5692b576e_AD_4nXdYg-i9uMlIdukShWLbvuDxKBW9pl4_HHTyJIk2AJ8zux3LNQUh7kklQjPphzjQSn1ErlCHG3jKV253U0HA7uJj6G8rEsaw6i9K3_yjgwtpHjpfgfb1fFz_dxVU3-qJyqpQCdzmTV1tnAE7emnIwF-Puptn.avif)

3. **Subtitles and CC**: Click on the "Subtitles" tab. Here, you can add, edit, or upload subtitles and closed captions (CC).

![how to translate youtube videos with YouTube Studio add language](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6474e7207c23ed3a6036fe99/6680231b177610bde3de0782_AD_4nXcyZbtgp763wuoEYq6C4v0whXExZ78Ugk2MsX6yyUwtNIqpRg3ACVo-G4P0Whiuz7gmjwT6w2hFjcvte-3RGG6fCLdtxuNiTj3Z9NpNlK8Du_pCXlUhR18YCoiDhJtuRJs0OwvXG7M0ZPzuXsEcLNUZNXM.avif)

4. **Add a New Language**: Click on "Add Language" and select the language you want to translate your video into. After selecting, you have the option to "Add" subtitles or closed captions in the chosen language.

![how to translate youtube videos with YouTube Studio add subtitles](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6474e7207c23ed3a6036fe99/6680231bdd03099d75a4e8d9_AD_4nXdNrVjdQq_M95zVbXbyGx6ju3fy7q_i0amMpIkwTRVS4HEBW7fO4n9mV51PPO2aVMMr2psUUvh0P3zMTQMX-ZXRooeMPj3IY2P3GwdYzBBGc8v_X-H9hhEv3SgsfjTgpbrLaZMUDoVIbjNr7T6hfKQekCw.avif)

For manual creation, use the YouTube subtitle editor. To upload, click on "Upload a file" and select your prepared subtitle file. If you choose the 'Upload File' option, ensure your subtitles include exact timings. Select the 'With timing' option and click 'Continue' to proceed.

# Why Manufacturers Need eLearning Translation: 3 Compelling Reasons

![Author Image](https://www.interproinc.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Dean-Haman.jpg)

[Dean Haman](https://www.interproinc.com/author/dean/)

18 Dec 2023 • 3 min read

[Tell us where you need help](https://www.interproinc.com/contact-us)

![elearning-manufacturers.social](https://www.interproinc.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/elearning-manufacturers.social.jpg)

With the rising growth of production and heavy competition in the global manufacturing sector, it’s still common for manufacturers to overlook or underestimate the importance of eLearning translation services.

Clear and accurate translations not only improve customer service and production quality, but effectively train a global workforce. Take it from us – training is not the area to skimp on by relying on your own employees to help translate training materials.

With a global workforce, training programs must be conducted in a multitude of languages and keep cultural nuances in mind. Furthermore, there are compliance laws, international laws, as well as regional laws to consider.1 Put simply, training employees around the globe takes an enormous amount of time and money. It’s extremely complex. To save money, increase time, and streamline content and communication, manufacturers are turning to eLearning technology to help with training needs.

Interpro has experience working with manufacturers on eLearning translation projects, and along the way, we’ve identified three key reasons why eLearning translation is important:

1. #### **Adapt and expand quickly**


![](https://www.interproinc.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/elearning-manufacturers.jpg)

The world of manufacturing is full of surprises. Opportunities may arise when you least expect them, and you must be ready to adjust and adapt quickly when there’s a new niche market or a sudden surge in demand. Plus, there will always be new hires, temps brought on to cover seasonal demand, or an unexpected big contract.

When suddenly faced with the task of training more people on a quick timeline (and they’re spread out around the globe), eLearning makes it a straightforward task. All types of training can be managed and developed from a centralized source, so adjustments are made quickly and efficiently.

Working with a professional translation company to manage your eLearning translation projects will ease the process of updating courses, re-using and expanding content, and sharing courses instantly between different departments, facilities, and plants across the world.2  Furthermore, it can take your training to the next level.


2. #### **Save money**


Training in the manufacturing industry can be very expensive, especially with a global workforce. Utilizing eLearning technology is an incredible way to help keep training costs down. It reduces travel and trainer costs, is much more flexible, and makes regular updates to course content (based on new systems, industry guidelines, or legislation) more feasible.3

Developing one high-quality training course and then localizing it in accordance with the cultural specifications of various target groups, is a better and more cost-efficient approach than developing multiple courses, training instructors, and then sending them around the globe.


3. #### **Retain employees**


Different types of people learn in different ways, which is why eLearning translation can be incredibly effective. Using different types of multimedia and interactive components, employees learn better when the course is in their native language and presents concepts they understand. This boosts confidence and self-esteem and will eventually lead to better results at work.

When your e-learning course is not just translated into a specific language, but properly optimized and localized, it shows a personalized approach and is a sign that you value your employees no matter where they are located. Naturally, this increases staff retention and satisfaction.


##### Closing Thoughts on eLearning Translation for Manufacturers

Training in the manufacturing industry is complex. It involves different types of training for different types of people of different countries, languages, rules, and regulations. Yet, it must all fall in place seamlessly to complete the whole picture: a flawless system working right from production, manufacturing, shipping, transportation to selling and customer satisfaction.4 eLearning translation may just the be answer to simplifying and improving your company’s training needs.

# Multilingual Safety Programs: Beyond Google Translate

- [Josh Drinkwater](https://ehscareers.com/employer-blog/author/anton/)
- [October 8, 2025](https://ehscareers.com/employer-blog/2025/10/08/)
- 8:42 am

Effective workplace safety requires more than basic translation tools; professional services ensure clear, culturally relevant communication to protect workers.

## share this post:

![](https://ehscareers.com/employer-blog/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/image_321f48e0ada2053c636320fb66b0b16d-1024x683.jpeg)

When workplace safety relies on clear communication, basic translation tools like [Google Translate](https://translate.google.com/) fall short. Misunderstandings caused by word-for-word translations can lead to serious accidents, compliance issues, or even legal penalties. Here’s why relying on these tools is risky and how professional translation services can make a difference:

- **Translation Tools Lack Context**: Free tools often misinterpret technical terms or cultural nuances, leading to errors in safety instructions.
- **Data Security Concerns**: Using free platforms like Google Translate can expose sensitive business information, as seen in a 2017 case where translated employee data became publicly searchable.
- **Legal Risks**: [OSHA](https://www.osha.gov/) mandates safety information be understandable to workers, making accurate translations a compliance necessity.

Professional services ensure accurate, secure, and worker-friendly translations. They go beyond simple word conversion by addressing tone, terminology, and worker familiarity with safety concepts. Investing in specialized translation providers, using tools like industry-specific glossaries, and prioritizing critical materials (e.g., Safety Data Sheets, training manuals) helps prevent accidents and builds trust.

**Key Takeaway**: Free tools are not enough for workplace safety. Businesses must prioritize accurate, secure, and tailored communication to protect workers and meet legal standards.

## Webinar: Overcoming Language Barriers for a Safer Workplace

## Why Multilingual Safety Programs Matter

Multilingual safety programs aren’t just about being inclusive – they’re essential for protecting workers, meeting legal obligations, and ensuring smooth operations. Employers who recognize this can better understand why it’s crucial to go beyond basic translation tools. Let’s dive into how workforce diversity, legal expectations, and common translation pitfalls highlight the importance of these programs.

### U.S. Workforce Language Diversity

The U.S. workforce is becoming more linguistically diverse, with many workers relying on languages other than English as their primary mode of communication. This trend spans industries like construction, manufacturing, healthcare, and hospitality. When safety programs are offered only in English, language barriers can leave workers unable to fully grasp critical safety protocols.

For example, foreign-born workers often bring valuable expertise to their jobs but may struggle with understanding safety guidelines. In states like California, Texas, and Florida, where Spanish is a primary language for many, the lack of tailored safety programs can lead to serious misunderstandings. Simply put, relying on English-only materials isn’t enough – clear, adapted communication is a must to ensure everyone stays safe on the job.

### Legal Requirements and [OSHA](https://www.osha.gov/) Guidelines

![OSHA](https://assets.seobotai.com/ehscareers.com/68e5e9ade04705e8b7120d11/e0f57a8bf3116549a3b2ba314d7c8d2c.jpg)

Language barriers aren’t just a workplace challenge – they’re a legal issue too. OSHA requires that safety information be provided in a way that workers can understand. Employers who fail to meet this standard risk citations, penalties, and liability. OSHA’s General Duty Clause further emphasizes that employers must create a workplace free from recognized hazards, which includes addressing language-related safety gaps.

### Problems with Basic Translation Tools

Relying on basic translation tools for safety materials can lead to serious issues. These tools often lack the precision needed to convey technical safety instructions. Consider this: language barriers contribute to 25% of job-related accidents [\[1\]](https://www.shrm.org/topics-tools/news/risk-management/training-native-language-makes-workplaces-safer). Even more concerning, fatal injury rates are 69% higher for foreign-born Hispanic workers compared to their native-born counterparts [\[1\]](https://www.shrm.org/topics-tools/news/risk-management/training-native-language-makes-workplaces-safer). Hispanic or Latino workers also consistently face the highest fatality rates across many industries [\[1\]](https://www.shrm.org/topics-tools/news/risk-management/training-native-language-makes-workplaces-safer).

Beyond physical safety, language confusion can erode psychological safety. Workers who don’t fully understand safety protocols may hesitate to ask questions, further increasing the risk of accidents. This highlights why accurate, culturally aware translations are so important for fostering both understanding and trust in the workplace.

## Professional Translation and Localization Methods

Creating effective safety materials requires more than just basic translation tools. It demands a thoughtful strategy that blends professional expertise with a deep understanding of cultural nuances. To get started, it’s important to understand the distinctions between translation and localization, choose the right providers, and focus on translating the most critical safety resources for your team.

### Translation vs. Localization Differences

Translation focuses on converting words from one language to another, while localization adapts content to align with the cultural context of the target audience.

In workplace safety, this difference can be crucial. For example, translating "hard hat" into Spanish might yield "casco", but localization goes further by determining whether workers are more familiar with "casco de seguridad" or "casco protector." Localization also takes into account cultural attitudes toward authority, risk, and communication styles.

In some cultures, workers may prefer indirect communication and might hesitate to report safety concerns directly to supervisors. Localized safety materials can address this by offering alternative reporting methods or emphasizing the importance of direct communication in U.S. workplace safety practices. A professional localization approach ensures that safety messages are not only accurate but also resonate with the intended audience.

### How to Select Translation Providers

Choosing the right provider for safety materials requires careful consideration of their expertise, processes, and commitment to quality. It’s not enough to rely on general translation services – your provider should specialize in technical and safety documentation.

Look for providers with experience in Environmental, Health, and Safety (EHS) standards, including OSHA regulations and specific hazards. They should understand materials like Safety Data Sheets (SDSs), lockout/tagout procedures, and emergency protocols. Industry-specific expertise is key, as safety requirements can vary widely between sectors.

Certifications matter. Providers with [ISO 17100](https://www.iso.org/standard/59149.html) certification demonstrate adherence to rigorous quality management standards. Individual translators should also hold relevant credentials and have a proven track record in technical translation, particularly in workplace safety.

Quality control is another critical factor. Reputable providers follow a multi-step process, including initial translation, editing by a second linguist, final proofreading, and reviews by subject matter experts. Some even offer back-translation services to ensure nothing is lost in the process. These steps help catch subtle nuances that might be overl

# Knowledge Base Video: Everything You Need to Know

Published by Florence on August 12, 2025

![Knowledge base Video](https://landen.imgix.net/blog_FyTBLRATRflRUMPt/assets/TidesjFwOlQyGunl.png?w=880)

## Table of Contents

13. [Production Checklist (Narrative)](https://weet.co/blog/knowledge-base-video-everything-you-need-to-know#checklist)

## What Is a Knowledge Base Video?

A knowledge base (KB) video is a short, single-purpose clip embedded in your help center, product KB, or internal wiki. Its job is simple: demonstrate a task clearly from start to finish. Unlike a static article, a video shows the cursor moving, the buttons being pressed, the confirmation messages that appear, and the exact state a user should expect to see when they’re done. The format is especially powerful for software workflows, but it’s just as useful for hardware, policy walkthroughs, and internal processes.

Well-made KB videos share a few traits. They focus on one outcome, so viewers never wonder if they’re in the right place. They’re brief—often two to five minutes—so a person can watch and do the task in the same sitting. And they’re easy to scan, with chapters and captions that make it simple to jump to the exact step a person needs.

Organizations use KB videos to reduce repeat tickets, speed up onboarding, and standardize how tasks are performed across teams. When a topic is high traffic or frequently misunderstood, a video often does more to resolve confusion than additional text ever will.

## Benefits & Business Impact

The main benefit is faster understanding. By showing the path through the interface and narrating what’s happening, a KB video removes the guesswork that sometimes slips into written instructions. The viewer sees the timing, the confirmation states, and the context a screenshot can’t capture.

Retention improves as well. When people hear, see, and follow along at the same time, they’re more likely to repeat the task correctly later. That pays off in fewer re-opened tickets, fewer back-and-forth emails, and fewer mistakes caused by misreading an article.

There’s an operational benefit too: consistency. A video becomes the canonical demonstration for how a task should be done. Everyone—customers, new hires, and seasoned teammates—can align on the same, approved procedure. That consistency translates into time and cost savings because teams don’t need to schedule repeated live demos, and support agents can link to a trusted resource that resolves issues the first time.

## Video Types & When to Use Them

Different problems call for different video formats. For hands-on software tasks, a screen recording is usually best. If you’re demonstrating a physical workflow—like installing a device—use a live camera with close-ups. When the concept is abstract, an animated explainer can visualize ideas that don’t exist on a screen. Sometimes a talking-head intro is useful to frame a change, such as a policy update or a new release, and then you can cut to screen or B-roll for the practical steps.

| Type | Use When | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Screen Recording** | Demonstrating tasks in web or desktop apps | Zoom the UI, narrate concisely, and show the end state before moving on. |
| **Live Demo (Camera)** | Hardware steps, device setup, physical procedures | Stabilize the camera, capture close-ups, and add short on-screen callouts. |
| **Animated Explainer** | Concepts, architectures, or policies without a UI | Use motion sparingly to support the narration instead of distracting from it. |
| **Talking Head** | Announcements, release overviews, or change management | Keep it brief and pair with screen inserts or links for deeper steps. |
| **Hybrid** | End-to-end workflows that benefit from both face and screen | Use chapters to mark transitions so viewers can jump where they need. |

## End-to-End Workflow (Plan → Publish)

### 1) Identify High-Impact Topics

Start where demand is obvious: your most-searched help articles, your most common tickets, and the steps that repeatedly slow down onboarding. If a topic has high volume and solving it improves activation, adoption, or resolution time, it belongs on your short list.

### 2) Define the Outcome and Audience

Each video should promise a single outcome. Say who the video is for, what they’ll be able to do when it’s over, and any prerequisites. This framing lets viewers self-select quickly and reduces abandonment.

### 3) Outline and Script

Use a tight outline: context, the steps in order, the visible result, and the next action to take. Script the intro and transitions so you sound confident, but keep the step narration flexible. Over-scripting often produces stiff delivery.

### 4) Record

Capture your screen and mic in the browser so you can move from recording to editing without file wrangling. Record in a quiet room with a stable mic level. If your UI text is small, zoom in so the video remains legible on mobile. Silence system notifications to avoid interruptions.

### 5) Edit for Clarity

Trim pauses, remove missteps, and speed up waits. Add short labels or subtle highlights where clicks happen. Insert chapters so viewers can jump directly to the step they need. Generate captions automatically, then scan for product terms and fix them.

### 6) Review and Approve

Ask a subject-matter expert to validate the sequence and terminology. Resolve comments before publishing. A short sign-off process pays for itself by preventing re-records caused by small inaccuracies.

### 7) Publish and Embed

Publish the video and embed it in the canonical article above the fold. Keep a compressed text version of the steps below the video for people who prefer to skim. If support agents often send this resource, save a macro that links to the exact chapter timestamp.

### 8) Measure and Iterate

Monitor completion rates, drop-off points, and search-to-play conversion. If viewers exit at the same timestamp, that step likely needs clearer narration or a closer zoom. Re-record just the affected clip instead of starting from scratch.

**Time-saving tip:** Batch several related topics and record them in one session. Consistent settings and momentum reduce total production time.

## Script Template & On-Screen Formula

The most reliable structure is simple and repeatable. Hook the viewer by naming the outcome and time commitment. Set expectations about prerequisites. Walk through the steps with concise narration and purposeful zooms. Show the success state, then direct viewers to the most relevant next action.

# Simplify Multilingual Content with the Right Microlearning Platform

## Large organizations may have a global audience of diverse learners who speak and read multiple languages.

Or all the employees might be located in one country but have a variety of native languages and different levels of proficiency with the company’s primary language.

In either case, training materials cannot be effective if learners cannot understand them. Multilingual training is the best way to meet the needs of a diverse learner population and ensure that all learners can access and understand their training.

### Creating global [microlearning](https://www.ottolearn.com/microlearning) can be complicated

Many training platforms don’t allow learners to specify a language for the interface display or the content, often pushing content to learners in languages they don’t understand well. Struggling to navigate training in an unfamiliar language adds to learners’ [cognitive load](https://www.ottolearn.com/microlearning/science-of-microlearning) and harms training effectiveness.

Ideally, you’d easily be able to reach all of your learners with inclusive global microlearning — [microlearning](https://www.ottolearn.com/microlearning) that:

- Allows learners to have a preferred language for screens, menus and content.
- Delivers training to each learner that matches their language preferences.
- Makes it easy for training developers to create content in their chosen language.
- Easily enables training developers to accurately translate content into several languages.
- Provides unified reporting by having a single module containing all language versions of your content.

#### But … Translating eLearning is often tricky

Some platforms allow creation of training with only a single user-interface language, for example.

Even where content can be offered in multiple languages, developers generally need to create a new course for each different-language version of the same content. To do so, they need to address text that appears in graphics, alt text, inside scripts and any other text element, ensuring that all get translated accurately and in a contextually appropriate way. Developers also have to be mindful of how translation changes the length of text and adjust their layout and design accordingly — for each language. Then, each time content changes or new content is added, the developers have to repeat the process for each language version of the course.

In addition, most eLearning platforms track learners in different-language versions of a course as if they are taking different courses — because, essentially, they are! That makes it hard for training admins or learners’ managers to quickly see the progress of a learner who uses content in more than one language.

### It shouldn’t be that hard

![A dial showing a series of country flags; the center of the dial says "Select" with arrows pointing out indicating a selected country flag.](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5cd48707e0162300f8e4ed59/5e31e6565be31e5f18660384_Simplify%20Multilingual%20Content%20with%20the%20Right%20Microlearning%20Platform.jpg)

Course development software should, ideally, make it easy to both create multilingual training and provide a multilingual user interface that allows learners to choose their display language.

Content creators should easily be able to reproduce training content in multiple languages — and learners should be able to specify which languages they understand well enough to receive training in.

And, no matter which languages a learner uses, their manager should be able to track their progress in a unified way.

In a global world, you need a global training platform that simplifies creation and maintenance of multilingual content — and allows learners to choose training in their own language.

### OttoLearn provides global training

OttoLearn's [adaptive microlearning platform](https://www.ottolearn.com/microlearning) understands that learners have different needs — including the need for multilingual content that works. OttoLearn separates the display language — user interface, including menus, buttons, etc. — from the content language and supports customization of both.

Then, with a few clicks, you can activate the translated content, making it available to your learners.

With OttoLearn, training admins do not have to line up translators for each language, then track and verify that the content has been updated in all languages. OttoLearn automatically translates new content into each language and treats all the different-language versions of training content as a single module. This makes managing multilingual content simple and efficient.

It also simplifies learner data tracking, since Otto knows which courses a learner engages with, even if the learner accesses the content in multiple languages.

## Turn to OttoLearn for truly global training

Stop worrying about where to find translators and wondering whether the different-language versions of your content are accurate. Put your energy into creating great content, and let OttoLearn take care of the rest.

# How to Translate Videos: A Step-by-Step Guide

By [Guest](https://ivannovation.com/blog/author/guestauthor/ "Posts by Guest")November 9, 2020June 17th, 2024[General Translation](https://ivannovation.com/blog/category/general-translation/)

[No Comments](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#respond)

Did you know that in the U.S. alone, over 70 million people watch online videos every day. From Facebook Live to YouTube and Vimeo among many others, video clearly reigns. It’s also popular around the world which means that knowing how to translate videos is important for every company wanting to grow abroad.

###### Table of Contents

- [Step 1: Preparation](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#step1)
- [Step 2: Decide on Video Content Output](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#step2)
- [Step 3: Decide What to Localize](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#step3)
- [Step 4: Decide What NOT to Localize](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#step4)
- [Step 5: Build a Knowledge Base](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#step5)
- [Step 6: Find a Language Service Provider](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#step6)
- [Step 7: Know Legal Requirements & Compliance](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#step7)
- [Step 8: Set up Your Technical Team](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#step8)
- [Step 9: Translate Videos](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#step9)
- [Step 10: Consider Voice Talent](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#step10)
- [STEP 11: Review the Translated Video](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#step11)
- [Conclusion](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#concl)

When used effectively, translated videos are one of the most versatile and profitable marketing tools available to help you reach global audiences.

It’s easy to see how video adds back engagement and personality into what would otherwise be a very impersonal digital experience. Take a look at these stats:

- _90% of consumers say a video will help them make a purchasing decision._ ( [Social Media Today](https://www.socialmediatoday.com/news/the-state-of-video-marketing-in-2018-infographic/518339/))
- _Over 80% of all_ _tr_ _affic_ _will consist of video by 2021._ ( [Cisco](https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/solutions/collateral/service-provider/visual-networking-index-vni/complete-white-paper-c11-481360.html#_Toc484813971))
- _97% of marketers claim that videos help customers understand products._( [Hubspot](https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/state-of-video-marketing-new-data))

![how to translate youtube videos](https://ivannovation.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/translate-youtube-video.jpg)

Translating YouTube videos is a powerful way to get content out to a wider audience.

###### Do I Need Video Translation? or Localization?

Translation of videos refers strictly to converting one language to another, but **video localization** is a process that takes video content from one language locale and adapts it to a different language locale or target audience. So what changes may be more than just the language, it could involve a whole range of other elements such as images, formats, music, etc.

In this article we’ll use the terms somewhat interchangeably since the term “ [localization](https://ivannovation.com/language-services/localization/)” is less well known by those not in the translation industry.

Companies translate videos for all types of content such as

- Marketing videos
- Product demos
- Product tutorials
- Technical support videos
- e-Learning video courses

For these types of video content and more, your customer conversion hinges on how well your localized videos are able to connect with their intended audiences.

But, video translation can be complex. If not done properly, it can lead to a massive waste of human resources, time, and money, and the result can be an unsightly embarrassing video.

That’s why we’ve provided this **step-by-step guide** on the process of translating your video content:

[Go to top](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#topofpage)

## **Step 1: Preparation**

Regardless of whether you want to translate movies or whether you want to translate YouTube videos, a successful project is built upon good preparation.

- Research. You’ll need to understand the culture of your new audience.
- Know the basics of video localization. Remember it’s the **audio** part and subtitles that are localized. The visual experience may not change unless there’s something in your content that doesn’t align with the culture of your target audience.
- Familiarize yourself with the lingo of video localization.
- Decide whether to use the video as is or if parts of the video will need to be changed with [video editing](https://instasize.com/) tools for the new target audience. If something is obviously inappropriate in your video, it makes sense to avoid the cultural offense that including it might cause.

![videographer video localization](https://ivannovation.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/videographer-video-localization.jpg)

The best time to think about how to localize a video is during the planning stage even before beginning to film the video in the original English.

[Go to top](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#topofpage)

## **Step 2: Decide on Video Content Output**

- **Audio voiceover recording**: The script is first translated, then using the voice of a true native speaker, it’s integrated back into the video.
- **Voiceover and subtitles**: Using a combination of both voiceover translation and subtitle translation is an option that works well for the hearing impaired.
- **Subtitling**: Subtitling on its own works well if you have budget constraints. It’s relatively easy to translate SRT files. You can transcribe the original version into the target version either by subtitling or closed captioning.

###### What is an SRT file?

SRT stands for “SubRip Subtitle.” It’s an extremely simple file format for the subtitles of a video. It’s set up like this:

- Line number
- Beginning and ending time stamp for when the text will be displayed
- Text to display on the screen.

Here’s an example from The SpongeBob Movie

```
1
00:00:24,566 --> 00:00:26,151
I'm ready!

2
00:01:02,353 --> 00:01:04,731
The temperate, pristine,

3
00:01:04,814 --> 00:01:07,650
shallow seas of the tropics.
```

So clearly, if translating SRT files is all you need to do, then the project will be much easier than if more extensive changes need to be made.

![videotapes localize video](https://ivannovation.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/videotapes-localize-video.jpg)

Decide on the type of translation you will have in your final video. Will you have voiceover translation? Voiceover plus subtitles? Or translated subtitles only?

[Go to top](https://ivannovation.com/blog/how-to-translate-videos/#topofpage)

## **Step 3: Decide What to Localize**

- **Graphics:** You may need to change images, text, colors, among other elements, and even logo text if this is included in the source language.
- **Formatting & Design**: Special considerations include spacing issues, headers appearing in the right places and sections of the document, as well as ensuring that text reads in the right direction. For example, English text reads from left to right, as opposed to Hebrew, Arabic, Pashto, and Persian for example, where text reads from right to left.
- **Local units and conversions**: All units need to be converted to those used in the target locale. This includes time and dates, measurements, etc.

# The Power of Translation and Localization in Building a More Inclusive Workplace Culture

Jill Bishop

Today’s workforce is strongly committed to bringing about real change in company culture, and one of the key ways to do this is through effective diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) initiatives.

At its core, DEI is about fostering a culture that welcomes all and advocates for inclusive practices. Companies that prioritize DEI initiatives are more likely to attract and retain talented employees, and create an overall more productive work environment.

As your company develops DEI initiatives, remember that when it comes to language, DEI is about more than simply translating expressions. You should carefully consider how language plays a role in empowering your multilingual employees and leveraging their unique strengths. Let’s take an in-depth look at how [translation services](https://multilingualconnections.com/services/translation/) go hand-in-hand with a strong corporate DEI program.

## Why Translation and Localization for DEI Initiatives?

Diversity comes in many shapes and sizes, and multilingualism and multiculturalism are key elements. Language services can go a long way in advancing your workplace’s inclusion of team members who speak languages other than English. This is especially the case for global companies with remote employees in different regions worldwide.

Language services can make your team members feel supported and valued, ultimately leading to key results such as:

- **Empowering multilingual employees.** An inclusive workplace should aim to communicate in the language employees feel most comfortable in, both for vital documents such as contracts and everyday scenarios such as a corporate newsletter.
- **Streamlining communication and productivity across global locations.** Employees will be more productive if they collaborate clearly and share knowledge about customers, products and needs without barriers.
- **Engaging with your multilingual audience.** Your multilingual employees may be a great advantage in reaching your multilingual audience. Listening to diverse voices at your company can prevent cultural misunderstandings and lead to high impact when tailoring customer messaging.
- **Leveraging employees’ unique skill sets**. While your multilingual employees should never take on responsibilities that aren’t part of their skill set (i.e., becoming in-house translators without training), their unique multilingual perspectives may enrich company projects and/or strategic planning.
- **Navigating safety and warnings.** Many workplaces rely on accurately translated signage, manuals and training sessions to ensure employee safety. In fact, an estimated [25% of job accidents occur due to language barriers.](https://www.shrm.org/resourcesandtools/hr-topics/risk-management/pages/training-native-language-workplace-safe.aspx)
- **Reducing turnover rates.** Team members who feel supported are more likely to stick around and become loyal to the company. In contrast, language misunderstandings can lead to poor morale and high turnover.

## How to Use Translation to Make Diversity and Inclusion Part of Your Company Culture

DEI initiatives can be tailored to meet the unique needs of each company and address specific areas for improvement. There are many ways to support the success of these initiatives, including translating the following materials:

- Employee handbooks and onboarding materials
- Corporate training/e-learning platforms
- Company newsletters and communications
- Safety training to decrease accidents and Safety Data Sheets (SDSs)
- Signs and warning labels around the workplace
- Training materials to increase promotability
- Expansion of customer service offerings
- Live interpretation for company events, like meetings and conferences

## ![Dominic Carter quote](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

## Building an Effective DEI Plan With Translation Services

Ultimately, you can enrich your DEI plan and implement it more effectively by utilizing translation services. By communicating with multilingual employees in their preferred languages, you’ll build connections and trust in real ways—here’s how.

#### 1\. Translate Employee Handbooks and Training Materials

First, language support is a critical part of making multilingual employees feel safe and comfortable. Companies should consider providing [translated materials](https://multilingualconnections.com/industry-experience/human-resources/), language resources and multilingual technology. This can help ensure that all employees have access to the same information, regardless of their language background.

In addition, offering language classes is a great idea. Companies committed to multilingualism may even provide several different language classes (besides just English) so that the initiative encourages all staff to expand their language knowledge base.

#### 2\. Emphasize the Importance of Effective Communication

Fluid communication is a clear indicator of team cohesion and productivity. That’s why your company should consider training employees to better communicate, e.g., over email and during meetings. This can help get teams on the same page regarding best communication practices, regardless of their language proficiency.

Moreover, these training sessions are an opportunity to discuss cross-cultural communication and how to respect different communication methods that arise with team members, customers, investors and more.

#### 3\. Leverage Multilingualism and Diversity

Encouraging diversity is the right thing to do, and it’s also good for your company. According to a McKinsey report, companies with greater diversity are 35% more likely to achieve higher-than-average financial returns (McKinsey) and 70% more likely to capture a new market ( [Harvard Business Review](https://hbr.org/2013/12/how-diversity-can-drive-innovation)).

Diversity, including multilingualism, is, therefore, a huge asset that companies should leverage. Value the multilingual skills of your team members and listen closely to their cultural perspectives. They may become your go-to consultants for better serving and reaching new clients.

#### 4\. Foster a Culture of Inclusivity Beyond Language

Finally, language is just one element of encouraging DEI in the workplace. Creating a culture of belonging may also involve:

- Respecting and supporting cultural practices (e.g., having a prayer room on-site or offering floating holidays for employees to celebrate the days they choose)
- Bolstering anti-discrimination and grievance resolution processes
- Building diversity-focused recruitment and leadership pipelines
- Creating DEI toolkits and resources
- Hosting DEI-focused events and workshops
- Encouraging out-of-the-box thinking through innovation days

At the end of the day, don’t make assumptions about what your DEI program should look like. Ask your teams what their pain points and goals are so you can tailor your initiatives with them in mind.

## Enrich Your DEI Programs By Partnering With Multilingual Connections for Translation Services

Diversity, equity and inclusion are critical components for any company looking to build trust with team members and stakeholders, leading to better understanding and stronger relationships.

As translators and linguists, we’re especially aware of the importance of multilingualism in society and the workplace today. [Learn more about how Multilingual Connections can help you achieve your DEI goals](https://multilingualconnections.com/industry-experience/human-resources/) and build stronger relationships with your employees and stakeholders.
